experiments.title: Does Increasing the Number of Appeal Links Boost Email Clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the # of donation appeal links for a campaign appeal email will achieve a higher number of clicks to the donation page because the additional links will reduce friction and capture reader attention at different moments of engagement within the email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2026 - 02/28/2026
Bible League Canada (BLC) is a ministry organization dedicated to placing Bibles and biblical resources into the hands of waiting believers in Canada and around the world. During one of their fundraising campaigns, we wanted to test whether increasing the number of appeal links in an email would help drive more visitors to the donation page.
We decided to test this in Email #2 of this campaign, which served as a key follow-up touchpoint. While the first email introduced the need, Email #2 was designed to reinforce urgency and convert engaged readers into donors. Because it targeted an already-warmed audience, it represented a meaningful opportunity to optimize click-through behavior and drive additional gifts.
This experiment asked: Would increasing the number of donation links within the body of the email increase clicks to the donation page? Or is a single plain-text link sufficient once donor motivation is established?
The Control version included one plain-text donation link placed within the email.
The Treatment version included the same plain-text donation link, but also hyperlinked two additional portions of body copy higher up in the email. These additional links directed to the same donation page and provided readers with more opportunities to click as they engaged with the message.
The language, story, and overall appeal copy did not change between versions. The only variable tested was the number and placement of donation links within the email.
We randomly split the Email #2 audience between the two versions and measured clicks to the donation page as the primary outcome. Clicks on non-donation links (such as social media icons or the organization logo) were excluded from our analysis. Click results were pulled from February 11 – 28.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test confirmed our hypothesis that increasing the number of appeal links within the email would cause a lift in clicks to the donation page.
The Control email generated 47 clicks, resulting in a 1.8% click-through rate. The Treatment generated 90 clicks, resulting in a 3.5% click-through rate. This means that adding additional appeal links in the email drove a 90.9% increase in clicks to the donation page, with a statistically significant 90.9% level of confidence.
When readers feel motivated to act, even briefly, having an immediate opportunity to click increases the likelihood of follow-through. The additional links likely reduced friction by allowing readers to click at the moment conviction or urgency peaked — instead of requiring them to scroll down to find the link.
Due to the donation sample size from this test, we were not able to validate any specific downstream impacts, such as differences in total gifts or revenue between versions. As a result, this test confirms increased giving intent (clicks), but does not yet confirm increased giving outcomes.
Further testing is needed to determine if this lift in clicks consistently translates to increased gifts and revenue, and also to confirm whether this effect holds across other campaign emails like a launch email or final day ask.
Our recommended next step is to test this hypothesis again across multiple emails within a fundraising campaign and evaluate not only clicks, but downstream conversion and revenue impact. Aggregating results across several sends will help determine whether this tactic should become a standard structural best practice for Bible League Canada’s email program.

experiments.title: How a Dear Reader on Blog Pages Impacts Email and Donor Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dear reader presenting a content offer for users on the Time of Grace blog and written devotion pages will increase email and donor acquisition, as it offers Time of Grace content to users who are already interested in and enjoy Time of Grace resources.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/14/2024 - 08/26/2024
Time of Grace receives significant traffic to its blogs and written devotion pages. Our hypothesis was that this traffic left a huge opportunity to capture emails and donors by presenting traffic that we already know is interested in Time of Grace content, with an opportunity to receive a free content offer. At the end of this funnel, they would also then be presented with an ask.
experiments.keyLearnings: Key Learnings from this Experiment: The experiment showed that presenting a content offer to users on Time of Grace blog and written devotion pages resulted in a significant increase in email acquisition. This indicates that users who are already engaged with Time of Grace content are more likely to provide their email in exchange for additional resources. The experiment also demonstrated a substantial increase in transactions, showcasing the potential to convert these engaged users into donors, resulting in $802 in revenue from this additional passive stream of audience cultivation. This indicates that providing valuable content offerings can not only increase email acquisition but also boost conversion rates. Further, both the increase in email acquisition and transactions were statistically significant, underscoring the validity and reliability of the findings. Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the importance of providing valuable content offerings to an already engaged audience to boost email acquisition and drive transactions. By leveraging these insights and implementing them in future experimentation, Time of Grace Ministry can further enhance its outreach and impact on its audience.

experiments.title: How quantifying the time investment in a high urgency email appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe quantifying the time investment for donors will increase the click-through rate because more people will see it as within reach.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2025 - 12/18/2025
The IMB was running a donor survey as part of their year end campaign. They knew that people generally enjoyed giving their opinions, but wondered whether many thought it might take too long and therefore wouldn’t click.
The survey was short, but that couldn’t be seen in the email. They decided to run an experiment with a treatment that explicitly stated the survey would take “two minutes” to help donors answer the mental question: “Do I have time for this?” and see if it impacted click-through rate from the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that quantifying the time investment for donors by stating that the survey would only take "two minutes" led to a 6.2% increase in clickthrough rate from the email. This indicates that explicitly mentioning the survey's short duration positively affected recipients' decision to engage with it.
In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing the effectiveness of providing specific details, such as time investment, to reduce donors' perceived effort. This approach can increase engagement and ultimately improve the success of campaigns or initiatives designed to elicit responses from the target audience. Additionally, it is important to incorporate similar strategies across other communication channels to further enhance donor participation and interaction.

experiments.title: How writing more direct copy in an email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a more direct email for the housefile will achieve an increase in click-through rate because shorter copy would allow the user to grasp the message of the email quicker and help the user focus on the CTA. because  shorter copy would allow the user to grasp the message of the email quicker and help the user focus on the CTA.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/27/2025
For Time of Grace, we send out emails to the housefile for TOG’s premium offers. For this email, we wanted to test how presenting a more direct email with shorter copy would affect click-through rate for their email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting a more direct email with shorter copy resulted in a significant increase in click-through rate for Time of Grace Ministry's premium offers. The treatment group saw a 153.9% increase in clicks compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 100.0%.
During this experiment, we also saw a valid 111% increase in donations.
This outcome indicates that users responded positively to the shorter, more direct messaging by being able to grasp the email's message quicker and focus on the call-to-action. In future experimentation, Time of Grace Ministry should consider using shorter and more direct copy in their emails to the housefile to potentially increase click-through rates. This strategy could lead to more effective communication with their audience and ultimately drive more conversions or engagement with their premium offers.

experiments.title: How removing an image in a fundraising direct appeal email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the image for a direct appeal email will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because it will simplify the email, reduce distractions, and focus the reader’s attention on the call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2024 - 11/25/2024
America Needs Fatima’s Fatima Statue Custodians travel across the country, bringing the statue of Our Lady of Fatima into homes to foster prayer, healing, and spiritual renewal. To sustain this mission, they launched a fundraising campaign to cover rising fuel costs and tested how an email image might impact engagement.
In a previous test, we found that removing an image of a Custodian visit significantly decreased clicks, suggesting that the image played a key role in engagement. However, we wanted to further explore whether this finding applied across different types of images.
For this follow-up test, we examined whether an image of the sender, Mr. Ferrera, an active Fatima Statue Custodian, standing with the statue of Our Lady, would help or hinder email engagement. The Control email included this image, while the Treatment removed it.
We hypothesized that removing the image would increase clicks by simplifying the email, reducing distractions, and keeping the reader focused on the call to action.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control email (with the image) and the other half receiving the Treatment email (without the image).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Surprisingly, removing the image of the sender led to a 59.6% increase in email clicks, with a 97% statistical level of confidence. This result contradicts the first test, where removing an image of a Custodian visit led to a significant decrease in clicks.

This suggests that not all images contribute equally to engagement—context and content matter. Several psychological principles may help explain this outcome:
Personal Connection vs. Mission Focus: While personal images can build trust, they can also shift focus away from the larger mission. The first test’s image reinforced the mission (showing a Custodian in action), whereas this test’s image may have felt less necessary.
 	Reader Expectations: Donors might be more compelled by an image that directly reinforces the impact of their gift (e.g., a Custodian at work) rather than one featuring the sender.
testing different image types
how different images—such as those of the people served or of Custodians in action—affect engagement and donor response

experiments.title: How shortening email copy for a content offer impacts non-donor signups and donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening email copy for non donors will achieve a change in content offer KPIs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2025 -
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and Non-Donor audiences. For this specific recirculation, we tested whether shorter, more concise email copy would impact clicks, email signups, and donation conversions.
This experiment result is for non-donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that shortening email copy for non-donors resulted in a significant increase in clicks across all traffic. The treatment group saw a 14.2% increase in clicks with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the change was not due to random chance.

Moreover, when looking at the downstream impacts of this test:
The treatment version had 6% more email signups, with an 80% level of confidence.
 	The treatment version showed a 50% increase in donations after email signup, when considering total emails deployed, with a 56% level of confidence (note that the donation sample size was small, however). When comparing this number for those who signed up and THEN donated, the treatment version had a 49% increase in donations with a 56% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a personal ask to match a gift amount affects overall conversions in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal ask to match a gift for potential donors will increase the conversion rate because it creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel that their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, especially when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
The American Cornerstone Institute sought to determine the impact of a more personal approach to soliciting a year-end gift match on overall conversion rates, including click-through rates from the email to the donation page and donation conversion rates.
They tested this concept through three emails sent during their year-end campaign. This write-up presents the results of email 3, which served as the kickoff email.
The control email presented the matching gift by stating that their gift would be doubled, thanks to a year-end matching gift challenge of up to $100,000. The treatment email presented the matching gift by saying that a generous donor wants to personally match their gift, allowing them to have double the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that utilizing a more personal approach when asking for a matching gift can significantly increase conversion rates. In this case, the treatment email, which presented the matching gift as coming from a generous donor wanting to match their gift personally, resulted in a 125.8% increase in donations compared to the control email.
This suggests that creating a sense of urgency and personal connection in donation appeals can motivate potential donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, particularly when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
Regarding the effect on email click-through rate, the treatment email showed no difference in click-through rate. There was a 3.9% decrease in clicks with only a 42% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of personalization and emotional appeals in donation requests. By consistently incorporating these elements into fundraising campaigns, nonprofits like American Cornerstone Institute can further increase engagement, donations, and the overall success of their campaigns.

experiments.title: How using ease language impacts clicks in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using ease language for people who receive the email will achieve more clicks because they will be more encouraged to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
When asking donors to consider monthly giving, the way you frame the opportunity can make all the difference. In this test, the team focused on how to talk about ease. Rather than only highlighting the benefits of monthly giving, the copy emphasized how simple and straightforward it is to sign up as a recurring donor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language about the ease of becoming a monthly donor significantly increased clicks in the email. The treatment, which focused on the ease of becoming a monthly donor, saw a 67.4% increase in clicks for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 96.5%. This means the hypothesis was supported, showing that people were more encouraged to become a monthly donor when presented with language highlighting the ease of doing so.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of language on donor behavior. Donations like Dallas Theological Seminary can increase donor engagement and acquisition by incorporating ease-of-donation language in future appeals and communications. It is also crucial to analyze the impact of different messaging strategies on other metrics such as donation amount, donor retention, and overall campaign success. Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of using language that resonates with donors and simplifies the donation process to inspire action.

experiments.title: Will donors be more likely to give when their gift funds a specific, tangible outcome rather than a broader appeal?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the tangible ask for Deaflympics 2025 will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it gave donors a clear, tangible way to fund the ministry side of the trip rather than the logistics of getting missionaries there.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/22/2025
IMB is raising funds for the 2025 Deaflympics in Tokyo to send sign-language-fluent missionaries who can share the Gospel with the Deaf community—one of the world’s most underserved groups.
We tested two approaches for our kickoff housefile email: a broad appeal highlighting the need to send missionaries versus a tactical ask where donors could directly fund Olympic pins to spark Gospel conversations. We anticipated the treatment would resonate more, since it gave donors a clear, tangible way to fund the ministry side of the trip rather than the logistics of getting missionaries there.
We wanted to know if donors are more likely to give when their gift supports a specific, tangible outcome (pins for Gospel conversations) rather than a broad appeal for trip costs.
Note: The control used more urgent language, which may have influenced results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the broad appeal highlighting the overall need to send missionaries for the 2025 Deaflympics in Tokyo was more effective in generating donations compared to the tactical ask to fund Olympic pins. Despite the hypothesis that the tangible ask would increase donor conversion metrics by providing a clear and tangible way to fund the ministry side of the trip, the results showed a 58.5% decrease in donations for the treatment group.
Furthermore, the average gift size in the control was $112 while the treatment was $33, indicating that the control led to not only more gifts but also larger gifts.
It is important to note that this was not an entirely clean test. It could indicate that the pin-focused language was less compelling or too confusing for IMB's audience.
In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the effectiveness of different messaging strategies and appeals when soliciting donations. It may be beneficial to continue testing various approaches to understand donor preferences and behavior better. Additionally, conducting surveys or focus groups with donors could be valuable in gathering feedback on their motivations for giving and preferences for communication. This information can help tailor future campaigns to better resonate with donors and improve donation outcomes.

experiments.title: How a testimonial email from a donor performed against a faux-forward email during a fundraising appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donor testimonial email from a real donor for people receiving a campaign appeal email will achieve an increase in email clicks to the donation page because personal stories can increase social proof and encourage readers to join them by becoming a donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
During the critical calendar year-end fundraising period, Luther Seminary wanted to test the effectiveness of their Giving Tuesday PM email communication strategy. The traditional approach has used a faux-forward email, where the PM message includes a “forwarded” copy of the AM email, with a brief added note from the person forwarding it.
For this experiment, we tested the faux-forward strategy against a Treatment email that replaced the forwarded content with a testimonial from a real donor, Greg, sharing his personal reasons for supporting Luther Seminary. This test aimed to determine whether focusing on a donor testimonial would lead to higher engagement and more conversions compared to the repetitive messaging of the faux-forward email.
Donor stories would create stronger emotional connections and trust, motivating readers to act and contribute to Luther Seminary’s mission. A personal endorsement from a peer would resonate more deeply with recipients, leading to increased clicks to the donation page. At the same time, the faux-forward approach might contribute to disengagement or message fatigue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we found that the Treatment email, featuring a testimonial from a donor (Greg), led to a -39% decrease in clicks to the donation page, with a 97.9% statistical level of confidence. This result was unexpected, as we hypothesized that the personal donor story would increase trust and emotional connection, ultimately driving more clicks to the donation page.

One major factor contributing to this decrease could be the -21.8% decrease in email opens for the Treatment email, with a 100% statistical level of confidence. This drop in opens suggests that the unfamiliar sender, Greg Myhr, may have impacted the email's ability to capture the recipient’s attention. People are more likely to open emails from familiar senders, a concept known as sender recognition or trust transfer. In this case, emails from Luther Seminary staff may have felt more familiar and trustworthy, prompting more recipients to open the email.

Psychologically, cognitive fluency plays a role here: people are more likely to engage with content that feels easy, familiar, and comfortable. When the email is from a known and trusted source (like Luther Seminary staff), recipients may feel more at ease engaging with the content. When the email comes from an unfamiliar source (like a donor), it may subconsciously signal uncertainty or a potential risk, causing people to hesitate or overlook the email altogether.

Additionally, while social proof can be a powerful motivator, it needs to be framed in a way that maximizes its impact. Although Greg’s testimonial aimed to leverage the principle of social proof by showing how a fellow donor supports Luther Seminary, the email’s unfamiliar sender might have diminished its credibility. In-group bias suggests that people tend to trust and engage more with others who belong to their perceived social group or network. Because Greg was an outsider to the recipients (compared to the staff sender), the emotional pull of his testimonial may have been weakened.

Another contributing factor could be cognitive overload. The Treatment email included a personal story from Greg, which while emotionally powerful, might have added a layer of complexity or unfamiliarity that decreased its effectiveness compared to the more straightforward faux-forward email (the Control). The faux-forward email, which came from a familiar source, was simpler and less cognitively demanding for the reader, making it easier to process and act upon.

This suggests that while donor testimonials are effective in fostering connection and trust, the familiarity of the sender is crucial in determining how much attention and engagement the email receives. If the recipient doesn't recognize or trust the sender immediately, they may be less likely to open the email and, as a result, less likely to engage with the content, including the donation link.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for donors can significantly increase the click-through rate. In this case, the treatment with the button resulted in a 59.2% increase in clicks compared to the control group with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

This result confirms the hypothesis that a clear, visually distinct call-to-action, such as a button, can reduce friction, draw the reader’s eye, and make it easier for people to take the intended next step.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of different call-to-action elements, such as buttons, in emails targeted to donors. By consistently analyzing and optimizing our email designs, we can maximize engagement and ultimately drive more conversions for The Heritage Foundation’s campaigns.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for subscribers
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for subscribers will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for subscribers resulted in a significant increase in the click-through rate. The treatment group, which received the email with a button linking to the ebook "Flags of Our Fathers," saw a 25.6% increase in clicks compared to the control group, which had a raw URL link.

This outcome supports the hypothesis that visually distinct call-to-action buttons reduce friction, draw the reader's eye, and make it easier for people to take the intended next step. Moving forward, this learning should be applied to future experimentation by emphasizing the use of clear and prominent buttons in emails to encourage higher engagement and click-through rates. Additionally, A/B testing different variations of buttons, such as color, text, size, and placement, can further optimize email performance and drive desired actions from subscribers.

experiments.title: How does defaulting appeal-language to a monthly ask impact overall transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a monthly ask for users receiving the premium appeal will achieve an increase in recurring gifts without decreasing overall transactions because it prioritizes recurring, while still giving the opportunity to make a one-time gift if they choose..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/28/2025
Every month, Time of Grace shares an opportunity to receive the latest monthly book offer as a thank-you for a gift. However, donors also have the ability to request the monthly resource every single month when they begin a recurring gift. Each month, this ask neatly lends itself to a recurring ask. We hypothesized that defaulting the appeal language to a monthly gift, while still allowing the donor to make a one-time gift if they choose, will increase recurring gifts while not negatively impacting overall transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment was responsible for the only 2 recurring gifts received from this appeal, however, it was still below the level needed for statistical validity. Additionally, the experiment disproved our hypothesis that defaulting to monthly would not negatively impact overall transactions. We saw a 64% decrease in transactions with a 96% level of confidence. 

In terms of next steps, this experiment provides us with key pieces of data for actions items:

Users will make a recurring gift to the monthly premium if they are asked. This alone is valuable. Typically, Time of Grace only presents a Grace Partner ask to users during the annual high-urgency push for monthly donors. However, the two gifts here indicate that there is an opportunity to highlight the monthly ask and perks in the standard premium appeal that takes place every month. However, composing the email around the Grace Partner ask has a decrease in transactions overall that outweighs the gain. Which leads us to the second action item.

Time of Grace should explore a way of prioritizing monthly that isn't so intrusive to the experience of donors who would've made a one-time ask. Defaulting to a Grace Partner ask could cause donors to exclude themselves: The ministry is asking for monthly. If I can't give monthly, then maybe I shouldn't give at all. An opportunity for further testing could be focusing our efforts on the donation page. Keeping standard language in email and on the donation page, but prioritizing monthly or the premium-related perks when selecting gift frequency. This will ensure we don't discourage clicks and traffic overall at the top of funnnel, while still highlighting monthly for those who would be inclined to start a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How including a button in an appeal email affects donor conversion rates for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button that says “Double My Gift” will increase donor conversion rate because it draws more attention than a plain-text hyperlink, creates a clearer and more compelling call to action, and reduces friction by making the next step more obvious and easier to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/31/2025
The Heritage Foundation conducted this experiment as part of their President’s Challenge matching gift campaign. This campaign was designed to drive donor engagement by offering a limited-time opportunity to double the impact of a donor’s gift through a matching challenge. The email in question was the first send in the campaign sequence, making it a high-leverage moment for influencing donor behavior and maximizing initial response rates.
Given the high stakes of the campaign’s early momentum and the importance of clearly communicating the match opportunity, the Heritage Foundation sought to test whether different call-to-action (CTA) formats would affect click-through and conversion rates.
The test was deployed via email, targeting the Heritage Foundation’s member list.
Both versions of the email—control and treatment—were linked to the same donation landing page but differed in their CTA presentation.
The experiment was driven by a hypothesis rooted in behavioral and conversion rate optimization principles: a visually prominent, action-oriented button might outperform a plain-text hyperlink in driving user action.
Key motivations included:
Improving clarity and visibility of the CTA: While the raw URL in the control version is technically functional, it lacks the immediate visual weight and action language of a button. The team suspected that this might cause the CTA to be overlooked or feel less urgent.
Reducing cognitive friction: A clear, concise button like “Double My Gift” directly mirrors the donor’s intent and may simplify decision-making.
Encouraging mobile-friendly design: Buttons often perform better on mobile devices where touch interaction is key, and plain-text links can be harder to tap.
This test is a good example of using micro-optimizations in email fundraising design to explore how seemingly small changes can have an outsized effect on user behavior and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the visually prominent, action-oriented button with the text "Double My Gift" did not perform as expected. In fact, it resulted in a 47.7% decrease in donations compared to the plain-text hyperlink used in the control version of the email. This outcome was statistically significant with a confidence level of 99.7%.

In future experimentation, it is important to remember that assumptions based on design principles and best practices may not always hold true for every audience or context. It is crucial to test different elements and approaches to see what resonates best with donors. Additionally, it is essential to prioritize evidence-based decision-making over assumptions, even if they seem logical on the surface. This result highlights the importance of testing variations and being open to unexpected outcomes to truly understand donor behavior and optimize conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: How testing whether an urgency-driven subject line or a specific biblical reference leads to higher opens and engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a specific and intriguing biblical reference (“Discover the richness of Exodus”) instead of a more generic faith-based phrase (“Don’t miss this opportunity to deepen your faith”) for people viewing a content offer email in their inbox will achieve an increase in email opens because specificity and curiosity drive engagement, making the subject line feel more relevant and compelling to the reader.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2025 - 02/08/2025
Luther Seminary creates ebooks and digital resources to help their audience deepen their faith and biblical knowledge. Their latest ebook explores the book of Exodus, and they aimed to maximize engagement by optimizing the subject line of their promotional email to their housefile audience.
To determine what type of subject line would drive more opens and clicks, we conducted a series of A/B tests. In a prior test, we compared exclusivity-based language to a curiosity-driven subject line, finding that a curiosity-driving subject line increased opens by 9.4% with 100% statistical confidence.
For this next test, we wanted to know whether an urgency-based subject line (“Don’t miss this opportunity to deepen your faith”) or a subject line that was more specific and intriguing (“Discover the richness of Exodus”) would drive higher engagement. We hypothesized that the more specific and curiosity-driven subject line would increase opens by making the content feel more relevant and compelling to the reader.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control subject line and the other half receiving the Treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test produced unexpected but valuable insights about the relationship between subject line messaging and user engagement.

While the Control subject line ("Don't miss this opportunity to deepen your faith") generated more opens, the Treatment subject line ("Discover the richness of Exodus") resulted in fewer opens (a 3.8% decrease) but led to a 24.5% increase in email clicks, with a 98.4% statistical level of confidence.

This suggests that while urgency-driven language in the Control created a stronger initial impulse to open the email, the specificity of the Treatment subject line attracted a more engaged audience—those who opened were more likely to click through to the content.

Psychologically, this aligns with two key principles:
FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) and urgency – The Control subject line may have driven more opens by making the email feel time-sensitive and broadly relevant.
 	Relevance and commitment – The Treatment subject line set clearer expectations about the content, likely attracting recipients who were already interested in the topic, leading to higher engagement post-open.

experiments.title: How comparing a curiosity-driven subject line to an exclusivity-based subject line to determine impact on opens and engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a vague, curiosity-driven subject line (“Here’s your first look”) instead of an exclusivity-based subject line (“Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide”) for people viewing a content offer email in their inbox will achieve an increase in email opens because the curiosity gap encourages recipients to open the email to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/17/2025
Luther Seminary develops useful ebooks and other digital resources that help their audience deepen their faith and knowledge of the Bible. They recently created a new ebook about the book of Exodus and wanted to send it to their housefile email audience.
We wanted to know what kind of subject line would drive more opens and clicks for this content offer email, so we decided to run an A/B test.
The Control subject line used exclusivity messaging: “Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide.” The Treatment subject line was shorter and designed to spark curiosity: “Here’s your first look.”
We hypothesized that the curiosity-driven subject line would generate more opens by enticing recipients to learn more.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control subject line and the other half receiving the Treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment confirmed that a curiosity-driven subject line can significantly increase email opens. The Treatment subject line ("Here's your first look") outperformed the Control ("Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide") by increasing opens 9.4%, with a 100% statistical level of confidence.

The success of the curiosity-based subject line aligns with the Curiosity Gap Theory, which suggests that people feel compelled to seek out missing information when presented with an incomplete or intriguing statement. Unlike the Control, which relied on exclusivity messaging, the Treatment sparked interest without revealing too much—prompting recipients to open the email to learn more.

However, while opens increased, click rates remained the same (~2.2% for both versions). This suggests that while curiosity successfully drove more people to open the email, it did not necessarily increase engagement with the content inside. A possible explanation is that the Treatment subject line created intrigue but did not set clear expectations about the offer, leading some recipients to open the email without a strong intention to engage further.

To build on this learning, future tests should explore:
Refining curiosity-driven subject lines – Testing variations that maintain intrigue while hinting more explicitly at the content's value.
 	Combining curiosity with faith-based language – Testing curiosity-driven phrasing with biblical references to see if it enhances both opens and clicks. Update: We ran a follow-up experiment to test curiosity-based language against a subject line featuring a specific biblical reference to see which approach is more effective at engaging faith-driven audiences.
 	Aligning email content with subject line expectations – Ensuring that the email itself delivers on the curiosity generated in the inbox, potentially increasing clicks.

experiments.title: How including a donation ask earlier in an email appeal affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a donation ask upfront will increase donor conversion rate because readers will see the call to action before they potentially lose interest or skim past it, making it more likely that they take action immediately rather than waiting until the end of the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/26/2025
This experiment was conducted on Email 5 of The Navigators’ Sustainers Campaign, which focused on acquiring new recurring donors.
The goal was to determine whether placing the donation ask at the beginning of the email would increase donor conversion rates.
Given that email recipients often skim content or lose interest before reaching the end, we wanted to test whether an upfront call to action would drive more immediate engagement and donations.
This experiment was motivated by the need to optimize email structure for maximum impact, ensuring that potential donors saw and acted on the ask before disengaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Placing the donation ask at the beginning of the email resulted in a significant decrease in donation conversion rates compared to placing it at the end.

2. The hypothesis that including the donation ask upfront would increase donor conversion rates was proven to be incorrect in this case.

3. The control version, with the donation ask at the end of the email, outperformed the treatment in terms of donation conversions.

Recommendations for Future Experimentation:

1. Consider the flow and structure of the email carefully. While it may seem intuitive to place the donation ask upfront for immediate action, this may not always yield the best results.

2. Test different placement strategies for the donation ask in future email campaigns to see which location generates the highest conversion rates.

3. Continue to track and analyze data from A/B experiments to inform decision-making and optimize future campaigns for better results.

experiments.title: How a story-based faux-forward email performed against a standard faux-forward email on Giving Tuesday

rows.name: Control - Faux Forward with Standard Direct Appeal
Treatment - Faux Forward with a Story

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a donor testimonial in a faux-forward for a direct ask email will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because personal stories from donors can build trust, create emotional connection, and motivate action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/03/2024
During the critical Giving Tuesday fundraising period, Human Coalition wanted to test the effectiveness of their Giving Tuesday PM email communication strategy. Our traditional approach has been to use a faux-forward email, where the PM message included a “forwarded” copy of the AM email, with a brief added note from the person forwarding it.
For this experiment, we wanted to test the faux-forward against a treatment email that still kept the forwarded AM content but instead focused on a testimonial from two Human Coalition donors sharing their personal connection to the mission.
The goal of this test was to determine if focusing on a donor testimonial rather than reiterating the AM email’s message would lead to higher engagement and more conversions. Our theory was that using donor stories would drive more trust and emotional connection to help inspire the reader to take action, while the repeated messaging of the faux-forward strategy could be leading to fatigue or disengagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that personal donor testimonials were far more effective in driving engagement than the traditional faux-forward approach. The Treatment email, which focused on a heartfelt testimonial from two Human Coalition donors, saw a 41.2% increase in clicks to the donation page, suggesting that emotional connection and social proof are powerful motivators for potential donors.
The results indicate that people are more likely to take action when they hear directly from fellow supporters who share their values and experiences. Testimonies not only provide social proof but also humanize the organization, fostering a deeper sense of community and purpose. This highlights the psychological principle of social proof, where individuals are influenced by the actions and opinions of others, especially those they can relate to.
On the other hand, the Control email, which repeated the morning’s message in a faux-forward format, did not resonate as strongly. This suggests that while reminders can be helpful, they may not always be as compelling as personalized, story-driven content, which has a stronger emotional pull.
Moving forward, it will be beneficial to incorporate more donor testimonials and personal stories into email campaigns, especially in critical fundraising periods like Giving Tuesday, to build trust and drive higher engagement.

experiments.title: How a testimonial email from a donor performed against a faux-forward email during a fundraising appeal

rows.name: Control - Faux Forward Email from Luther Staff
Treatment - Testimonial Email from a Donor

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donor testimonial email from a real donor for people receiving a campaign appeal email will achieve an increase in email clicks to the donation page because personal stories can increase social proof and encourage readers to join them by becoming a donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/03/2024
During the critical calendar year-end fundraising period, Luther Seminary wanted to test the effectiveness of their Giving Tuesday PM email communication strategy. The traditional approach has been to use a faux-forward email, where the PM message includes a “forwarded” copy of the AM email, with a brief added note from the person forwarding it.
For this experiment, we tested the faux-forward strategy against a Treatment email that replaced the forwarded content with a testimonial from a real donor, Greg, sharing his personal reasons for supporting Luther Seminary. The goal of this test was to determine whether focusing on a donor testimonial would lead to higher engagement and more conversions compared to the repetitive messaging of the faux-forward email.
Our hypothesis was that donor stories would create stronger emotional connections and trust, motivating the reader to take action and contribute to Luther Seminary’s mission. We believed that a personal endorsement from a peer would resonate more deeply with recipients, leading to increased clicks to the donation page, while the faux-forward approach might contribute to disengagement or message fatigue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we found that the Treatment email, featuring a testimonial from a donor (Greg), led to a -39% decrease in clicks to the donation page, with a 97.9% statistical level of confidence. This result was unexpected, as we hypothesized that the personal donor story would increase trust and emotional connection, ultimately driving more clicks to the donation page.

One major factor contributing to this decrease could be the -21.8% decrease in email opens for the Treatment email, with a 100% statistical level of confidence. This drop in opens suggests that the unfamiliar sender, Greg Myhr, may have impacted the email's ability to capture the recipient’s attention. People are more likely to open emails from familiar senders, a concept known as sender recognition or trust transfer. In this case, emails from Luther Seminary staff may have felt more familiar and trustworthy, prompting more recipients to open the email.

Psychologically, cognitive fluency plays a role here: people are more likely to engage with content that feels easy, familiar, and comfortable. When the email is from a known and trusted source (like Luther Seminary staff), recipients may feel more at ease engaging with the content. When the email comes from an unfamiliar source (like a donor), it may subconsciously signal uncertainty or a potential risk, causing people to hesitate or overlook the email altogether.

Additionally, while social proof can be a powerful motivator, it needs to be framed in a way that maximizes its impact. Although Greg’s testimonial aimed to leverage the principle of social proof by showing how a fellow donor supports Luther Seminary, the email’s unfamiliar sender might have diminished its credibility. In-group bias suggests that people tend to trust and engage more with others who belong to their perceived social group or network. Because Greg was an outsider to the recipients (compared to the staff sender), the emotional pull of his testimonial may have been weakened.

Another contributing factor could be cognitive overload. The Treatment email included a personal story from Greg, which while emotionally powerful, might have added a layer of complexity or unfamiliarity that decreased its effectiveness compared to the more straightforward faux-forward email (the Control). The faux-forward email, which came from a familiar source, was simpler and less cognitively demanding for the reader, making it easier to process and act upon.

This suggests that while donor testimonials are effective in fostering connection and trust, the familiarity of the sender is crucial in determining how much attention and engagement the email receives. If the recipient doesn't recognize or trust the sender immediately, they may be less likely to open the email and, as a result, less likely to engage with the content, including the donation link.

experiments.title: How beginning a high urgency appeal email with a story impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that beginning an email appeal with a story for CYE Email 4 recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because the reader will be immediately engaged and drawn in by the narrative therefore increasing the reader retention.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For Email 4 of The Navigators CYE campaign, we opted to test into removing a personal reflection from the beginning of an appeal email.
We believe that starting the email with the story directly will immediately engage the reader by drawing them into a compelling narrative, leading to increased reader retention and higher click-through rates.
This approach bypasses the personal reflection, which may delay the email’s emotional hook, ensuring the audience connects more quickly with the mission and impact of the Navigator staff.
The treatment was sent to 50% of the Email 4 recipients.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Beginning an email appeal with a story for CYE Email 4 recipients resulted in a 44.0% increase in clicks compared to starting with a personal reflection.

2. The treatment showed a high level of statistical confidence at 97.4%, indicating that the results are reliable and significant.

3. By immediately engaging the reader with a compelling narrative, the treatment increased reader retention and click-through rates.

Future Experimentation:

1. Consider incorporating storytelling at the beginning of email appeals for other campaigns to enhance reader engagement and drive desired actions.

2. Test different types of stories, such as success stories, impact stories, or personal narratives, to determine which resonates best with the audience.

3. Continue to prioritize testing and data analysis to identify effective strategies for engaging donors and supporters in email communication.

By leveraging the success of this experiment and applying the key learnings to future campaigns, The Navigators can continue to optimize their email appeals for increased impact and engagement.

experiments.title: How using ease language impacts clicks in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using ease language for people who receive the email will achieve more clicks because they will be more encouraged to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
DTS was sending an appeal during their calendar year end campaign. The goal of this appeal was to acquire new monthly donors. We wanted to test how using language about the ease of becoming a monthly donor impacts clicks in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language about the ease of becoming a monthly donor significantly increased clicks in the email. The treatment, which focused on the ease of becoming a monthly donor, saw a 67.4% increase in clicks for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 96.5%. This means that the hypothesis was supported, showing that people were more encouraged to become a monthly donor when presented with language highlighting the ease of doing so.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of language on donor behavior. By incorporating ease of donation language in future appeals and communications, nonprofits like Dallas Theological Seminary can potentially increase donor engagement and acquisition. It is also crucial to analyze the impact of different messaging strategies on other metrics such as donation amount, donor retention, and overall campaign success. Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of using language that resonates with donors and simplifies the donation process to inspire action.

experiments.title: How a personal ask to match a gift amount affects overall conversions in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal ask to match a gift for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate  because   it creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, especially when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
American Cornerstone Institute wanted to test how a more personal approach to asking to match a gift during year-end would affect overall conversion rates including clickthrough rate from the email to donation page and donation conversion rate.
They tested this concept within three emails they sent throughout their year-end campaign. This write up shows the results of email 3 which was the kickoff email.
The control email presented the matching gift by stating that their gift would be doubled thanks to their year-end matching gift challenge up to $100,000. The treatment email presented the matching gift by stating that a generous donor wants to personally match their gift allowing them to have double the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that utilizing a more personal approach when asking for a matching gift can significantly increase conversion rates. In this case, the treatment email that presented the matching gift as coming from a generous donor wanting to personally match their gift resulted in a 125.8% increase in donations compared to the control email.
This suggests that creating a sense of urgency and personal connection in donation appeals can motivate potential donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, particularly when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
As for the effect on email click-through rate, the treatment email saw no difference in click-through rate. There was a 3.9% decrease in clicks with only a 42% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of personalization and emotional appeals in donation requests. By consistently incorporating these elements into fundraising campaigns, nonprofits like American Cornerstone Institute may be able to further increase engagement, donations, and overall success of their campaigns.

experiments.title: How shortening email copy for a content offer impacts non-donor signups and donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening email copy for non donors will achieve a change in content offer KPIs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2024 - 09/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation, we wanted to test whether shorter, more-to-the-point email copy impacted clicks and email signups, as well as donation conversions.
This experiment result is for non-donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that shortening email copy for non-donors led to a significant increase in clicks for all traffic. The treatment group saw a 14.2% increase in clicks with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the change was not due to random chance.
Moreover, when looking at downstream impacts of this test:
The treatment version had 6% more email signups, with an 80% level of confidenceThe treatment version had a 50% increase in donations post email-signup when looking at total emails deployed, with a 56% level of confidence (donation sample size was small, however). When comparing this number for those who signed up and THEN donated, the treatment version had a 49% increase in donations with a 56% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a personal ask to match a gift amount affects overall conversions in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal ask to match a gift for donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because  it creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, especially when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/04/2024
The Navigators wanted to test how a more personal approach to asking to match a gift on Giving Tuesday would affect overall conversion rates including clickthrough rate from the email to donation page and donation conversion rate.
They tested this concept within the four emails they sent on Giving Tuesday. The control group received the same four email and the treatment group received the same treatment emails.
The control emails presented the matching gift by stating a generous donor has offered to match all gifts up the gift amount like below –
This is vital and exciting work! And we have a generous donor who agrees and who has committed $100,000 as a matching challenge to encourage others of like heart to show their generosity today on Giving Tuesday.
Whereas the treatment emails presented the matching gift by stating there was a generous donor who wanted to personally match their gift like below –
This is vital and exciting work! And we have a generous donor who would like to encourage you to give personally, [First name] through a matching challenge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a personal ask to match a gift for donors significantly increases the clickthrough rate from the email to the donation page. The treatment group, which received the personalized matching gift ask, saw a 57.0% increase in clicks compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, demonstrating that the treatment was more effective in driving donor engagement.
Additionally, when comparing clicks from the email to donations on the donation page, we saw a 30.6% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 99% level of confidence. But we believe these numbers to be skewed since the treatment sent so much more traffic to the donation page. Therefore we calculated the email delivered to donation conversion rate and saw a 9% increase in donor conversion rate with a 67.7% level of confidence.
Although those results did not validate, we believe this test to have an overall positive impact as it drove so much more traffic to the donation page.
In future experimentation, The Navigators should consider implementing personalized asks for matching gifts in their communications to donors. This approach creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact. By offering donors the opportunity to double their support through matching, organizations can potentially increase donor engagement and achieve higher conversion rates. It is important to continue testing and optimizing personalized approaches to matching gift asks to further enhance donor response and maximize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How a donor goal and exclusivity language in a high-urgency email appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a donor goal and exclusivity language for Reason to Believe’s housefile appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it leverages that the donor is part of a special group, and breaks the goal into something they can visualize their impact upon.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2024 - 12/03/2024
This year, rather than celebrating Giving Tuesday, Reasons to Believe decided to launch the organization’s very first RTB Giving Day. This was a high-urgency appeal that would last only one-day, with the goal of raising $15,000. We hypothesized that translating this to a 180 donor goal rather than a revenue goal, would increase donor conversion rate for two reasons. First, we believed that it would help underscore these donors as being part of a special group — donors to the first RTB Giving Day; second, we believed that this would help break the more intimidating $15,000 goal into something manageable that the donor could more easily see their own impact in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment illustrate that the use of a donor goal and exclusivity language in the appeal significantly increased the donor conversion rate for Reasons to Believe's RTB Giving Day campaign. This approach helped emphasize the donors' participation in a special group and made the donation goal more manageable and tangible for the donors. The treatment group, which received the donor goal and exclusivity language, saw a 54.0% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence (95.3%).
In future experimentation, we should consider the context around a high-urgency goal and the donor's place in them. The amount of time communicated to reach the goal and perceived difficulty of reaching the goal can all be things that potentially discourage the donor from understanding their impact rather than communicating it. Within the context of a 24-hour appeal and the efforts of RTB to start something unique, it appears that speaking directly to the donors needed to accomplish this was most effective.

experiments.title: How focusing copy in a donor email on a specific course being promoted impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on a course for donors will achieve a change in offer performance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/19/2024 - 09/19/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of the Blueprint for America course, we wanted to see whether a variant of an email that focused on the course specifically would have an impact on sign ups and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that focusing on a course specifically for donors did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing sign-ups and donations. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence.

What's even more interesting is looking at this experiment from a full funnel perspective.

In terms of email signups, the treatment version had 19% LESS signups than the control, with an 86% level of confidence.

The treatment version also had a 6% decline in instant donations after people signed up, albeit with just a 14% level of confidence.

In this experiment, the control, which had a much more conversational tone to it, clearly outperformed the email that focused on the course itself.

experiments.title: How adding a popularity number impacted email clicks and donations on a NonDonor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that refreshing the email copy for Hoover’s non-donor audience will achieve an increase in clicks and conversions because they see that other people wanted this with the “30,000 people” callout.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2024 - 11/13/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Victory and the Savior Generals. This experiment was for their non-donor audience.
We wanted to update the copy to have these updates:
Shorter
Mentions well-known and respected Hoover fellow, Victor Davis Hanson, at the beginning of email
And have the callout “Already downloaded by more than 30,000 people in the last 6 months, you don’t want to miss your chance to learn from history’s most influential leaders from one of our country’s most esteemed historians.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Updating the email copy to be shorter and mentioning Hoover fellow Victor Davis Hanson at the beginning proved to be effective in driving engagement. This indicates that concise and personalized content resonates well with the non-donor audience.

2. The callout highlighting the popularity of the offer with "Already downloaded by more than 30,000 people in the last 6 months" likely created a sense of urgency and social proof, contributing to the increase in clicks and conversions.

3. The treatment showed a significant 11.2% increase in Clicks for All Traffic, a 19% increase in Opens, and a 117% decrease in Clicks to Opens, all with high levels of statistical confidence.

 

However, with the donations Version B showed a 30% decrease in number of donations with a 79% level of confidence.

With total revenue being:

Version A: $1415 with 30 gifts

Version B: $890 with 21 gifts

 

 

To leverage these learnings in future experimentation, Hoover Institution should consider:

1. Continuing to focus on crafting concise and personalized content that resonates with the audience.

2. Utilizing social proof and urgency in callouts to drive engagement and conversions.

By applying these learnings, Hoover Institution can optimize their email marketing strategy to better engage their non-donor audience and drive conversions effectively.

experiments.title: How changing the tone and structure of an email impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the tone and structure for email subscribers will achieve an increase in engagement because the content is present more encouraging and easier to read.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/12/2024
PTV has seen a decrease in performance for this specific offer so we decided to take a radical approach to address a few different opportunities.
Key Differences Between Versions
Tone and Phrasing:
Email A has a more direct and serious tone, emphasizing the certainty of heaven as a “real, tangible place.”
Email B uses a slightly more conversational tone with phrases like, “Have you ever wondered…” and “It’s a question that many of us ponder.”
Positioning of Benefits:
Email A frames the book as a resource to “encourage you and help you prepare for the ultimate destination.”
Email B frames the book as one that will “fill you with hope, curiosity, and confidence.”
Call to Action Language:
Email A says, “I’d love to send you a copy.”
Email B says, “I’d be honored to send you a copy.”
P.S. Section Structure and Content:
The P.S. sections in both emails include different phrasing and lists of additional resources, potentially impacting donor interest.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the tone, phrasing, positioning of benefits, and call to action language in email communications can have a significant impact on donor engagement. The treatment email, with its more conversational tone and positive framing of benefits, resulted in a 66.2% increase in revenue for all traffic compared to the control email. Although the statistical confidence level of 95.0% was not reached, the results are promising and suggest that further testing should be done to validate the findings.

experiments.title: How does a button CTA in an email impact engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button CTA for email subscribers will achieve a higher engagement because it is a clear and obvious place to click and take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/05/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a featured resource. For November 2024, this is the book “A Place Called Heaven”
The main CTA in PTV featured resource emails is to make a generous gift of any amount and receive the resource as a thank you. Periodically we will do a PS where the subscriber can make a larger donation to receive a bundle that include the monthly resource.
For this email, we decided to test the PS vs a button CTA to request the book.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button CTA for email subscribers resulted in a significant increase in engagement compared to the PS (post-script) CTA. The treatment group, which included the button CTA, saw a 74.0% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% level of statistical confidence, indicating that the button CTA was more effective in encouraging subscribers to take action.
While the control offers a PS to make a larger gift, we interestingly saw a slightly higher average gift for the treatment version $28 vs $30.
For donations we saw no change between the control or treatment.
This test is intriguing, not only that the button received more clicks, but that we did not see a decrease in revenue or gifts given.

experiments.title: How more hyperlinks within a high-urgency campaign email appeal impact email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a higher number of links  for Eagle Lake email appeal recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because it gives the reader more opportunities to navigate to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 between June and July to raise money for the camps they host.
We have seen success using more hyperlinks within an email appeals in terms of both unique clicks and transactions, therefore, we wanted to try testing this for The Navigators.
The control maintained the number of clicks that we used in each of the previous appeal emails – typically 1, occasionally 2. However, the treatment contained a total of 5 links to the donation page.
Our hypothesis was that giving the reader more opportunities to navigate to the donation page the more likely they are to click.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the number of links in email appeals for The Navigators' Eagle Lake campaign led to a significant increase in clicks from recipients. The treatment group, which had 5 links to the donation page, saw a 32.8% increase in clicks compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a confidence level of 97.5%. These results are valid - meaning it is recommended to use this tactic moving forward if your goal is to increase email clicks.
Further, we also wanted to see what the downstream metrics looked like on the donation page for this test. From email clicks to donations, the treatment saw a 10.5% decrease in transactions at a 40.4% level of confidence. While from email sends to donations, the treatment actually saw an 18.9% lift in transactions at a 52.4% level of confidence.
It is important to view both of these metrics as the different perspectives offer very different results. While these metrics did not validate, it is important to remember that when we push more traffic to a donation page, the instant conversion rate with decrease. But in actuality, there was an 18.9% lift in transactions.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of the number of links in email appeals. This suggests that providing recipients with more opportunities to navigate to the donation page can result in higher engagement and potentially more donations. It would be interesting to test different variations of the number of links to determine the optimal amount for maximizing clicks and conversions.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of testing and optimizing email strategies to enhance the effectiveness of fundraising campaigns for The Navigators.

experiments.title: How a relevant image in a high urgency email appeal impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a relevant image for Eagle Lake 2 email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because people give to people and it would establish a more personable connection to the camp.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 between June and July to raise money for the camps they host.
The Navigators audience is very comfortable and familiar with Abby Anderson, the main sender we use for their emails and texts. Therefore, we wanted to see whether an image of Abby at Eagle Lake camps when she was young would influence email clicks and increase donations as a result.
Our hypothesis was that the image of Abby would allow the recipient to feel more connected to Eagle Lake camps therefore increasing the likelihood that they would click the donation link, then give a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a relevant image of Abby at Eagle Lake camps in the email led to a significant increase in clicks for all traffic. The treatment group, which received the email with the image, saw a 48.1% increase in clicks compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 99.9% confidence level, indicating a strong impact of the image on recipient engagement.
These results are valid and therefore, we should consider using relevant images within appeal emails moving forward.
We also pulled number of donations out of the number of clicks within this test and saw an invalid 25.7 decrease in donations at an 85.9% level of confidence. As well as number of donations put of the number of email sends resulted in a 10.1% lift in donations at a 33.4% level of confidence.
We decided to view both of these metrics because based if we only viewed the number of donations out of clickthrough rate, it appears that the image of Abby decreased conversions, but in actuality, there was a 10% increase in donations. It only seems as though there was a drop because we pushed a larger group of people to the donation page.
Neither of these metrics validated, but the results do offer helpful learnings and context of The Navigators audience.
This experiment demonstrates the effectiveness of using personal, relatable images to establish a more personable connection with the audience and drive engagement. In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue incorporating relevant images of people or scenarios that resonate with the audience to increase interaction and response rates. Additionally, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of testing different elements in email campaigns to identify strategies that yield the best results and improve overall campaign performance.

experiments.title: How a Facebook call to action impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the CTA to Download for Facebook traffic will achieve higher conversions because the action step is clear.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/15/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University.
Hoover runs many offers through Facebook ads, therefore in an effort to increase donor conversions on content offers, we decided to test the standard Learn More CTA vs Download CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the Call to Action (CTA) from "Learn More" to "Download" for Facebook traffic led to a significant decrease in donations. The treatment group, which had the "Download" CTA, saw a 56.1% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

This result suggests that the action step of downloading may not have been clear or compelling enough for the Facebook audience, leading to a decrease in conversions. It is important to note that this experiment achieved a high statistical level of confidence, indicating that the results are reliable and likely to represent the true outcome.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to consider the specific characteristics and preferences of the target audience when choosing and testing different CTAs. It may be helpful to gather feedback from the audience or conduct user research to better understand their motivations and expectations.

Additionally, it is essential to track and analyze the impact of any changes in CTAs on different metrics, such as conversions, click-through rates, and engagement. A/B testing allows for data-driven decision-making and helps identify what works best for maximizing the desired outcomes.
