experiments.title: How using a faux forward during a high urgency campaign impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a faux forward for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 11/29/2022
Belmont Abbey College is a university in North Carolina whose mission is to educate students in the liberal arts and sciences so that in all things God may be glorified. In this endeavor, they are guided by the Catholic intellectual tradition and the Benedictine spirit of prayer and learning.
Their Giving Tuesday campaign had a goal of raising $30,000 in a single day; this experiment focused on their donor segment, and looked at whether using a faux forward tactic in email, versus a resend of the AM email, could positively influence donor conversion rates.
Our hypothesis with this experiment is that by using a faux forward, we would be able to cut through the inbox clutter potential donors experience on Giving Tuesday, and thus increase conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the results of this experiment show that using a faux forward had a dramatic increase in conversion rates, this test did not have enough total donations to really show a valid result.

Moreover, the copy on the faux forward version referenced a micro-goal, whereas the control just had the overall goal, so our hypothesis is that it was a combination of that, plus the faux forward, that led to people donating more. This is further reinforced by the fact that open rates for both versions were essentially the same (23% versus 24%).

Further refinement of this test is warranted.

experiments.title: Does Longer Copy Affect Clicks in Email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing email length for an appeal email will achieve a higher click rate because the extra value proposition will increase motivation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding length, an additional value proposition, would increase the likelihood of somebody clicking through the the desired action.
This specific email that we tested was the first appeal of the FYE campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. In this experiment, increasing the email length for an appeal email did not achieve a higher click rate. On the contrary, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in clicks compared to the control group.

2. The extra value proposition included in the longer email did not motivate recipients to click through to the desired action as hypothesized.

3. The control group, which received the original length email, outperformed the treatment group in terms of click rate.

Future Implications:

1. When crafting appeal emails, it may be more effective to keep the message concise and to the point rather than adding extra content that may not necessarily resonate with the audience.

2. It is essential to test assumptions and hypotheses through A/B testing to ensure that changes to email content or format are truly impactful before implementing them across all communications.

3. Consider conducting further research or surveys to better understand the preferences and motivations of the target audience to tailor future email campaigns more effectively.

4. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future email marketing strategies and continue to iterate and test different approaches to optimize click-through rates.

experiments.title: How an additional Call-to-Action early in an email impacts clicks and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: it clarifies the context of the email within the campaign early-on and ensures anyone who doesn’t finish the full email doesn’t miss an ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2024 - 05/06/2024
For MAF’s 2024 Plane Maintenance campaign, the 7th email in the campaign was written to include an insightful story about the types of repairs these planes need in order to deliver the gospel. However, with no call-to-action until the very end of the email we hypothesized that users who do not read the email to the end may not even realize that the email is part of the campaign. We believe that adding an additional call-to-action early in the email copy that contextualized the communication and links directly to the donation page would improve click-through and donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including an additional call-to-action and donation page link early in a campaign email for Mission Aviation Fellowship's housefile can significantly increase clicks and donor conversion. In this specific case, the treatment version that included an early call-to-action saw a 17.4% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 95.9% statistical level of confidence compared to the control version.

Downstream Donor Conversion:

Control: 11,847 Samples | 5 Donations | Conv Rate 0.0%

Treatment: 11,721 Samples. |9 Donations | Conv Rate 0.1% | 81.9% Relative Difference | 72.3% Significance. 

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that campaign emails clearly communicate the purpose and include a strong call-to-action early on to capture the attention of recipients who may not read the entire email. By providing a direct link to the donation page, recipients are more likely to understand the context of the email and take action immediately, leading to higher engagement and conversion rates. This experiment highlights the importance of optimizing email content to drive desired outcomes and should be taken into consideration in future email marketing campaigns for Mission Aviation Fellowship.

experiments.title: How an email written at a 9th grade reading level impacts click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing copy at a lower reading level for people on ACI’s housefile will achieve a higher click-through rate because it will be easier for people to read and comprehend.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2024 - 04/26/2024
ACI was launching a new pledge to their housefile. While writing copy for this offer, we wondered if writing the copy at a lower reading level would impact click-through rate. We typically write copy at a 12th grade reading level for this client’s offers. For this experiment, we tried writing copy at a 9th grade level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that writing copy at a lower reading level for people on ACI's housefile did not result in a higher click-through rate. In fact, the treatment group, which received the copy at a 9th grade reading level, experienced a 33.7% decrease in clicks compared to the control group that received the copy at a 12th grade reading level. This outcome was statistically significant with a 99.9% level of confidence.

Moving forward, it is important to carefully consider and test hypotheses before making changes to copywriting strategies. While the intention was to make the content more accessible to readers, the results showed that the original approach might have been more effective in engaging the audience. Future experimentation should focus on testing different variables and closely monitoring the outcomes to make informed decisions about optimizing copy for better performance.

experiments.title: How letting lapsed members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, are appreciated affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Letting lapsed members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they will know their gift is helping support the mission
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if more lapsed members would click if they knew they would receive a Member’s Card no matter how much they gave. The treatment included the phrase, “Any amount you give is greatly appreciated” as part of the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that explicitly stating "Any amount you give is greatly appreciated" in the call to action message decreased click-through rates for lapsed members in The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment version with this added phrase saw a 34.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control version without the phrase. The conversion rate for the treatment email was also lower.

From this experiment, it is clear that the additional message about any amount being appreciated did not resonate positively with lapsed members. In future experimentation, it would be advisable to avoid incorporating similar messaging that emphasizes the size of the donation being irrelevant or downplays the importance of the gift. Instead, focusing on the impact of the donation in supporting the mission and achieving tangible outcomes may be more effective in encouraging lapsed members to re-engage and contribute.

Additionally, it is essential to consider the messaging context and align it closely with the organization's mission and values to ensure relevance and connection with the target audience. Experimentation should continue to test different messaging approaches, calls to action, and value propositions to identify what resonates best with lapsed members and drives higher engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How placing an additional call to action higher in the copy of a fundraising email for members affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding another call to action sooner in a fundraising email for current members will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they already support this organization and understand how their gift will be beneficial.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click if a call to action was placed earlier in the body of the email. Since members are familiar with the mission, values, and issues of the Heritage Foundation they wouldn’t need as much information to make an additional donation.
The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding a call to action sooner in a fundraising email for current members can significantly increase click-through and conversion rates.

2. Providing a clear and concise call to action early in the email can effectively prompt members to take action.

3. Current members of The Heritage Foundation are more likely to respond to donation requests since they already understand the organization's mission and values.

Note: The treatment email also received more conversations, though not at a significant statistical difference.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the placement and wording of calls to action in emails to optimize engagement and donation rates. Utilizing the knowledge that current members are more likely to donate, future emails can be structured to emphasize the impact of their contributions and make it easy for them to support the organization. Additionally, incorporating matching gift opportunities and other incentives can further motivate members to donate.

We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked non-members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if non-members would click if the matching gift offer was placed earlier in the body of the email. The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action that mentioned the matching gift opportunity after the second paragraph of the email.

The key learning from this experiment is that adding the matching gift offer sooner in a fundraising email did not increase the click-through rate for The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment saw a 7.6% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. This suggests that the incentive to give may not be as effective when emphasized early in the email.
Note: The conversion rate also decreased from the treatment email.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the placement of incentives in fundraising emails. The timing and presentation of matching gift offers and other incentives can have a significant impact on donor response rates. It may be beneficial to test different approaches to determine the most effective strategy for motivating recipients to donate. experiments.title: How adding 3 links to a fundraising email for non-members affects the click-through rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having three calls to action throughout an email for non-members will achieve a higher click-through rate and lead to an increase in donations because it provides more opportunity to take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2024 - 03/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. In the email, the President thanked non-members for their generosity and the issues America is facing. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if having three hyperlinked calls to action placed at the beginning, middle, and end of the email would encourage non-members to click more than having only one call to action at the end.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having three calls to action throughout an email for non-members resulted in a significantly higher click-through rate compared to having only one call to action at the end. The treatment group, which had three hyperlinked calls to action placed at the beginning, middle, and end of the email, saw a 100.2% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

Note: While the click-through rate increased the donation conversion rate was the same for both the control and treatment email.

In future experimentation, this learning should be applied by incorporating multiple calls to action strategically throughout emails or communication pieces. By providing more opportunities for recipients to take action, it increases the likelihood of engagement and conversion. Additionally, testing different placements and wording for these calls to action could further optimize the approach to maximize results. This experiment underscores the importance of experimentation and testing in marketing strategies to identify the most effective tactics for achieving goals.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits of a premium affect clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that explaining the benefits of a premium book for potential donors will achieve an increase in clicks because  the additional clarity on the premium will motivate them to click the link and learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test calling out the perceived benefits of the premium in an effort to pinpoint motivating factors for their audience. The control email called out the impact that their monthly gift would have on the organization whereas the treatment called out the benefits of reading the book that they would receive as a premium for becoming a monthly donor.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that explaining the benefits of a premium book for potential donors led to a significant increase in clicks compared to highlighting the impact of a monthly gift. The treatment saw a 27.8% lift in clicks at a 98.8% level of confidence, indicating that these results are valid. We can conclude that providing additional clarity and tangible benefits to potential donors can be a motivating factor for them to take action, and we should continue to utilize this motivation in future appeals. This test also suggests that using impact language may not be effective for their audience.
In future experimentation, we should continue to emphasize the benefits and values of becoming a monthly partner, such as offering premium gifts or exclusive content. This approach has shown to be effective in increasing engagement and click-through rates. Additionally, we should consider testing different types of premiums or incentives to see which resonates the most with our audience. By continually testing and optimizing our messaging and offerings, we can better understand what motivates donors and improve the overall effectiveness of our campaigns.

experiments.title: How does following up a direct mail renewal note with an email impact conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by sending a follow up email for Pathway Magazine subscribers that are ready for renewal will achieve a higher conversion rate because this is an additional touch point to the direct mail note.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
6 times a year, PTV puts out Pathway Magazine. A resource that includes daily devotionals for 2 months, articles by Dr. Jeffress and opportunities to learn about PTV’s newest books.
An individual can sign up to receive 3 free issues of Pathway Magazine, but on the 3rd issue, PTV attaches a message that this is the last issue unless a missional gift of $20 is made to support PTV.
The effectiveness of this note has been minimal, therefore we decided to test to see if a follow-up email would generate additional renewals.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment conducted by Pathway to Victory is that sending a follow-up email to Pathway Magazine subscribers who are due for renewal significantly increased donations compared to the traditional direct mail note. The treatment group, which received the follow-up email, experienced a 132.1% increase in donations with a 99.2% statistical level of confidence.
This experiment demonstrates the importance of utilizing multiple touchpoints to engage with subscribers and donors. By incorporating email communication as a follow-up to direct mail efforts, organizations like Pathway to Victory can enhance donor retention and increase overall engagement.
Key points of this experiment.
Only contacts with email addresses were included in the experimentDonations are all donations given either through direct mail or emailTreatment saw 17 gifts through direct mail, 12 gifts through email, and 1 donor contributed via email and direct mail for this appeal

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits associated with becoming a recurring donor impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the benefits of becoming a monthly partner for the donor will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the perceived exclusivity and incentives associated with becoming a monthly partner are more valuable to the user.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/01/2024
For The Navigator’s February Sustainers Appeal, we hypothesized that calling out the benefits of becoming a monthly partner would increase our click-through rate as well as our donor conversion rate. The control email emphasized the importance and need for monthly partners, focusing heavily on the difference it would make for the organization. Whereas, the treatment focused on the benefits for the donor themselves.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted for The Navigator's February Sustainers Appeal tested the impact of emphasizing the benefits of becoming a monthly partner for the donor, as opposed to focusing on the organization's need for monthly partners.

The treatment email, which highlighted the benefits for the donor, showed a 161.3% increase in donations for All Traffic compared to the control email with a 99.6% level of confidence. These results did validate based on the 95% level of confidence that we strive for. Therefore, we can conclude that the perceived benefits of becoming a monthly donor were very effective at motivating individuals to take the next step and initiate a recurring gift. This also suggests that the incentives will work well for The Navigators audience.

In future experimentation, it would be interesting to test where we can apply this concept to other campaigns and initiatives of the Navigators, including other appeals or their Eagle Lake campaign.

experiments.title: How offering a book as a premium with a donation vs at retail price impacts conversion rate and overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a new book as a premium gift in exchange for a donation rather than as a product for purchase for Time of Grace’s donor file will achieve an increase in transactions and revenue because users are more likely to be motivated by the generosity of the organization, and the value of receiving the gift for a donation.. because users are more likely to be motivated by the generosity of the organization, and the value of receiving the gift for a donation..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/16/2024
For Time of Grace’s monthly book promotion, we hypothesized that offering the book in exchange for a donation would result in increased transactions and revenue versus just offering it for purchase. Historically, Time of Grace has promoted their new books on their Shopify store at retail price. This test was accomplished by making a change to two areas of the funnel. The control vs treatment emails would have a change in the call-to-action copy. The control (for purchase) would lead them to Time of Grace’s Shopify page, whereas the treatment would lead them to a donation page that had the book as a premium on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a new book as a premium gift in exchange for a donation, instead of selling it at retail price, significantly increased the number of donations received. The treatment group saw a 92.2% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a high statistical level of confidence.

Not only did this result in an increase in donor/purchaser conversion rate, it also resulted in a valid 199% increase in revenue with a 98.9% level of confidence.

Control: 27 Purchases | $996 in Revenue | $36.89 Average Gift

Treatment 52 Donations |$2,985 in Revenue | $57.40 Average Gift

This approach also did not limit the ministry's impact in actual copies "sold" with 53 units being purchased under the control and 55 units being requested as premiums with a donation.

This indicates that users are more motivated by the generosity of the organization and the perceived value of receiving a gift in exchange for their donation. Therefore, in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue offering premium gifts as incentives for donations.

This could be applied to other products or promotions as well, where offering a premium gift or exclusive content in exchange for a donation or purchase may motivate users to take action and increase overall transactions and revenue. The organization should consider implementing this strategy more frequently to leverage the increased motivation and generosity of their audience.

Moody historically has used buttons and very heavily designed emails. I believe we see a sharp decline in this specific format because a link is harder to see in the HTML version.

While this is still a valuable learning to see how the audience responds to this, I believe it will need to be retested to see if it produces the same result as a personal email.

experiments.title: How leading a membership ask email with the problem for non-members affects click-through rate and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading an email with the problem will increase email click-through rate because it will give the reader more context as to why they should renew their membership with Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2024 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran on the first email of the campaign for non-members to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how non-members would react to leading the email with a question that poses the problem Heritage is trying to solve rather than leading the email with a direct ask to become a member. In doing so, we believe users will be more likely to click through to the donation page and activate their membership because the leading question allows them to relate to the issue and want to be apart of the change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leading an email with a problem statement did not increase email click-through rates for new members. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 39% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. In addition, the treatment group saw a 61% decrease in donations with a 99.9% level of confidence.

Based on this result, it is clear that the approach of posing a problem as the lead in the email is not effective in motivating non-members to click through and make a donation. In future experimentation, it would be advisable to consider alternative approaches to engage non-members and encourage them to become members. This could involve highlighting the benefits of membership, showcasing successful outcomes of past campaigns, or offering exclusive content or promotions for members. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further research to understand the reasons behind the negative response to the problem statement approach, such as through qualitative interviews or surveys.

experiments.title: How a simplified message that focuses only on impact in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects conversions for lapsed members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the message and only focusing on the impact in the final email of the campaign will produce a higher conversion rate because the treatment email presents a more straightforward and concise message, which can be easier for recipients to understand quickly.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how a concise message that only focuses on their impact in the final email of the campaign would affect conversion rates. We believe a clear and simple call-to-action may lead to a better response as it reduces cognitive load for the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the message and focusing only on the impact in the final email of the campaign resulted in a higher conversion rate. The treatment email, which presented a more straightforward and concise message, saw a 37.4% increase in clicks compared to the control email with a 96.4% level of confidence. Additionally, we saw a 15.4% decrease in donor conversion with only a 43% level of confidence which tells us that the change in email copy did not affect the motivation to give on the donation page.
Based on this learning, future experimentation should prioritize clear and simple call-to-action messages that highlight the impact of the organization. By reducing cognitive load for the readers and making it easier for them to understand the value of their membership, we can improve the conversion rates and encourage more individuals to reactivate their memberships.
Additionally, it is important to continue conducting A/B testing to validate and refine these findings. By testing different variations of messages, designs, and strategies, we can further optimize our campaigns and ensure that we are effectively engaging our audience.

The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the message and focusing on the impact in the final email of a campaign can lead to a higher conversion rate. The treatment email, which presented a more straightforward and concise message, resulted in a 9.5% increase in clicks for all traffic. experiments.title: How does calling out the loss of exclusive membership benefits for members affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out what they will lose by not renewing their membership for existing members will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the fear of losing something can be a more powerful motivator than the prospect of gaining something of equal value.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was sent to existing members in 2023 to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how existing members would react to calling out what they will lose by not renewing their membership in 2024.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that calling out what existing members will lose by not renewing their membership can be a powerful motivator for donations. The treatment that mentioned the potential loss saw a 41.9% increase in donations compared to the control group with a 99.4% level of confidence.
This suggests that emphasizing the negative consequences of not renewing membership can be more effective than highlighting the positive benefits or incentives of renewing. It taps into the fear of missing out and the desire to avoid losing something valuable.
To apply this learning in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to test different variations of messaging that focus on the potential loss. This could include highlighting specific benefits or privileges that members would no longer have access to, such as exclusive content, events, or discounts. It may also be effective to use personalized messaging that reminds individuals of the specific contributions they have made and the impact they have had as a member.
Overall, the key takeaway is to leverage the fear of loss as a motivator in membership renewal campaigns. By highlighting what individuals will miss out on if they don't renew, nonprofits like The Heritage Foundation can increase the likelihood of conversions and maintain their supporter base.

experiments.title: How leading a membership ask email with the problem for existing members affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading an email with the problem will increase email click-through rate because it will give the reader more context as to why they should renew their membership with Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran on the first email of the campaign for existing members in 2023 to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how existing members would react to leading the email with a question that poses the problem Heritage is trying to solve rather than leading the email with a direct ask to renew their membership. In doing so, we believe users will be more likely to click through to the donation page and renew their membership because the leading question allows them to relate to the issue and want to be apart of the change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leading an email with a question that poses the problem did not increase email click-through rate for existing members to renew their membership with The Heritage Foundation. In fact, the treatment group showed a 55.8% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. In addition, the treatment group saw a 23.5% decrease in donor conversion with a 97.6% level of confidence.

These findings suggest that existing members may not respond well to a problem-focused approach when it comes to renewing their membership. It is possible that they already have a strong understanding of the problem and are more interested in the direct ask to renew their support.

In future experimentation, it may be more effective to lead the email with a direct ask to renew membership rather than focusing on the problem. Additionally, it could be beneficial to test other strategies such as emphasizing the impact of their support or offering a specific benefit or incentive for renewing. These approaches may help to engage existing members and encourage them to click through and renew their membership.

experiments.title: How a specific donation ask in an email for non-donors impacted the email click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking non-donors for a gift of $10 or more instead of a gift of any amount will increase the click rate because of the added clarity in the copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2023 - 11/28/2023
Luther Seminary participated in Giving Tuesday and wanted to try to increase traffic to the donation page and hopefully also increase overall donations.
In order to achieve this, we wanted to see how adding a specified ask amount for nondonors would impact the click rate overall.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a specified ask amount for nondonors significantly increased the click rate overall. The treatment version, which asked for a gift of $10 or more, resulted in a 57.2% increase in cilcks with a high level of confidence.
This suggests that the added clarity in the copy, specifying the amount to be donated, is effective in motivating people to click and donate. It gives potential donors a concrete expectation and may make them more likely to take action.
However, for the overall donor conversion rate, there was a 4.4% increase in donation conversions at a 14.1% level of confidence. This means that while we can say this factor did lead to an increase in clicks, we can't with confidence say that this will always lead to an increase in donations.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing and refining the specified ask amount. Different donation amounts could be tested to see which has the greatest impact on click rate and donations. Additionally, exploring other ways to increase clarity and motivation in the copy, such as highlighting the impact of the donation, could also be tested.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of clear and specific messaging in fundraising campaigns. Providing a specific ask amount can help potential donors understand what is being asked of them and may increase their likelihood of taking action.

experiments.title: How moving the problem to the front of the email affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the problem earlier in the email for email recipients will achieve an increase in conversion rate because more readers will understand the problem to which they can be the solution.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/14/2023
The initial email of the Navigators fall Sustainers campaign used the example of how their team was able to go from “zero to one” to get missionaries on a specific college campus in Austin. However, this fact was buried in the email, which started with mission language that was somewhat organizational-centric.
The team hypothesized that the fact that a large college had ZERO missionaries on campus would be compelling and would engage more people if it was moved to the beginning of the email.
They believed by confronting the reader with this fact (and the fact that, thanks to donors, there now ARE missionaries on campus) that they would get more donations because more readers would understand the impact of their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Positioning the problem earlier in the email was effective: By highlighting the fact that a large college had zero missionaries on campus and emphasizing the impact of donor support in solving this problem, the treatment email achieved a significant increase in donations. This suggests that readers are more likely to respond when confronted with a clear problem and understand their role in solving it.

2. Donors want to see the impact of their gift: The fact that the treatment email successfully conveyed the impact of donor support in addressing the problem suggests that donors want to know how their contributions are making a difference. Future experiments should focus on highlighting the tangible outcomes and benefits that donors can achieve through their support.

3. Consider the reader's perspective: The original email was somewhat organizational-centric, starting with mission language. However, by repositioning the problem statement, the treatment email took into account the reader's perspective and engaged them more effectively. Future experimentation should continue to prioritize the reader's perspective and focus on conveying the message in a way that resonates with them.

Based on these learnings, future experimentation should continue to explore ways to highlight the problem and its impact early in the communication. Using donor-centered language and emphasizing the tangible outcomes of their support can help increase engagement and conversion rates. Additionally, ongoing testing should be conducted to fine-tune the messaging and ensure that it aligns with the readers' motivations and interests.

experiments.title: How emphasizing a premium instead of a notable figure affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing the book for email prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will increase the clarity of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/10/2023
American Cornerstone Institute had previously learned from experimentation that using their figurehead, Dr. Ben Carson, in their marketing helped improve conversion. But as they offered their new resource “Why America Matters”, if using Dr. Carson actually obscured the product that people would receive if they gave a gift — the book. They decided to test against their previous learning and see showing the book improved conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including the book image, in the marketing material significantly increased the conversion rate. The book-focused treatment version resulted in a 73.9% increase in donations compared to the control version.
This is an important learning because it means that previous learnings are not "one-size-fits-all". While Dr. Carson can provide familiarity for the organizational-level value proposition, it appears to be more effective to show the offer at the product-level value proposition.

experiments.title: How adding a second link impacted conversions for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a second link for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate because there are more opportunities to click .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2023 - 11/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
This is an experiment we ran for PTV a couple of years ago, but we wanted to test again to see if the results still held.
We tested a second link in the email to see if it would increase engagement and down funnel donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a second link in the email did not result in a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version saw a significant decrease in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group.

2. Clicks to Opens, which indicates the engagement level of subscribers, also experienced a significant decrease with the treatment version. This suggests that the added link did not capture their attention or encourage them to take action.

3 Of those that received the treatment, only 30% clicked the first link while 70% clicked the second link.

4 Donations saw a 14% increase on the treatment but only a 74% level of confidence.

 

Based on these results, the addition of a second link did not increase engagement.

experiments.title: How sending a follow up email impacted revenue from a text campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by sending a following email for text message recipients will achieve a higher conversion rate  because there is an additional touchpoint through another personal channel.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/26/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
In promoting a new book, we decided to test a follow-up email to half of the audience that received a text message.
For PTV, they rarely use text messages due to cost, and they never do it to the full file, they pick and choose select segments depending on offer, time of the year, and segment that needs extra activation. There are several reasons why we wanted to test this approach.
For one, following up with email, a known and regularly used channel, can help verify that the text wasn’t spam, as that is popular.
Second, if you have multiple channels that can be personalized and timed accurately, why not have those channels work together and support one another? And not in a just more than one is better. But the email called out the text. They are directly connected and not just random that someone received two different things from PTV.
In the case of PTV specifically, they typically suppress text recipients from receiving emails that week so as not to steal results from text. But we are able to show that that isn’t an effective strategy; it’s actually better for channels to play together than being so decisive and siloed
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that sending a follow-up email to text message recipients can significantly increase the conversion rate and revenue generated. The treatment group, which received both a text message and a follow-up email, saw an 80.1% increase in revenue compared to the control group that only received a text message.
This outcome suggests that adding an additional touchpoint through another personal channel, such as email, can be highly effective in capturing the attention and engagement of recipients. It is likely that the email served as a reminder or reinforcement of the message conveyed in the initial text message, leading to a higher conversion rate.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue using the strategy of sending follow-up emails to text message recipients. This can be implemented as a standard practice, potentially leading to consistent improvements in conversion rates and revenue. Additionally, it may be worth exploring the specific content and timing of the follow-up emails to optimize their effectiveness.
Overall, the success of this experiment reinforces the importance of a multi-channel approach in engaging with audiences and increasing conversion rates. By utilizing multiple channels and touchpoints, organizations can enhance their messaging and communication strategies, leading to improved outcomes and revenue generation.

experiments.title: How adding clarity around the length of a survey impacted clicks from non-donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clearly communicating the length of a survey for non-donors will achieve an increase in survey participation because of the enhanced appeal and clarity of the time and effort involved..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/06/2023 - 09/08/2023
When sending a survey to a non-donor audience, we wondered if including the number of questions in a survey within an email would enhance its appeal and clarity for recipients. By stating the number of questions upfront, recipients can gauge the time commitment required, making the survey seem more manageable and less daunting. This transparency builds trust and sets clear expectations, making the value proposition of participating in the survey more compelling. It assures recipients that their time is valued and that the survey won’t be overly lengthy or burdensome.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Clearly communicating the length of a survey upfront enhances its appeal and clarity for recipients.

2. Providing the number of questions in the survey helps recipients gauge the time commitment required, making it seem more manageable and less daunting.

3. Transparency builds trust and sets clear expectations, making the value proposition of participating in the survey more compelling.

4. Clearly communicating the length of the survey resulted in a significant increase in both opens and clicks.

5. The treatment outperformed the control group with a 33.1% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with 99% level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, future experimentation should continue to focus on clear communication and setting expectations with recipients. Testing variations in the way the length of surveys is communicated, such as using different formats or emphasizing the time commitment in different ways, can provide further insights into what resonates most effectively with the target audience.

Additionally, the significant increases in Clicks for All Traffic indicate that clearly communicating the length of a survey motivates more people to click on the survey link if the perceived effort required seems low. Exploring other elements of the email or survey content that could enhance click-through rates can be valuable for future experimentation.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of transparency and setting clear expectations when communicating with non-donor audiences. By incorporating these learnings into future experimentation, Care Net can continue to optimize engagement and ultimately increase survey participation.

experiments.title: How including gift array options in an email impacted clicks in a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a sample gift array for a direct email appeal will achieve an increase in engagement because there will be additional cognitive momentum to continue the process to the next step.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2023 - 07/13/2023
In a high urgency campaign, we typically keep the decisions isolated per each step. In the email step, we help the reader decide “Do I want to click?” and then at the donation page step we help the reader decide “How much do I want to give?” In one email, we wanted to test if adding gift array options at the email level would help more people begin thinking about both questions at once. The treatment version added a simple line of copy, letting the reader know that donations of all sizes and amounts were needed and welcomed and would qualify toward the campaign goal: “Whether you give $10, $100, $1,000 or more, every donation counts.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we learned by running this experiment is that adding a sample gift array in the email increased engagement: The treatment version, which included a line of copy mentioning various donation amounts, resulted in a 23.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that introducing a gift array at the email level can help in generating additional cognitive momentum and increasing engagement. We also believed this may have motivated more people who were considering making a gift but did not have all of the information needed to move forward with the decision, by reminding them that they could have the autonomy if they wanted to choose to give a gift of $10 or $100 and that all gifts would be counted.
Based on these learnings, future experimentation should consider incorporating sample gift arrays or suggested donation amounts in email appeals. This can potentially encourage donors to consider different giving options and increase their likelihood of clicking on the donation link. Additionally, these findings may also be useful in exploring the impact of dynamic gift arrays in other communication that are based on previous giving history—a tactic often used in direct mail letters that we could test applying in the digital space.

experiments.title: How social proof and communal language impacted donor conversion rate in an email update
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using social proof and communal language for those receiving JEN emails will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because   people will identify with being a compassionate person and will want to join others who have donated.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2023
CaringBridge sends out what are called JEN asks. These are auto-generated emails that are sent out to those connected to sites. They provide journal updates and always include an ask in the email. We wanted to test using social proof/communal language to let donors know that they’re joining a larger group of compassionate people when they give. The last test we ran for this experiment generated a decrease in gifts. We had tested using fear of loss language so our hypothesis is that CaringBridge’s audience may respond more positively to joining in on something rather than missing out on something.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Before going through results, it is worth noting that CaringBridge underwent a change in donation platform on the same day that this experiment was launched. Therefore, the results of this experiment were compromised and it should be launched again to confirm results.
The key learning from this experiment is that using social proof and communal language in JEN asks did not result in an increase in donor conversion rate for CaringBridge. In fact, the treatment resulted in a significant decrease in donations compared to the control. We saw a valid 58% decrease in donations resulting in a level of confidence of 99%.
This suggests that CaringBridge's audience may not respond well to communal language and instead, may require a different approach to encourage donations. However, given the change in an additional variable (the donation platform), it's recommended we retest this approach.

experiments.title: How adding privacy language below a survey sign up form impacted sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a more formal privacy statement at the bottom of the form will increase the education survey sign up rate because it will establish more credibility and trust in ACI and the survey itself.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/28/2023
ACI’s Education Survey is producing a 43.3% sign up rate over the last month. While this metric is performing above-benchmark, we believe there is room for improvement for this offer to match the sign up rates of other offers such as the Cancel Culture Pledge (59.5% sign up rate).
In reviewing the current landing page, we wonder if emphasizing the privacy language below the form will build trust with our audience and lead a higher percentage of our audience to submit their survey. We’ve seen this tactic work across other similar organizations.
People can feel vulnerable giving away their information, so adding this language could provide them more comfort and trust and it may be the added punch that this page needs to improve sign up rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a more formal privacy statement at the bottom of the form did not increase the Education Survey sign up rate for ACI. The treatment saw a 4.3% decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic with a 99.5% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control version performed better.
An important metric to review regarding data privacy is new visitors, as they may not have strong familiarity with the organization they'd be giving their information to. In this case, new visitors produced a valid 4.7% decrease in sign up rate, showing that they were actually less likely to submit their information when viewing the data privacy language.
Interestingly, donation revenue increased by 23.7% with an 84.1% level of confidence. Although it did not validate, the results are encouraging and show that those who did build trust through the landing page privacy language did give more once they reached the donation page.
The downstream metrics tell us that we're onto something with this approach. So as a next test, we will test shorter, more direct privacy language and place it below the submit button as a way to maintain the user momentum. We hypothesize that because the privacy language was placed between the form and the submit button for this experiment, this may have introduced friction, making users think twice about  submitting their information. The language should compliment the sign up process instead of impede it.

experiments.title: How fear of loss language impacted donor conversion rate in an email update
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using fear of loss language for those receiving JEN emails will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because   people will be motivated to ensure that others don’t lose their sites and communities.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/09/2023
CaringBridge sends out what are called JEN asks. These are auto-generated emails that are sent out to those connected to sites. They provide journal updates and always include an ask in the email. We wanted to test using fear of loss language to communicate to prospective donors what happens when they don’t give a gift: people don’t have a site and don’t have the community they need when going through their health journeys. We hypothesized that this additional context would increase donor conversion rate through this email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using fear of loss language did not increase donor conversion rate and actually resulted in a decrease in donations. The control version that focused on appreciation for staying connected and powering the site for updates was more effective in motivating donors. The experiment resulted in a valid 19% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 95%.
In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing different messaging that focuses on the benefits of donating rather than the negative consequences of not donating.

experiments.title: How using lapsed donor information in email impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by referencing a donors last gift date and amount for donors lapsed over 365 days will achieve a higher conversion rate because they are reminded that they have previously been invested and the work that has been possible.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/26/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
As we look to reactivate lapsed donors (no gift in over 12 months) we decided to test including information on their last gift date and amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that referencing a donor's last gift date and amount in communications can significantly increase revenue from lapsed donors. The treatment version, which included this information, saw a 282.4% increase in revenue compared to the control version without this information.
In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of personalization in communication with lapsed donors. We can experiment with different ways of incorporating donor history, such as highlighting specific projects or programs that the donor has previously supported. We should also consider segmenting lapsed donors based on their giving history and tailoring messaging accordingly. Overall, the findings suggest that personalization and targeted messaging can be effective in re-engaging lapsed donors and increasing revenue for the organization.

experiments.title: How referencing last gift information for lapsed donor segment impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including last gift date and amount for lapsed donors will achieve a higher conversion of donors because donors are reminded of their last gift and are encouraged to make another gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2023 - 04/19/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
As we look to reactivate lapsed donors (no gift in over 12 months) we decided to test including information on their last gift date and amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by Pathway to Victory tested the inclusion of last gift date and amount for lapsed donors in their donation appeal email. The hypothesis was that reminding donors of their last gift would encourage them to make another donation. However, the results showed a significant decrease in Clicks to Opens for All Traffic (33.1%) with a high level of confidence (98.9%).
On the other hand, the treatment version saw a substantial increase in donations (2,669.6%) (1 donation vs 21 donations) with a level of confidence of 100%. This suggests that including last gift date and amount will cause recipients to make a donation.
When looking at last gift amount compared to the gift given, on average we saw a -$3 in their gift amount. However, the average length of reactivated donors was 3 years since last gift!

experiments.title: How adding intentional name capture links in a high traffic page affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that linking to a landing page instead of a PDF download for users interested in Bible reading plans will allow The Navigators to take advantage of the opportunity for email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2023 - 03/30/2023
One of The Navigators’ most popular resources is its Bible reading plans. We noticed that this particular resource page had a large amount of organic traffic. The existing page setup prioritized direct PDF downloads, but this lack of forms on the page meant that The Navigators’ wasn’t collecting emails or names of those who downloaded these resources. There was an email capture form at the bottom of the page but it’s likely that once someone downloaded a PDF, they would not scroll to the bottom of the page to fill out a form.
We hypothesized that by keeping the page layout the same, but just swapping out the PDF links in favor of a landing page link to each reading plan—would allow the Navigators to keep distributing this resource while also collecting emails in the process.
Additionally, we only targeted those who were first time visitors to the site so as not to diminish ministry impact for the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that swapping out PDF downloads for a landing page link is a more effective approach to acquire email addresses from users interested in Bible reading plans. The A/B test demonstrated a 433% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
Moreover, the experiment also revealed that the new approach was successful across various visitor types such as mobile, desktop, organic search, male, female, and varying age ranges. The highest increase in email acquisition was seen among new visitors, organic search visitors, and female visitors.
The key metric that The Navigators were concerned about was whether this funnel process would mean that the number of those who wanted to get the resource would decrease.
When looking at the control, we had 596 get the instant PDF. In the treatment we saw 64% of those who clicked the link, followed through in filling out the form to get the resource. Meaning, most people actually took the next step to give their information in exchange for the resource. Because we targeted first time visitors, these are likely new names acquired for the organization.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition language to a direct appeal email impacted donations.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a value proposition-enhanced email for email recipients will achieve an increase in donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2022 - 12/30/2022
As part of a high-urgency campaign for a public policy client, we emailed subscribers to their weekly newsletter, The Policy Shop, asking if they would make a donation in support of The Policy Shop. The email was short and sweet, including a cartoon, a thanks for being a part of The Policy Shop, and an ask for support.
We wondered how a more in-depth, value proposition-focused email would impact donations. The treatment version highlights the problem(s) that The Policy Shop helps solve, provides background on The Policy Shop, provides evidentials on the impact of The Policy Shop, and tells the recipients how their gift in support of The Policy Shop will be used. The treatment also takes a more personal approach by greeting the recipient by name and including a raw donation link instead of a button and graphic.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in a valid 1.3% increase in donations, with six coming from the treatment and none coming from the control. Although the treatment produced fewer clicks, it did produce a far better donor conversion rate, which is more valuable. This proves that the value proposition-heavy email was more valuable. Those who clicked the treatment email were more aware of the impact The Policy Shop has and why it needs support to continue to have such an impact. They had greater clarity on why The Policy Shop is so important. This clarity and motivation gained from the value proposition in the treatment could not be gained from the control. The experiment stresses the importance of value proposition copy and proves that value proposition should be a strong focus in emails going forward.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy statement below the submit button on content offer landing page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement below the submit button for landing page visitors will achieve greater email conversion because it will decrease anxiety about data security and increase the appeal of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2022 - 09/30/2022
For a faith-based media organization, we wanted to know how including a privacy statement below the form submission button would affect email acquisition rates. We know that privacy continues to be a growing priority for users online and we wanted to test whether or not anticipating any reluctance to submit payment information online at the point of requesting a devotional eBook would reduce anxiety and encourage more people to complete the form.
The privacy statement below the button on the treatment reads: “*We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to send you your eBook. “
~50% of traffic received the control and ~50% of traffic received the treatment.  We set this up in Unbounce and, unfortunately, didn’t reset the conversions report so the samples look skewed in the report below as “control” reflects all samples since the launch of the content offer not just the ones during the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on the results from this Unbounce experiment, it's clear that including a privacy statement below the form submission button had a major impact on email acquisition rates.
This shows that anticipating privacy concerns in our customers and adapting our strategies to address those concerns can have a substantive impact on our overall marketing efforts. In the future, it will be valuable to continue testing our privacy statements to determine what language is most effective in communicating security, understanding that our customers expect transparency and taking responsible steps to secure their personal data. We will be testing this approach on donation pages as well.
Unfortunately, we were not able to validate a similar increase in downstream donor conversion from this test.

experiments.title: How the use of story in an email appeal impacts click thru rate and donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that continuing the story on a donation page  for email recipients will achieve a higher click thru rate and donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2023
During Giving Tuesday the Navigators wanted to use a story for their email appeal. We wanted to see if we could get more people to click thru the email if we continued the story in the donation page as opposed to providing the entire story in the email. This would accomplish two things: reduce the length of the email (which we hypothesized would cause less people to click thru to give) and give people incentive to click thru so that they can land on the donation page, read the rest of the story and give. In turn, the treatment would increase click thru rate and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: We successfully increase click thru rate by 90% with a 100% level of confidence. However, when looking at donor conversion rate, we saw a valid 62% decrease with a level of confidence of 99%. So, while many more people were motivated to click thru, those were not the people who were motivated to give. These people could've just been interested in the story but had no intention to give a gift. It's recommended that this is retested to understand how to best use stories for the Navigators audience. It's recommended that the control is set as-is and the treatment would be split into two emails: one email that's pure cultivation with the testimonial/story (no ask), and a follow up email referencing the story and including a direct appeal. It could be that splitting up the experience in this way, captures all those who were interested in the story. It allows everyone to see the evidential and opportunity for them to give later.

experiments.title: How a $10 vs $15 ask for nondonors impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a $15 ask compare to a $10 ask for nondonors via email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2023 - 02/06/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Church Dallas. Through radio, television, books, and digital resources, PTV shares the truth of God’s Word in an effort to pierce a darkening culture.
For February 2023, Pathway to Victory highlighted one of their books “Choosing the Extraordinary Life.” Historically this offer has had a mediocre response to activating donors. Therefore in an effort to increase donor conversion, we decided to test a $10 vs $15 ask.
Several months ago we tested the PTV usual approach of a generic “generous gift of any amount” vs $10 and found these results https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4544
For this month, we decided to test a $10 vs $15 ask for nondonors to see if we could increase the entry point while still converting at the same level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
On the email level, we saw that clicks experienced a 20.2% increase with a 96% confidence level.
Digging into donations between the 2 variants we saw a 17.2% decrease in donations with a 58.6% confidence level.
For revenue, we saw a 12.8% decrease with a 65.9% level of confidence.
With the average gift for the $10 ask being $33.25 and the average gift for the $15 ask at $35.21.
What remained positive is the trend of not having gifts less than $5. When we tested this back in September with "generous gift" vs $10 we saw that the $10 version had fewer "low ball" donations as people met the goal of the amount presented or even exceeded.
For this test, we saw the same trends with
$10 ask gifts being
8 - $10 gifts2 - $15 gifts1 - $20 gift19 - $25 gifts5 - $50 gifts1 - $75 gift 4 - $100 gifts
For the $15 ask, we saw
1 - $7 gift2 - $10 gifts6 - $15 gifts5 - $20 gifts11 - $25 gifts1 - $30 gift1 - $40 gift2 - $50 gifts2 - $75 gifts1 - $100 gift1 - $250 gift
What is additionally interesting is that the gift array on the donation page is $25, $50, $100, and $250. Meaning that for a majority of these donations, the user typed in the different amount.
For gifts of $75 or over, the user receives a bundle of resources.
These results are based on a single email sent to PTV nondonors, we will continue testing amounts to find the sweet spot for PTV nondonors and their activation.

experiments.title: How communal language vs. listed value proposition impacted a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the value offered for engaged website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2022 - 01/01/2023
During calendar year end, we were running a direct ask as a Dear Reader beneath articles, blogs, and podcast pages site-wide. There was a housefile cultivation email called “8 Ways TGC Will Continue Serving You” that was sent on Dec. 13 and we were curious if including these 8 ways as a reminder of the value added to the readers would propel them to make a gift. The control copy used communal language that was conversational in style and the treatment listed out the 8 ways readers are served by TGC. We split traffic between the two variants and let the experiment run for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that listing out the various ways the readers were ministered to by the organization resulted in a 48% decrease in conversion rate with 96.9% confidence compared to the control language so the results are statistically valid, however we were just shy of recording 50 total transactions that we would typically target as a minimum for a test. Because valid decreases were observed across multiple channels and visitor segments after only running for one week during such a crucial time of year for fundraising, we decided to pull down the treatment copy that was diminishing results and chose to roll out the control to 100% of traffic during the final days of the year to take advantage of the higher performing copy.

experiments.title: How including an image of the non-profit leader impacts donations in email prospecting
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the non-profit leader will increase donations by building credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2023
We referenced the following experiment for a public policy client and utilized it for a faith-based client. Since the results of the past experience were affirmative, we wanted to test the same experiment for a different client. https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4973
In an effort to see a lift in donations in email prospecting for a faith-based client, we wanted to measure the effect of adding credibility and interest to the offer by means of utilizing an image of the non-profit leader.
The offer deployed a daily devotional direct appeal. The control version used the cover image of the daily devotional, while the treatment version utilized the image of the non-profit leader.
This test was set up in which ~50% of the audience would get the control version and ~50% of the audience would get the treatment version. However, once results were clear to email brokers they only deployed the winning version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 91% decrease in donations (with a 99.9% level of confidence) on the treatment creative, we are able to conclude with clarifying results that our experiment hypothesis for this particular client did not match the results of a similar experiment for a different client. We believe that adding an image of the non-profit leader will increase donations by building credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
The execution of the past experiment was quite similar to this one: We replaced the image of a book/offer premium with the image of the non-profit leader. However, the results were quite different.
It is of importance to note that for this particular client, all other channels tested out of utilizing the image of the leader’s image. We can conclude that the channel email prospecting is no different.

experiments.title: How opening a loop of curiosity via the subject line affects open rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a less direct subject line will increase open rates by creating an open loop of curiosity that recipients will want to close by opening the emails to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/14/2022
During a year-end campaign when we know potential donors will be receiving a high volume of emails soliciting donations, we wanted to know whether or not using less direct subject lines would improve open rates by opening a loop of curiosity that the recipient would want to close by opening the email.
Recipients of this campaign were segmented between previous donors and non-donors and roughly half of both segments were sent emails with subject lines that spoke directly to the contents of the email while the other half received emails that opened a loop of curiosity with less direct subject lines … but which (importantly) would still clearly relate to the subject matter of the email once the recipient opened. This test was run across three appeal emails in an effort to ensure the results were not isolated to a single send.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at the results of this experiment a number of interesting observations came to light:
The curiosity-building subject lines did indeed increase open rates by 2.6% (99.9% LoC)However, the curiosity-building subject lines also decreased click-through rates by -8.5% (97.4% LoC)
But because these sends were all segmented between previous donors and non-donors, drilling down into the data may help paint a more complete picture of our results, for instance:
Among non-donors:
Curiosity-building subject lines produced a 2.4% increase in open rates (100% LoC)But they also produced a 16.6% decrease in click-through rates  (100% LoC)
Among previous donors:
Curiosity-building subject lines produced a 4% increase in open rates (100% LoC)While also producing a 33.8% increase in click-through rates (99.9% LoC)
And so we see that not all recipients behave the same, further emphasizing the importance of tailoring communications to the audience that you are targeting, and doing away with the assumption that all audiences behave the same.
Non-donors may be less familiar with (or less bought into) the value American Cornerstone Institute provides—hence their status as non-donors—while previous donors, who better understand (or better align) with the value received from ACI were hooked by the curiosity-building subject line and continued their momentum through the appeal contained inside the email, ultimately clicking through to the donation page at a significantly higher rate.
Based on these results, we will want to send emails with direct subject lines to the non-donor segment while sending curiosity-building emails to the previous donor segments as the results from this experiment have clearly indicated each segment's preference.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition language to a direct appeal email impacted click through rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a value proposition-enhanced email for email recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2022 - 12/30/2022
As part of a high-urgency campaign for a public policy client, we emailed subscribers to their weekly newsletter, The Policy Shop, asking if they would make a donation in support of The Policy Shop. The email was short and sweet, including a cartoon, a thanks for being a part of The Policy Shop, and an ask for support.
We wondered how a more in-depth, value proposition-focused email would impact clicks (and donations). The treatment version highlights the problem(s) that The Policy Shop helps solve, provides background on The Policy Shop, provides evidentials on the impact of The Policy Shop, and tells the recipients how their gift in support of The Policy Shop will be used. The treatment also takes a more personal approach by greeting the recipient by name and including a raw donation link instead of a button and graphic.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in a valid 20.5% decrease in click rate. The brief email with a cartoon proved to be more click-friendly than the longer email with no graphic. Some users may have even clicked the cartoon to see what it led to before even reading the email and noticing that its purpose was to ask for support. What is far more telling with this experiment, however, is that the treatment produced 6 donations compared to none from the control. So although the treatment produced fewer clicks, it did produce a far better donor conversion rate, which is more valuable. This proves that the value proposition-heavy email was more valuable. Those who clicked the treatment email were more aware of the impact The Policy Shop has and why it needs support to continue to have such an impact. They had greater clarity on why The Policy Shop is so important. This clarity and motivation gained from the value proposition in the treatment could not be gained from the control. The experiment stresses the importance of value proposition copy and proves that value proposition should be a strong focus in emails going forward.
