Live Action was running a high-urgency campaign around Twitter banning their pro-life message and ads from it’s platform. The first email in the campaign was a petition to their housefile asking people to add their name to the list of pro-life advocates. They did a resend of that same email and wanted to test the subject line of the resend to see if they could get more people to open the email, read the contents in it, and click-through to the landing page. The control used a similar approach that we had worked well in a previous campaign, “Did you see Lila’s email?” The treatment used a personalization tactic in the subject line, “[FIRSTNAME], your name is missing from our Twitter petition”.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment increased opens by 4.2%. While the control subject line was engaging, the treatment directly told called the end reader out and told them the purpose of the email. Even though this subject line experiment only slightly impacted open rate, it also impact click-rate. The treatment also helped increase the amount of people clicking through to the landing page because it reinforced the value proposition of the email content.

experiments.title: How the email presentation impacted click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified version of the email increase the number of subscribers getting to the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2016 - 12/09/2016
As part of a monthly appeal, Live Action wanted to test out the standard email design that had been in use for the last several months. This email template included the standard Live Action branding and an image of the Live Action president Lila Rose. The hypothesis was that if we could make the email feel more genuine, we could engage a greater proportion of the audience in the appeal.
To test this hypothesis, we stripped out the majority of the design elements and also softened the copy so that it did not have as many of the popular direct mail tactics such as bolding significant portions of text and including an ask array in the email (i.e. “would you consider making a $25, $50, or $100 gift.”).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The simplified design increased the subscriber click through rate by 208%. This increased traffic resulted in a 50% lift to the number of donations made to to the treatment.
This experiment demonstrated the importance of facilitating a one-to-one relationship in mass-marketing emails. It is our belief that people give to people and when they feel marketed to, they are not as likely to engage with an organization. By making this email look more genuine, more people engaged in the message. As we plan for future iterations of testing, this is a concept that we want to continue to explore with further testing.

experiments.title: How removing an image from an email impacts clicks-through
experiments.researchQuestion: Would removing the picture from the email motivate more people to click-through to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2017 - 12/23/2016
During Live Action’s year-end campaign, they sent a series of emails asking people to support their mission and give a gift to their organization. One of the emails in the campaign was a story about a baby boy named Jaxon whose parents decided against abortion when they found out he had a disease. To reinforce the message and bring a more personal connection to the story they included a picture of baby Jaxon in the control email. We hypothesized that by removing the picture, we would be able to make the email feel more genuine and believable that a person, and not an organization, had sent this email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The email version without the picture of the baby had an increase in clicks by 54%. By removing the image in the email, we were able to make the email look and feel more authentic – as if the sender personally sent the message.
This reinforces our learnings from a previous test that people are more highly motivated to engage with us when they feel like they aren’t being mass-marketed to, but rather communicated with from a one-to-one basis.

experiments.title: How the tone of a subject line impacts open rate

rows.name: NEW VIDEO: Prenatal care nonexistent at Planned Parenthood

experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line would get a higher open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2017 - 02/01/2017
Live Action produces investigative videos where they go undercover inside abortion facilities. These investigative videos receive millions of views. Whenever they release a new investigative video, they send it out to their email house file first. Traditionally, the subject line hints at the release of the latest video by saying “NEW VIDEO: Prenatal care nonexistent at Planned Parenthood”. They wandered if the would be able to increase the number of people who opened the email if they took a more casual approach and didn’t mention that the email was a “new video” release. They developed a treatment with the subject line: “One option: abortion:”. They A/B tested the two different subject lines.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment email that didn’t mention the new video in the subject line had a 14% increase in opens. While it might seem strategic to mention the new video in the subject line since that is what the email is about, the tone of the control subject line may have come off too organization-centric causing less people to want to open it. When subject lines are more casual and believable that Lila has actually sent the email, people are more motivated to open them.

experiments.title: How clarity in the subject line impacts click-through

rows.name: Former managers: Planned Parenthood is not health care

experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line would get more people to click-through?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2017 - 02/27/2017
For the release of a new investigative video, Live Action sent an email to their house file and included a link for people to view it. In an effort to get more people to open and click through to watch the video, they tested two different subject lines. The control took a personal approach by including the recipients name in the subject line. The treatment was much more matter of fact and told people exactly what the email was about. They split their file and tested.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Their wasn’t a significant difference in open rate between the control and the treatment, but the treatment got a 20% increase in click-through. What we can learn from this is that:
Clarity trumps persuasion. While the control subject line was personalized and this can be a highly motivating tactic, it was also very unclear and may have caused some friction. The treatment told people exactly the topic of the email.
The treatment subject line helped reinforce the email value proposition and strengthened the message, resulting in more people clicking-through from the treatment.

experiments.title: How making a subject line personal to the reader impacts open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line would motivate more people to open the email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2017 - 03/13/2017
Some of the content Live Action sends in their emails is story-based and about a particular person who they name and identify in the copy. For Live Action’s recent fundraising email, it was about a person named Emma. Normally they use the name of the person that the email is about in their subject line to give it a more personal approach. For this email, they wanted to test the motivation of the reader and see which subject line would get more opens and ultimately more clicks to their landing page. The two subject lines they tested were “Emma chose life” and “You saved a life”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment subject line, “You saved a life” had a 23.6% increase in opens. Not only did this subject line significantly increase the open rate, it also got a 33% increase in clicks. What we can learn from this experiment is:
1. By saying “You” saved a life, it made the subject line personal to the reader. It also empowered them and caused intrigue into “how” they saved a life resulting in more people wanting to open the email and read it.
2. The treatment subject line reinforced the call-to-action for people to help save more lives. This resulted in more people clicking through to the donation page to give.

experiments.title: How the type of sender (individual vs. organizations) impacts open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending an email from an individual while speaking to individual responsibility in the subject line will get more opens than sending from the organization and talking about group responsibility?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/10/2014 - 09/12/2014
As part of a multivariate test to improve the overall results of their monthly email appeal, the Federation for American Immigration Reform ran a test on every aspect of their campaign. The goal was to find a way to lift each of the metrics of the donor pathway that could then be transferrable to future appeals.
The first aspect to be tested was the email “envelope” which includes both the sender and the subject line. The hypothesis was that we could increase the open rate by sending from an individual and addressing the responsibility of just the individual subscriber instead instead of sending from the organization and speaking generally, ie. “tax payers.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in a lift of 10% to the open rate. Future tests would be needed to identify which aspect of the treatment was most impactful (the sender or subject line) but this does demonstrate that the one-to-one communication is the most successful at increasing engagement.

experiments.title: How assumption of desire for an offer affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will assuming the recipient wants an offer affect open rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2019
Hillsdale College was sending complimentary pocket Constitutions to their constituents and donors. They knew they had a list of people who had not received a free pocket Constitution, but they weren’t sure if the people wanted a pocket Constitution. They decided to test two different approaches in the subject line. The first approach asked a question—”Do you want a free pocket Constitution?” The second assumed that they did want it, and offered them to them: “Get your complimentary pocket Constitution”. They split the file and sent a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The subject line that assumed the recipient wanted the offer produced a 30% lift in open rate. There are two possible reasons for this. First, the question format allowed the recipient to decide whether or not to open the email before they even opened the email. If they didn't want it, many people may have just archived or deleted the email. Second, the two subject lines also used two different words to describe the offer: "free" and "complimentary". This may have had an impact, as "free" implies that the offer is widely available and of limited value, while "complimentary" implies that the offer is of some value but is given free of charge due to a relationship. This element should be specifically targeted to determine impact in a future test.

experiments.title: How the fear of loss of membership benefits increased donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adjusting the motivation of a message to focus on the “fear of loss” increase donor conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2019 - 02/12/2019
Within the 2019 Heritage Foundation Kickoff Campaign, most of the control messaging delivered was highly transaction — largely focusing upon driving members online to renew their membership for the year. On the final day of the campaign, we wanted to test adjusting motivation for the file that had not yet given by treating the email with what they would lose if they didn’t renew their membership benefits, as opposed to the control message (which was tailoring motivation towards “hope of gain”).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.2% level of confidence, we successfully validated this experiment for the treatment ("fear of loss" message) with a 21.7% lift in donor conversion rates.
Other metrics impacted are noted as follows (all values shown are relative to the control version's performance in the same performance metric category):
Email Open Rate: +2.0% (with a 87.2% LoC)Email Clickthrough Rate: -4.6% (with a 64.4% LoC)On-Page Conversion Rate: +8.5%Revenue/Visitor: +4.2%Average Gift: +6.0%
Further experimentation should be attempted, however these results are encouraging that "fear of loss" may be a better motivator for members to renew their membership, as opposed to the "hope of gain."
We theorize that the personal impact of the loss is potentially felt more than the collective gain that we together can create. The tangibility of the impact of renewing their gift is more poignant, and is felt more -- which appears to have driven the increase in motivation to go online and renew.
These messages were sent on the morning of the final day. They both included referencing that it was the final day (e.g. created a sense of urgency to take action today), and structurally they were the same (in terms of sender, subject line, template, etc).
We would advise using a "fear of loss" message at least on the final day of a campaign, but can it provide good lift earlier in the campaign without weakening the final day message, as well? Further experimentation is recommended on timing within the campaign timeline.

experiments.title: How we are able to use subject lines to learn an audience’s affinities
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using the scripture verse discussed in the email as the subject line increase email engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2019 - 01/20/2019
Buckner International was sending out their monthly cultivation email to their house file. In recent months, one of the primary discussion points related to their subscribers revolved around how we could increase their overall engagement. The open rate of the file had slipped in recent months.
In addition to evaluating technical solutions to the problem, we also wanted to see if we could identify topics that may reactivate people. Since many of these subscribers came from an offer related to scripture, we wanted to see if incorporating scripture earlier in the email (like the subject line) would increase their engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a 34.8% increase in the open rate of the email when using the scripture verse as the subject line. We are evaluating the success of this experiment solely on open rate since there were no links to the website or possibilities to give. This is a great reminder of the power of utilizing scripture in our communications as a way to better relate to our own audience.
We will test this further in March by running a multi-email experiment using a segment that will receive solicitations with scriptural references and comparing their giving to a segment that does not have any scripture in their communications.

experiments.title: How a more specific subject line affects opens, clicks, and conversions

rows.name: How are your year-end plans coming?
A new year-end fundraising webinar for you

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more specific subject line lead to greater email performance?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2018 - 10/24/2018
In this email experiment, the original subject line posed a question that would be completely normal to receive from a colleague at this time of year…”How are your year-end plans coming?” It felt personal and genuine, but we wondered if it wasn’t doing enough to prime recipients for the upcoming call-to-action. So for the treatment, we created an subject like that maintained the personal nature (using the word “you”) and increased the specificity and clarity around what the content of the email was ultimately leading towards. For the treatment, we said “A new year-end fundraising webinar for you”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As we've seen in the past, even when the subject line doesn't affect the open rate, it can still have a dramatic effect on downstream metrics. In this case, the open rate remained nearly identical – 36.14% vs 36.16%. But the treatment version increased clicks to the webinar registration page by 42.7% at a 99% level of confidence.
The treatment also increased webinar registrations by 18.8% and was approaching statistical validity at an 89% level of confidence. This shows us 2 important learnings:

1 - The subject line has a powerful effect on how recipients view and respond to the entire email, even if it makes no difference in the open rate.
2 - A subject line that makes a clear tie to the ultimate call-to-action has a greater chance of getting a click and a conversion.

experiments.title: How the sender influences email open rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending the email from an individual (Kristen Waggoner) have a positive impact on open rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/26/2018
Alliance Defending Freedom was sending a fundraising email as part of their March campaign. We had created a series of optimizations to test on the email, the first of which was who the email came from. Our goal for the whole email was to make it feel more personalized; like it was coming from an individual and not a faceless organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a 26% increase to the open rate by sending from an individual. All other aspects of the email envelope were the same (subject line and preview text) which means the email sent from an individual was able to garner much better engagement than the one sent from the organization. It is worth pointing out that this may be the result of the sender being seen as a "new shiny object." If this tactic is used as the new control moving forward, it may begin to lose its impact over time.  This is something we will want to iteratively test over time.

experiments.title: How delaying the ask impacted click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will delaying the hard donation ask until the end of the email increase the email clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/26/2018
The standard Alliance Defending Freedom fundraising email will generally include a long story that is divided into multiple sections with donation asks made between each section. It was our hypothesis that we could actually drive both a greater volume and a more highly motivated audience if we delayed the hard donation ask until the end of the email. The idea was that if we allow the reader to consumer the full story prior to the ask being made, we could increase the volume and motivation of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this treatment, we removed the direct call-to-action that was found in both the pre-header of the email and the middle. We replaced the hard ask in the middle with a soft call to action and additional context for the story being told. These changes resulted in a 74% increase in the number of people going to the website  for those that had opened the email.

The result of this test illustrates the need to fully convey the background and case for support prior to making the a fundraising ask. We often worry about the increased friction that comes with longer copy and less calls to action but this experiment is a good example of the impact that comes from communicating the value proposition prior to an ask being made.

* As a note, this is part of a larger experiment where we also changed the sender which is why the two sample sizes are not the same.

73.6% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate

experiments.title: How a storytelling messaging approach impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a storytelling approach to fundraising have a significant impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2018 - 03/26/2018
In an effort to optimize their monthly appeal process, Alliance Defending Freedom decided to test the standard format of their email appeals. Historically, these appeals had often been sent from the organization instead of an individual person and the appeals would have multiple parts that were broken up with donation asks in between.
We wanted to see if telling a cohesive story could help improve the overall results. This meant moving the donation asks to the end, once the full story had been told and also sending the email from an individual since the “organization” sender could not convincingly add the personal context necessary convey the story. To test this hypothesis, we split the email file in two and sent the two email versions as an experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The storytelling approach to communicating the appeal resulted a 47.7% increase to donor conversion. Not only that, when we analyzed the full funnel, we found that this approached improved every significant metric in the donation funnel.
25.6% increase to opens
73.6% increase to clicks
and a 101.5% increase in average gift
A key concept to take away from this experiment is that people give to people. The recipients responded better when the email was sent by an individual and focused on communicating a cohesive story instead of asking for something at every step along the way.

				gift).

0% increase in traffic
				× 47.7% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a radical redesign to an email appeal affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would an email that looked, felt, and sounded more personal increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2018 - 01/23/2018
Traditionally, Focus on the Family sends monthly email appeals that are branded and templates for their organization. They also feature an image or a video with a button for a call-to-action and include a signature of the email sender. They hypothesized that they might be able to increase engagement and conversion of their monthly appeal if they made the email look, feel and sound as if the sender personally sent the appeal himself. To do this, they developed a treatment that stripped the email of it’s branded template to make it look like a plain text email and also wrote the copy in a way that was more conversational. They split their email file in half and tested each version against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment increased donor conversion by 77.5%. This significant increase in conversion can be attributed to the radical approach in testing.
By stripping the email of the organization-centric template and features and changing the tone of the message to be more conversational, we were able to make the email feel like a traditional email sent personally by the sender. People want to feel like they are being personally engaged and that the appeal is only written to them from Jim Daly. People give to people, not mass marketing machines. This test reinforces that idea.
Moving forward, we will test specific elements of this email to gain an even deeper understanding of what motivates people to give.

				× 77.5% increase in conversion rate

experiments.title: How the type of subject line affects open rate in an appeal

rows.name: Will you help me share some love?

experiments.researchQuestion: Does the type of subject in an email affect open rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/24/2018
NBCF was sending out an email appeal to their housefile for their Valentine’s Day Hope Kit campaign. Their goal for the experiment was to get more eyes on the email and more opens. The hypothesis was that the subject line “720 women” would be more compelling than asking the reader a question or to do something, for example, “Will you help me do this?”. The email itself remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment headline produced a 19% increase in open rate of the email appeal. This is possibly due to the workload involved in the first subject line, which requires "work" on the reader's behalf and immediately adds friction to the opening process, and partially due to the compelling nature of the treatment, "720 women", which refers to the 720 women diagnosed with breast cancer every day.

experiments.title: How using a non-traditional sender and conversational subject line impacts open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase the amount of people who open the emails by using a conversational subject line and different sender?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2018
Care Net created a special giving campaign to acquire more monthly recurring donors. This campaign was similar to a money bomb where they sent multiple emails over a short period of time. Recently, the majority of their emails came from their President, Roland C. Warren. They hypothesized that using a conversational subject line with a different sender might break through the clutter of nondonor’s email inbox and motivate them to want to open it. They tested two different senders and a different subject line against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment subject line and sender increased opens by 53%. By sending the treatment email from a name that different from the sender they have been used to seeing along with a conversational and intriguing subject line, people were more interested in opening and reading what the email was all about. While the increase in opens doesn't directly tell us if more people ultimately converted and gave a gift, it is a critical point in the conversion funnel to get more people to open and read the email and potentially convert. For future campaigns, we should consider using a non-traditional sender with a conversational style subject line.

experiments.title: How a value-focused headline affects open rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a value-focused headline affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2017 - 11/13/2017
Heartlight Ministries was offering their Developing Rules and Consequences eBook to the housefile, and wanted to test the headline of the email to increase open rates. The original subject line talked more about the resource itself, while the treatment mentioned the topic of the offer instead of the “free resource.” The emails were identical otherwise.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment headline increased open rate by 29%. This tells us that their file is more motivated by the topical value of the email rather than a random free resource that we want to give them. The treatment subject line also has a little bit of mystery to prompt the reader to click and open the email.

experiments.title: How a relevant subject line not only impacts opens, but clicks and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line would get more people to open the email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2017 - 06/20/2017
Harvest Ministries had a high-urgency fundraising campaign leading up to a large-scale evangelism event, Harvest America. They sent a series of emails during this campaign to acquire donations and revenue. In an effort to increase the number of people who opened their emails and ultimately give, they tested a subject line of an email as they got closer to the campaign’s final day. The control used the subject line, “One Thing Remains” and the treatment tested something that was more timely, “This Sunday…”
experiments.keyLearnings: While both subject lines were relatively vague, the treatment subject line increased opens by 5.8%. The treatment was more relevant to the campaign and created a sense of urgency. Because we were able to get almost 6% more people to open the email, the relevancy of the subject line with the email copy also lead to a 9% increase in clicks and ultimately 28% more donations!

experiments.title: How a personalized email affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: What motivates donors to give a donation?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2016 - 08/05/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending their monthly fundraising email for the month of August and wanted to test the motivation behind people’s donations. All three email variations that were sent and tested offered a monthly premium, a book by Greg Laurie call, Tell Someone. Greg Laurie was the sender of all variations.
The control email was written and laid out how Harvest traditionally would send their monthly email. They developed two other treatments to test against the control. For the first treatment, they tested using urgency as a motivating factor to give a donation. The layout and design of the email was similar to the control, but the call-to-action had copy that created a sense of urgency and immediacy (“Time is running out, get this resource now before it’s too late.”).  For the second treatment, they tested a more personal approach to the email. They stripped down the traditional marketing email template to make it look like it was an Outlook email personally from Greg and changed the tone of the copy and email to be more casual and conversational as if Greg Laurie had sat down to personally write the donor this appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the urgency focused email didn’t quite validate, both treatments increased donations from the control. The urgency email had an increase in donations by 36.3% and the personalized email treatment had an increase in donations by 116%. The personalized email results are 100% valid. What this tells us is that people are more motivated to give if they know and feel that Greg is personally asking them. It wasn’t just the copy that contributed to this lift. Having the email not look like a traditional email, people didn’t feel like Greg was marketing to them. People want to feel like they are being personally engaged and that the appeal is only written to them from Greg. Moving forward it will be important to send emails that are more high-touch like this winning treatment and look and feel like a “normal” email a person would send.

									75.4% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How sending a more personal sounding subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line would motivate the most people to open?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2016 - 08/05/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending their monthly fundraising email for August and wanted to test people’s motivation to open, click and donate. They developed two treatments to test against the control. Treatment one used urgency to try and motivate someone to open it. Treatment two used a more personal and casual approach.
experiments.keyLearnings: Both treatments saw an increase in open rate compared to the control. The urgency treatment had an increase in opens by 7.2% and the personal treatment had a 25.2% increase in open rate. When we create a sense of urgency in our subject lines, people are more motivated to open them. More importantly, when a subject line becomes personal or sounds like the sender is personally emailing the recipient, people are even more motivated to open it. The email starts to feel less like a marketing appeal and more like an email you would receive from someone who knows you. It is important to talk to donors and prospective donors like they are people – even as early on in the communication process as the subject line.

experiments.title: How a raw link in an email affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: What kind of link would acquire more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2016 - 09/19/2016
Harvest Ministries has been learning a lot about their donors through their monthly appeal testing. After a series of different tests they have learned that their donors respond best when the appeals have a personal and more casual tone to them. There are a lot of different variables that attribute to an email looking, feeling and sounding more personal and casual, so they wanted to test the specific variables within the email to gain an even deeper understanding of their donors. Keeping all other variables consistent between the control and the treatment email, they first tested the link in the email. The control had their standard link with a group of text hyperlinked and the treatment had a raw link that actually showed the URL a person would be sent to.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The raw link not only had a 12.4% increase in click-throughs, but a 77.6% increase in donations! The raw link helps the email to look and feel more personal by making it look as if the writer of the email had just pasted the link into an email. This method is much more believable and reinforces the personal approach. If this email was, in fact, an email sent from one person to another, a sender would most likely put a raw link in the copy rather than highlighting specific text and hyperlinking it – as validated in this test, we should take that same approach with these personalized appeal emails.
The raw link also brought clarity to where the link would take the end user. This reduces any anxiety a person might have in clicking the link because the URL specifically tells a person where they are being sent.

experiments.title: How “human” fundraising subject lines affect clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will “humanized copy” increase open rate and increase motivation to click?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2016 - 04/19/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending their mid-month email, which was a highly transactional, premium-focused offer. The offer featured a book that was relevant to Mother’s Day, which was coming up in just a few weeks. The treatment subject line was appropriately transactional: “Order Today to Receive for Mother’s Day”. We had been running some experiments with “humanized” copy — that is, copy that a normal person would write, rather than copy that is explicitly “marketing”. We hypothesized that a more “human” subject line would increase open rate, but we also wanted to measure the effect on clickthrough rate as well, since the two do not operate independently.
At the same time as the subject line test, we were running an email copy and design test as well. The control (which was matched to the control subject line) incentivized the click, a strategy that has shown to attract more clicks, but often less motivated traffic. The treatment made a more direct ask, which often results in fewer clicks but more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment subject line actually delivered a statistically valid 7.5% increase in open rate. Which is good…if the experiment ended there. But the ultimate goal was gifts, which relies on a click. So we needed to measure the entire funnel.
Once we looked past the subject line, we saw that the treatment email (which made the direct ask) produced a 59% decrease in clickthrough rate, which resulted in a 43% drop in conversion. So, the increase in open rate didn’t really matter that much. It highlights an important learning: open rate is only the beginning of the relationship. Getting 7.5% more people to open the email is a win, but not if it doesn’t pay off when we look at our ultimate metric.
This shows us that “human” subject lines can work, but they may affect reader motivation differently. The control subject line, though it had fewer opens, resulted in many more people showing interest in the offer — most likely due to a combination of the urgency it produced and the design of the control email.

				× 0% increase in conversion rate

experiments.title: How personal subject line tone affects fundraising email open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more personal subject line increase open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/02/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending a mid-month update on their fundraising appeal for the month. The offer was a book by Joni Earackson Tada, and the control subject line was all about the offer. We’ve seen that when an offer is obviously marketing, our prospects become resistant to it and don’t open the email. So we tested an alternate subject line that sounded like one a friend might actually write: “An idea for you”.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment lifted open rate by 8%. There is a possibility that these openers might be less motivated, since the control subject line was clearly an offer, so we must make sure to measure all the follow-on metrics to ensure that we aren’t leaving conversions on the table.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency in email copy affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increased urgency increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/02/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending a mid-month update on their fundraising appeal for the month. The offer was a book by Joni Earackson Tada. The control email highly incentivized the click by asking the reader to “find out why” the sender (Greg Laurie) admires Joni, the author of the book. While this might drive a lot of clicks, we’ve seen this approach drive much lower conversion rates, as those who click through are not as motivated to accept the offer.
Our first experiment showed that increased clarity, in this case, decreased clickthrough rate. We devised a new treatment that leaned into the transactional nature of this email and maximized urgency to get their own copy of the book before Mother’s Day.
We split the file and ran a test to see which email won.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 28.8% decrease in clickthrough rate, which showed that the “tease” copy in the control still provided a greater incentive to click. However, the treatment email produced a 32.6% higher conversion rate on the page, which made revenue almost even between the two treatments. This prompts further testing to find an email that incentivizes the click while providing motivation to complete the donation.

experiments.title: How personal tone in a subject line affects email open rate

rows.name: "I want to share a personal story..."

experiments.researchQuestion: How would a personal tone subject line affect open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2016 - 05/08/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending a monthly fundraising appeal. The offer was the book Coming Home by James MacDonald. The offer book connected with the message of the email – prodigal children. In the email, Pastor Greg told of his own prodigal son, Jonathan. The control email encouraged the reader to open the email with a subject line “My son Jonathan”. We wanted to see if we could increase opens by making the subject line even more personal. We know that readers want to feel personally engaged with organizations. If we could make the subject line feel and sound like Greg was personally emailing them to tell them something, we might be able to increase open rate.
The treatment subject line was “I want to share a personal story with you”.
We split the file and ran a test to see which email won.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we hypothesized that the treatment was more personal than the control and might help increase opens, the treatment produced a 22.2% decrease in open rate. The treatment was personal, but in hindsight, the control may have been even more personal because Greg mentions his son’s name in the subject line. Readers were more interested in what Greg had to say or tell them about his son Jonathan than some self-proclaimed “…personal story” he had to share. The treatment didn’t provide enough clarity with what the personal story was about and fewer people opened as a result.

experiments.keyLearnings: The subtle changes seen when emphasizing the cost over value, both in the headline and button copy, reduced the conversion rate by 9.6%.

Harvest Ministries was planning to send fundraising emails on Giving Tuesday. They tested a new subject line, but also new email copy that took a more direct approach to see if increased clarity improved clickthrough rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that our email prospects respond to the value proposition with the most clarity, rather than the most persuasive value proposition. When we can be direct with our prospects, we have a better chance of them taking the intended action.

experiments.title: How adding urgency to a repositioned call to action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will highlighting the urgency of a deadline increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2015 - 12/09/2015
Harvest Ministries prepared an appeal for their year end campaign that offered a book, “The Biggest Story”, as a premium. They had prepared an email that told a personal story, made a financial ask with a call-to-action button, and then followed up with some information about the premium. On the landing page, there was a callout box that indicated that time was running out to order the premium and receive it before Christmas.
Their team formed a hypothesis that if they moved this callout earlier in the donation funnel, that they would incentivize more readers of the email to click through to the page. They developed a treatment that added urgent copy that emphasized the impending deadline, and moved the call-to-action button after this copy.  They then ran an A/B split test to see the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This tells us that highlighting urgency on the landing page isn’t enough — we must move it earlier in the decision process if we want to motivate and incentivize the maximum amount of prospects. Because email performance rates decline naturally at each step (from delivery to open rate to clickthrough rate), we can reach more prospects with this message as we move it further down the inverted funnel. However, that doesn’t mean that we should move it to the subject line — when improperly placed, urgency can deceive and disincentivize prospects.

This also gives a valuable lesson about call-to-action placement. In the first email, the button was placed before the premium, which meant that many prospects made the decision before they were fully informed and incentivized. By moving the button to the end of the email (and strengthening the incentive that comes before it), Harvest was able to nearly double clickthrough rate.

experiments.title: How a contextual image affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a picture of C.S. Lewis increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2017 - 03/28/2017
Hillsdale College was promoting their course on C.S. Lewis, when they noticed that there might be an opportunity to optimize the email acquisition page.
The course enrollment page had the headline “Learn timeless principles from one of the world’s greatest authors”, and the reader had to go to the next line to find out who that was. C.S. Lewis’ name was only mentioned one on the page, and many readers who skimmed the content might not know who “one of the world’s greatest authors” referred to.
They hypothesized that putting a picture of C.S. Lewis on the page would help the reader connect the dots between the headline and the subject of the course, even if that person didn’t know who C.S. Lewis was at first glance.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the picture increased conversion by 10%—a nominal, but important increase. This prompted a new series of tests to see whether email acquisition could be further improved by creating more context for the course and third-party validation.

experiments.title: How inviting an email recipient to participate affects email open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a subject line that invites the recipient to participate in an action increase email open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2017 - 12/13/2017
Hillsdale College recently began their calendar year end campaign. This year they decided to focus more specifically on their independence and how it enables them to fulfill their mission–without accepting EVEN ONE PENNY of government funding! One of their appeal emails focused on their presence in Washington D.C. through the Kirby Center. Before sending, they wondered: Will recipients be more motivated to open an email that invites them to participate in an action (in this case, helping teach policymakers about the constitution). They created an A/B subject line test and monitored the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale College found that the treatment version actually decreased email open rate by about 5%. Though the relative impact of this test was small, it provides a valuable learning for future email subject line creation. Though the invitation to ‘Help teach policymakers the Constitution’ feels like a noble thing to ask recipients, it’s clear that it halted some in their inbox. It’s likely that the use of the word  ‘help’ struck some as a plea. This word makes people feel like the impetus is on them to do something. This can produce unnecessary anxiety in the mind of the recipient before they even have a chance to read the email.
This is an important thing to remember for any organization that is running a calendar year end campaign, and it will continue to inform Hillsdale College’s approach to email communication.

experiments.title: How increasing the relevancy of a subject line impacts email open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the relevancy of a subject line increase email open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2017 - 12/18/2017
Hillsdale College recently conducted a calendar year end campaign that focused on their unique independence from government funding. During the month of December, they sent appeals that focused on different initiatives they are advancing to secure education and restore constitutional liberty in America. In their fourth email appeal, they highlighted Imprimis–their popular monthly speech digest that has a wider readership than the Wall Street Journal. Before sending this appeal, they wondered: Will increasing the relevancy of the subject line increase email open rate? They created a treatment version with the words ‘Fake News’ and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Hillsdale College found that the treatment version did increase email open rate by nearly 9%. It’s clear that the use of the term ‘Fake News’ attracted more attention, and therefore, increased motivation of the email recipients. This is important to remember when crafting subject lines during anytime of the year, but especially during a calendar year end campaign. Email recipients are flooded with emails during the last month of the year, and it’s easy to get lost in the shuffle. You can increase your email open rates by using terms that recipients immediately recognize and have an interest in, as long as they are directly related to the topic you are discussing in the email copy.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2016 - 10/13/2016
National Breast Cancer Foundation send out a monthly newsletter to keep donors and supporters informed and engaged. They had traditionally used a functional subject line that clearly stated what the communication was—in this case, “October 2016 Newsletter | Helping Women Now”.
But they realized that the subject line is the initial point of interaction with their readers—and if they didn’t make it “open-worthy”, they might be causing some possible readers to delete it outright.
So they drafted a second subject line to give more insight into the communications and provide a greater incentive to open: “It’s A Special Month…”.
They split their email file to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new subject line produced a 10.4% increase in opens, which meant that they were able to engage a larger segment of their email file. This experiment also gave insight to future subject line testing by showing that increasing the force of the incentive to open the email produced a tangible increase in engagement.

experiments.title: How a subject line’s perceived impact on the subscriber influences click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will produce the greatest email engagement (opens and clicks)?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2017 - 03/17/2017
In an effort to educate their subscriber base on the public’s sentiment toward the state’s current budget crisis, the Illinois Policy Institute sent an email with the results of an independent 3rd party poll. The results of the poll aligned with the message IPI had been promoting since the current session began. It also supported the budget plan the the organization had developed and was working with state legislators to pass.
The goal of the email was to educate and engage the subscriber file so we decided to test out two subject lines that highlighted the contents of the message: “tax hikes” and “overspending.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
After sending to half of the email file, we found that “overspending” decreased the overall click through rate by 23%. It also negatively impacted the open rate by 5%. It is our hypothesis that this behavior is due to how the words are perceived by the subscriber. A “tax hike” is something that would impact them directly while “overspending” is something that they may not be in favor of but does not appear have any direct impact on them.
This serves as an important reminder that all aspects of the messaging should focus on how the topic will impact the recipient (when possible). We need to address not only why a message is important but why it is important to the reader.

experiments.keyLearnings: It turns out that the lowercase subject line continue to provide the greatest open rate for the file. In this situation, the proper case subject line lowered open rate by 15%. It is recommended to continue to utilize the lowercase subject lines moving forward.

rows.name: You still have time to double your...

experiments.researchQuestion: Which email would get more people to open it?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2017 - 12/19/2017
The emails for Focus on the Family’s year-end giving campaign are image heavy with a video on them to push people to the donation page. We hypothesized that a personal email, with a more conversational tone from the subject line to the body copy would increase conversion. The first goal was to get more people to open the emails in an effort to get more people to click and convert. We A/B split the email file and tested it.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate this experiment based off of donations, which is the ultimate goal, we were able to validate the experiment on opens. The treatment subject line took a more conversational approach that didn’t give any reference to giving or donating by saying, “Nothing is impossible for God”. This approach increased opens by 77%! Knowing that we can significantly increase the amount of people who see and read the email with this kind of approach, we recommend testing this approach again and doing a 50/50 split of the email file so that we have a greater chance at validating it off of donations to see what the impact of this approach really has on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How subject line copy that “teases” affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line results in a higher open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/17/2015 - 11/20/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a free online course studying the Gospel of John. They were promoting it by renting email lists, and wanted to determine which subject lines encouraged more recipients to open the email. They developed two subject lines based on hypotheses about the prospect. The first was very direct: “Study the Gospel of John with DTS”. The second teased the offer, and expanded from the acronym: “A special offer from Dallas Theological Seminary”.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that prospects are more likely to open an email when we tease the contents. While clarity has proven to trump persuasion over and over again, the subject line is a different element. It only has one job — to win the open. We can’t get people to click if they don’t open. So this suggests that teasing recipients of this email might increase open rate, which is the metric that affects every other following metric.

Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their newest online course on Genesis to their house file. Many of the people in their house file had already taken one of their first two courses.
Previous testing had shown that their prospects responded well to very personal invitations. However, they wanted to try a more graphical approach that presented the new course in a more dramatic way and gave the reader more to click on.
They created a treatment that inserted an image from the course into the copy (linked to the registration page) and put an image of Dr. Mark Bailey, the sender, next to his signature.
They split the file and sent an A/B email test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that when we are introducing a new course to our donors, we must be somewhat visual about it. Though previous testing had shown the power of a personal invitation, imagery can help attract attention and win more clicks and registrations.

experiments.title: How subject line personalization affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does subject line personalization affect open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2016 - 06/15/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their annual Christmas devotional series to their house email file. In the past, they had used a very straightforward subject line to promote the offer: Devotionals for the Christmas Season. But their new marketing platform allowed them to personalize the subject line with the recipient’s firstname, so they put it to a test.
They sent 10% of their house file each treatment and collected data to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the personalized subject line produced a 22.5% lift in open rate, which showed that including the recipient’s first name in the subject line increases open rate.

But the personalization wasn’t the only factor—the treatment subject line also obscured the actual payoff of the email by not disclosing the offer. This appeared to heighten interest in what the “reveal” would be, intensified by the personalization factor.

The combination of these two factors led to a significant increase in open rate, which helped inform future subject line copy. However, the “trick” of personalization also may come with a significant diminishing return if overused.

CaringBridge was running a fundraising campaign around Mother’s Day. Their email appeals typically came from Sona Mehring, their founder.

During the month of December, World Concern sent a series of emails for their calendar year-end fundraising campaign. experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment subject line, “I want to thank YOU” had an increase in opens by 19%. experiments.title: How subject line affects name acquisition conversion rate

rows.name: Get Your Free Pocket Constitution Now
Free Pocket Constitution, courtesy of Hillsdale College

experiments.researchQuestion: Do the treatment subject lines show a significant improvement in name acquisition conversion over the control subject line, not only for opens, but also for overall conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/05/2016 - 10/06/2016
Hillsdale College sent prospecting emails to a Townhall Media email file to generate signups for its free Pocket Constitution offer. Our hypothesis was that subject line could effect open rate, but that by clarifying the value proposition, the subject line could also drive a more motivated and qualified reader to the landing page, resulting in higher conversion rates.
The file was randomized into four segments, with each segment receiving the control or one of the three treatment versions. The subject line was the only variable changed between each test. All other email elements and landing pages were identical.
The subject lines used were:
+Control: Keep this in your pocket
+Treatment#1: Keep THIS in Your Pocket
+Treatment#2: Get Your Free Pocket Constitution Now
+Treatment#3: Free Pocket Constitution, courtesy of Hillsdale College
In this case, Samples count is the number of emails sent, and emails acquired is the total number of people who completed the signup process on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: Sometimes more ambiguous subject lines can tease a reader into opening an email. In this case, explicitly stating the value proposition at the front of the subject line drove higher clicks and higher conversions. This is the case when the value proposition is very direct and simple to understand (“Free Pocket Constitution”). People who opened  Treatments #2 and #3 are more qualified than those of the more ambiguous subject lines of Control and Treatment#1. Mentioning “Hillsdale College” serves to evoke credibility from a reputable and established institution that is well known among the target audience.

experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will produce the most visitors to the page?

When they were announcing their new online course, Hillsdale College wanted to offer the free course to their existing email file.  The majority of their email file had previously taken an online course so they wanted to frame this new offering around their previous experience.  As a way to increase the number of participants, we ran a test of two subject lines to see which would increase the number of people visiting the website.
The control in this test was letting the email list know that they could take another online course. The subject line read, “Your next course is ready”, which was the best performing subject line from past experiments. The treatment added specificity to the copy based upon the hypothesis that increased clarity would improve results. That subject line read, “A new course for you: American Heritage”.
experiments.keyLearnings: The increased clarity ended up dropping the unique number of website visitors by 15.1%. The increased clarity of the subject line reduced the number of constituents that opened the email.  While those that opened the email were more likely to visit the website, this increase in open to click rate was not enough to overcome the overall decrease in open rate.

experiments.title: How adding an image of the premium in an appeal email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the image of the premium to the appeal email for the housefile will increase the click-through rate because the cover image is unique and would encourage users to click through.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2025
Time of Grace offers a new premium each month. They send out appeal emails for this premium. The cover for their October premium was particularly distinctive and stood out to the audience.
Therefore, we wanted to test including the image in the email. We wanted to see how that would affect click-through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that adding the image of the premium to the appeal email did not achieve the desired increase in click-through rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 25.5% decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a 91.2% statistical level of confidence. While these results are not considered valid, they are approaching validity, indicating that further testing would be necessary to confirm the effect.
In terms of donations, however, we did see an increase in the treatment, but not with a 95% level of confidence.
Control - 11 Donations
Treatment - 17 Donations
LOC = 74.9%
Future studies should investigate how images in emails influence user behavior. Further testing can reveal whether certain images or situations are more effective. Keep analyzing results to improve email marketing strategies.

experiments.title: Does the fact that 100% of a donors gift goes to mission affect conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding transparency about where the donor’s gift is allocated will increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by clearly showing how their generosity directly supports missionaries and Kingdom work.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/03/2024
For Giving Tuesday, IMB wanted to test how adding transparency about the allocation of the donor’s gift would affect the donation conversion rate on both of their Giving Tuesday appeals.
We kept the control and treatment audiences consistent throughout both emails. The note of the allocation was added to the treatment emails as well as the donation page for those who received the treatment email.
They believed it would increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by showing how their gift directly supports missionary work.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding transparency about where the donor's gift is allocated can have a significant impact on the conversion rate for donations. In this case, the treatment group saw a 43.9% increase in donations compared to the control group, indicating that donors may be more inclined to give when they have a clear understanding of how their gift will be used.

Additionally, we calculated the impact of click-through rates in the emails and only saw a 1.1% increase with a 28.6% LoC. This means the addition of the transparency note did not have an effect on click-through rate, but helped increase giving once people got to the donation page.

To apply this learning in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of transparency on donation conversion rates. By conducting additional experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different variations of allocation transparency, we can further validate the initial findings and determine the most effective strategies for communicating allocation to donors.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that transparency in donation appeals can build trust and confidence in the organization, ultimately leading to increased support for the mission.

experiments.title: How removing checkboxes from a declaration affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the checkboxes for declaration signers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because we are removing friction from the form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2025 - 09/04/2025
This experiment tested the hypothesis that removing the “vote of confidence” checkboxes from the Vote of Confidence form would reduce friction and increase email acquisition. The checkboxes were originally included to allow signers to show public support, but they added an extra action to the form. We believed that eliminating these checkboxes while keeping their descriptions as a bulleted list, would create a smoother experience that encourages more people to complete the form and provide their email addresses.
We conducted a split URL test, sending half of the traffic to the original form (with checkboxes) and half to a new version of the form (with the checklist shown as a bulleted list). This setup ensured completely isolated experiences, minimizing cross-contamination between variants. Aside from the removal of the checkboxes, all other design, copy, and flow elements remained consistent. We tracked email acquisition as the primary success metric, while also monitoring revenue, and transaction rates as secondary indicators.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The removal of the checkboxes resulted in a -3.7% relative decrease in email acquisition, with an 88.6% level of confidence, indicating a directional but not statistically significant effect.

Contrary to our hypothesis, the 30-day split URL landing page test showed a small decrease in email acquisition when the checkboxes were removed. While the simplified form reduced friction, it may have also reduced the sense of commitment or engagement that the checkboxes provided, slightly lowering sign-up intent. This suggests that even seemingly minor elements can play a meaningful psychological role, and that removing them does not always result in higher conversions.

This suggests that while the checkboxes introduced a small amount of friction, they may also have served as a commitment or credibility cue, signaling seriousness or social proof to users. Removing them may have subtly reduced users’ sense of engagement or trust in completing the form, which could explain the dip in sign-ups.

For future tests, it may be valuable to explore alternative low-friction commitment indicators to preserve the psychological benefit of commitment signals while reducing perceived effort. Testing placement or wording changes rather than complete removal could also help identify the balance between friction and motivation.

experiments.title: How a shorter and more conversational housefile email impacted donations and email acquisitions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a shorter and more conversational emaill for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in donations because it will improve engagement and enhance clarity.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/26/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. Hoover recirculates their content offers by sending out an email to their Donors and Nondonors every Thursday offering a free eBook or course. On this Thursday, Hoover sent out a NEW offer, “Federal Entitlement Programs”. This specific email test was to their donor audience.
These were some key differences between the copy:
Conciseness – The treatment is shorter and more direct, removing the detailed breakdown of course modules.
Engagement – The treatment uses a more conversational tone with rhetorical questions (“Why does this matter?”) to draw the reader in.
Clarity & Readability – The treatment simplifies complex ideas, avoiding dense phrasing like “glacially slow economic growth”.
Urgency & Impact – The treatment highlights the real-world consequences of entitlement spending earlier and more clearly.
Call to Action (CTA) – The treatment places the course link in a more prominent, action-driven sentence.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
Shorter Emails Didn’t Increase Donations
The treatment email, while more concise and conversational, resulted in fewer donations (27 vs. 42) and fewer acquired emails (138 vs. 209) than the control.Next Step: Analyze why donations dropped—did the shorter format remove persuasive elements that resonated with donors?
Audience May Prefer More Detail
The control email provided a structured breakdown of the course, which may have been more compelling for Hoover’s donor audience.Next Step: Test a hybrid email that keeps the engaging tone of the treatment while reintroducing key persuasive elements from the control.
CTA Placement and Wording Could Be Optimized
The placement and clarity of the donation ask might have impacted conversions.Next Step: Experiment with stronger, more direct CTA wording and placement to drive more action.
Consider Multi-Touch Campaigns
A single email might not be enough to drive engagement and donations.Next Step: Explore follow-up emails, retargeting, or landing pages to reinforce the message and increase conversions.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and relevancy on the email copy on a recirculation email for donors impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and relevancy for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in donations because a direct, credible, and more streamlined approach will cause people to be more engaged.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/26/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. Hoover recirculates their content offers by sending out an email to their Donors and Nondonors every Thursday offering a free eBook or course. On this Thursday, Hoover sent out the offer, “Leaving Socialism”. This specific email test was to their donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment conducted by the Hoover Institution for their donor audience revolved around the importance of clarity and relevancy in communication. The treatment, which aimed to provide a more direct, credible, and streamlined approach, resulted in a significant 68.2% increase in donations for all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue focusing on clarity and relevancy in communication with the donor audience. This approach seems to have a positive impact on engagement and donation rates. Additionally, conducting more experiments with larger sample sizes could help in achieving a higher level of statistical confidence and providing more reliable results.

Overall, the key takeaway is that communicating directly and clearly with donors, providing relevant information, and streamlining the approach can lead to increased engagement and support. These learnings should be applied in future experiments to optimize communication strategies and drive donor participation.

experiments.title: How leading email appeal traffic to an aligned “holiday” donation page impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading email appeal traffic to an aligned “holiday appeal” donation page will increase donor conversion rate because it helps reinforce the 2-day match with additional value prop, while also tying the email into the larger December year-end goals.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, traffic from the email appeal is typically sent to a simplified form that is similar to the length of a general donation page – with match language added. We hypothesized that a longer, “holiday appeal” donation page that aligns with the overall campaign goals communicated in email and reinforces the 2-day match alongside the larger year-end goals will increase conversion rate
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that leading email appeal traffic to an aligned "holiday appeal" donation page can significantly increase donor conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 42.5% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the additional value proposition and alignment with the 2-day match and larger year-end goals had a positive impact on donor behavior.

However, the statistical level of confidence was 91.6%, which is just below the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for validity. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is needed for validity.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of leading email traffic to specialized donation pages tailored to specific campaigns or appeals. This experiment suggests that aligning the donation page with the messaging and goals of the email appeal can lead to increased donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How donor autonomy language on active donor impacts transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating donor autonomy language in our fundraising emails targeted at the active donor segment will lead to an increase in donations. By emphasizing the donor’s sense of choice and control, we expect to empower recipients, making them feel more confident and motivated to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted in partnership with WUNC, a North Carolina NPR station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The primary goal of this study was to explore the impact of donor autonomy language on behavior and engagement.
The experiment focused on a series of five emails sent to an active segment of WUNC’s donor base. Each email aimed to evaluate whether emphasizing donor autonomy—language that reinforces a donor’s sense of choice and control over their giving—would influence donation rates and overall responsiveness.
To measure the effect, we created two variations of the emails. The control group received messages without any autonomy-focused language, while the test group received emails that intentionally incorporated phrases designed to highlight donor choice and empowerment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment demonstrated that incorporating donor autonomy language did not lead to improved conversion rates among active donors. Despite both the control and treatment emails generating the same number of clicks (42 each), the control group consistently outperformed the treatment group in donation conversions, achieving a 0.44% conversion rate compared to the treatment’s 0.26% conversion rate—a 40.4% decrease.

The control group saw higher performance in all email sends except for the third email (as seen above), where the treatment briefly outperformed the control with 6 donations compared to 3. However, this isolated success was not enough to offset the overall underperformance of the treatment.

These findings suggest that donor autonomy language, as tested in this campaign, may not resonate as strongly with active donors and could potentially detract from conversion effectiveness in this context. Further testing may be required to refine the messaging approach or explore other engagement strategies.

experiments.title: How Donor Autonomy Language on Lapsed Donor Impacts Transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating donor autonomy language in fundraising emails targeted at a lapsed donor segment will re-engage recipients by emphasizing their sense of choice and control. This approach is expected to make lapsed donors feel more empowered and valued, ultimately increasing donation conversions and encouraging them to renew their support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted in partnership with WUNC, a North Carolina NPR station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The primary goal of this study was to explore the impact of donor autonomy language on behavior and engagement.
The experiment focused on a series of five emails sent to a lapsed segment of WUNC’s donor base. Each email aimed to evaluate whether emphasizing donor autonomy—language that reinforces a donor’s sense of choice and control over their giving—would influence donation rates and overall responsiveness.
To measure the effect, we created two variations of the emails. The control group received messages without any autonomy-focused language, while the test group received emails that intentionally incorporated phrases designed to highlight donor choice and empowerment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In the lapsed donor segment, incorporating donor autonomy language in the treatment emails resulted in a significant improvement in conversion rates compared to the control. While the control group had a conversion rate of 0.01%, the treatment group achieved 0.07%—a 398.4% relative increase with 89.7% confidence.

Although the control group generated slightly more clicks (44 vs. 39), the treatment group consistently outperformed the control in donations across all five emails. This suggests that donor autonomy language may be more effective at re-engaging lapsed donors, potentially resonating with their desire for choice and control as they consider renewing their support.

These results highlight an opportunity to further explore and optimize autonomy-driven messaging for reactivating lapsed donors.

experiments.title: How a multiple choice mission-focused question on an email affects the donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Using a support directed, mission-focused multiple choice question during a calendar year-end campaign will increase the conversion rate because they will read about the many ways the organization works towards their mission and the resources needed to accomplish them all.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
Dallas Theological Seminary wants to reach more people worldwide with biblical-truth and teach people how to study the Bible.
We ran this test during their calendar year-end campaign. Traffic came to this page through email. We sent the email to everyone on their house file.
We wanted to know if more people would donate after presented with four mission-focused and specific answers. The control email had a yes or no question, focusing only on one aspect of the DTS mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
1. The use of a support-directed, mission-focused multiple choice question during a calendar year-end campaign resulted in a significant increase in donations for Dallas Theological Seminary.
2. The treatment group, which was presented with four mission-focused and specific answers, experienced a 137.9% increase in donations compared to the control group.
3. The statistical level of confidence was 92.8%, indicating that the results are approaching validity but further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the treatment.
Future Implications:
1. Incorporating multiple mission-focused options in donation appeals can potentially improve the effectiveness of fundraising campaigns.
2. Future experimentation should continue to explore different ways of presenting the organization's mission and impact to donors to maximize donation outcomes.
3. Conducting additional tests with larger sample sizes and varied messaging strategies can help validate the initial findings and provide more robust insights for future campaign optimization.

experiments.title: How including specific goals and stats in an email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including stats and goals for 2025 will increase click-through rate because specific and measurable outcomes demonstrate the tangible impact of a donor’s gift, making the appeal more compelling and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
This experiment involved testing email copy for The Navigators’ donor appeals in their calendar year-end campaign. The test was conducted within an email campaign sent to existing and prospective donors, comparing two variations: the Control copy, which used general language about the impact of donations, and the Treatment copy, which included specific statistics and measurable goals for 2025.
This experiment was prompted by the desire to understand whether more detailed and concrete information about The Navigators’ progress and goals would resonate more effectively with donors. The idea stemmed from the assumption that presenting measurable outcomes and future plans would create a stronger emotional and logical connection, leading to higher engagement and click-through rates. Additionally, recent donor feedback highlighted a preference for knowing the direct impact of their contributions, which further inspired the inclusion of these stats and goals in the Treatment copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including specific statistics and measurable goals for the future in donor appeals can significantly increase the click-through rate compared to using general language about the impact of donations. The Treatment copy, which contained detailed information about the organization's progress and goals for 2025, outperformed the Control copy with a 30.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with an 87% level of confidence.

Additionally, we saw the treatment group increase donor conversion rate by 27% but with only a 48% level of confidence.
