In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of including specific and measurable outcomes in donor appeals to further validate these findings. It would also be valuable to explore how different types of statistics and goals resonate with donors, as well as the optimal way to present this information for maximum effectiveness. Additionally, considering donor feedback and preferences when crafting email copy and appeals can help tailor messages to better meet their expectations and motivations for giving.

Overall, these results suggest that donors respond positively to concrete data and future plans, indicating that this strategy may enhance donor engagement and support for The Navigators. Further experimentation and refinement of this approach can help optimize future fundraising efforts and drive continued success in connecting with donors.

experiments.title: Does adding a sample of a daily devotional in the body of an email impact donations for a non-donor audience?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a sample of a devotional for nondonors will achieve a change in donation conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2024 - 12/13/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
One of their most popular offers every year is a 265-page daily devotional that is sent to the donor as a thank you for their donation of any amount.
For this experiment, which was run on their housefile and targeted their non-donor audience, we wanted to see whether adding a sample passage from the devotional made an impact on donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing a sample of a devotional for non-donors potentially has a positive impact on donation conversions, as evidenced by the 57.3% increase in donations for all traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical level of confidence of 81.8% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, indicating that these results may not be reliable and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure a sufficient sample size and statistical power to confidently determine the effectiveness of such strategies. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. This will help in making more informed decisions based on the outcomes of the experiments.

experiments.title: Does the fact that 100% of a donors gift goes to mission affect conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding transparency about where the donor’s gift is allocated will increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by clearly showing how their generosity directly supports missionaries and Kingdom work.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/03/2024
For Giving Tuesday, IMB wanted to test how adding transparency about the allocation of the donor’s gift would affect the donation conversion rate on both of their Giving Tuesday appeals.
We kept the control and treatment audiences consistent throughout both emails. The note of the allocation was added to the treatment emails as well as the donation page for those who received the treatment email.
They believed it would increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by showing how their gift directly supports missionary work.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding transparency about where the donor's gift is allocated can have a significant impact on the conversion rate for donations. In this case, the treatment group saw a 43.9% increase in donations compared to the control group, indicating that donors may be more inclined to give when they have a clear understanding of how their gift will be used.

Additionally, we calculated the impact of click-through rates in the emails and only saw a 1.1% increase with a 28.6% LoC. This means the addition of the transparency note did not have an effect on click-through rate, but helped increase giving once people got to the donation page.

To apply this learning in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of transparency on donation conversion rates. By conducting additional experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different variations of allocation transparency, we can further validate the initial findings and determine the most effective strategies for communicating allocation to donors.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that transparency in donation appeals can build trust and confidence in the organization, ultimately leading to increased support for the mission.

experiments.title: How a more conversational tone on an email offering an ebook impacts click through rates for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a more conversational tone for non-donors will achieve an increase in click throughs and email sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/03/2024 - 10/08/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Income Inequality, we wanted to see if a more conversational approach to email copy made an impact on click throughs and email sign ups for a non-donor audience
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a more conversational tone in email copy for non-donors resulted in a 6.1% increase in click-through rates. While this indicates a positive impact, the statistical level of confidence of 83.8% is below the threshold of 85% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following points:

1. Ensure a higher level of statistical confidence: To draw reliable conclusions, it is crucial to have a statistical level of confidence of at least 85%. This will help in making informed decisions based on the experiment results.

2. Continue testing conversational tone: The positive trend observed in this experiment suggests that a conversational tone may resonate well with non-donor audiences. It could be beneficial to further test this approach with a larger sample size to validate the impact.

3. Explore other variables: Apart from tone, it may be worthwhile to test other variables such as subject lines, call-to-action language, or design elements to optimize email performance for non-donor audiences.

4. Collect qualitative feedback: In addition to quantitative data, gathering qualitative feedback from the audience can provide valuable insights into their preferences and behaviors. This could help in refining future experiments and strategies.

Overall, while the experiment showed a positive trend, it is essential to prioritize statistical significance and continue testing different approaches to effectively engage non-donor audiences and drive conversions.

experiments.title: How using more focused copy on a donor email offering up a content offer impacted offer performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing copy for donors will achieve a change in email performance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Personal Freedom and the Moral Case for Capitalism, we wanted to see whether copy that focused more on the offer, and not use pop culture references, impact email and content offer performance. This experiment was for an active donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that focusing the copy on the offer itself can have a positive impact on email performance for donors. The treatment group, which received the email with copy focused on the offer without using pop culture references, saw an 11.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group.
Moreover, the treatment version saw a 55% increase in email sign-ups, with a 100% level of confidence. And it also saw an 11% increase in donations after someone signed up to get the offer, but only with a 35% level of confidence.
We can then say that for an active donor audience, for this specific content offfer, using more focused copy led to an increase in all KPIs. Future experiments could look at making sure there is congruency at the landing and donation page level.

experiments.title: How adding relevancy to email copy impacted clicks and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding relevancy to an offer for non donors will achieve an increase in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/03/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and Non Donor audiences. This specific test was on their non donor audience. For this test on one of their most popular offers, we wanted to see whether email copy focused on relevancy outperformed the control copy, which has been used multiple times.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding relevancy to an offer for non donors led to a 5.6% increase in clicks compared to the control email. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 80.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. Therefore, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Donations had a 64% increase on the treatment with a 80.7% level of confidence. The control had 11 gifts, while the treatment with the relevancy copy had 18 gifts. It would be interesting to further test relevancy copy to see if this continues to impacts donations and makes it feel more personal to the donor.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is at least 95% to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different ways of adding relevancy to offers for non donors to see if it consistently leads to increased engagement. By conducting experiments with a higher level of confidence and testing various approaches to relevancy, the Hoover Institution can gather more reliable insights to inform their email marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How Adding More Than One Link In Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a second link for the FYE email will achieve a higher click rate because there will be more opportunity to click and go to the survey page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/22/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campagin every year. For this campaign the organization will publish ads, web elements, and email in an effort to support the campaign.
For this test we wanted to see if we could increase traffic to a survey page from their email channel. To do this we tested having one link vs. two link in the email body. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a second link in the FYE email resulted in a 5.2% increase in clicks for all traffic. While this indicates a positive impact on click rates, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

It is also important to note that once a user completes the survey, the user gets redirected to a donation page. While we don't have the data to see if increasing the number of links increased the completion rate of the survey, we can see that people who did receive the treatment had a 11% in giving at a 41% LoC (40 gifts vs 45 gifts).

experiments.title: How Adding An Image To an Email Effects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image for the edu FYE email appeal will achieve a higher click rate because the image will resonate with the user to continue reading the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campaign each year that consists of a full email campaign to their Education housefile.
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding an image to the email copy led to more link clicks to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we learned that adding an image to the email decreases the click rate if you look at the overall impact. To make this test even, we split the housefile into donors, nondonors, and lapsed donors. Each segment did produce a decrease in clicks, however, the treatment version with the image created a 47% lift in unsubscribes.

Looking at transactions from each email, the control also had a significant increase in transactions and revenue.

experiments.title: Does Changing the Position of an Image Affect Clicks in Email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image at the bottom of an email for the FYE EDU appeals will achieve a higher click rate because the image will be reinforcing the copy that was just read.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Earlier in Moody’s FYE campaign we tested adding an image towards the top of an email to see if it could catch peoples attention and make them want to read more of them email. The results showed that it did the opposite and made people click out or unsubscribe.
For this test we wanted to see if placing an image at the bottom of an email copy could reinforce the message and create a higher click rate in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image at the bottom of an email for FYE EDU appeals did not achieve a higher click rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, if you look at revenue you can see a slight increase in the generosity of the donors when the image is present in the email. Both the control and treatment received the same number of donations, however the gift the average gift is up slightly when compared to the control. It is important to note that the donation size is very small, and the control did receive large outlying gifts that had to be taken out to not skew the average gift.

It would be interesting to test this tactic again to get a larger pool of donors to see if this is something that can be validated.

experiments.title: titw: Does Adding Moody Bible Institute in Emails Affect Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that tying the message back to MBI for a FYE Appeal Email will achieve a higher donor conversion rate  because donors will give more if the ask is associated with the parent ministry.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
For this appeal email, we wanted to see if a drawing a line back to Moody Bible Institute instead of Today in the Word could aspire more giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment, which tied the message back to Moody Bible Institute instead of Today in the Word, resulted in a 24.5% decrease in donations for all traffic. Backing up the top of the funnel, there was also a 1% decrease in clicks in the treatment

experiments.title: How using the concept of autonomy and donor volition impacts giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on autonomous motivation for email recipients will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because we focused less on urgency/pressure and more on value .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2024
In an effort to increase donor conversion rate, we wanted shift the messaging of a campaign email to be less urgency/pressure focused and more autonomy driven – giving the donor the choice to give/not give. This is based on autonomous (volitional) motivation – donors give because they personally understand and value the cause; it’s their way to enact and share their values. This test was a complete copy overhaul and was tested across 2 of the last emails of a high urgency campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that focusing on autonomous motivation for email recipients led to a significant decrease in donations compared to the urgency/pressure focused messaging; a directional 46% decrease in donor conversion rate with a LoC of 93%. This indicates that the shift in messaging strategy did not resonate as effectively with donors as intended.

Additionally we experienced a valid 16% decrease in clicks, indicating that the messaging did not resonate enough with recipients to even click through the donation page.

Given that these are the last 2 emails of a campaign, where urgency is what typically drives action, it may be that RTB's audience is primed and expectant of this type of messaging as they approach the deadline.

experiments.title: How an autonomy-supportive treatment impacted clicks on a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing more autonomy  for the housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there will be an enhanced level of motivation that follows the increase in the copy’s autonomy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2024 - 07/09/2024
When running a fiscal year end campaign, we were curious if giving the email readers more autonomy in the email would lead to higher motivation to give and increase clicks in the email.
First and foremost, autonomy support begins with an orientation. As much as we might wish to reduce autonomy support to an algorithmic set of tactics (e.g., offering choice, avoiding pressure or guilt), true autonomy support starts with an attitude: we are not persuading donors, but rather inviting them to support a mission we believe they would value, and informing them about the ways they can help.
Having said that, when we start with “make this match-back or premium more autonomy supportive,” we are already a few steps behind. Within the “autonomy paradigm,” one would not even offer a premium or match-back to entice or seduce donors.
Nonetheless, we believe our treatment copy has made the appeals less pressuring or controlling and, in some instances, supportive of autonomy. This is demonstrated by the removal of controlling language such as “I still need your help” and “We need you”, simplifying sentences to enhance readability and clarity, using “your support” and “your contribution” to emphasize the reader’s autonomy, maintaining urgency without using pressuring language, reducing redundant phrases to streamline the appeal, preserving the core message and call to action, replacing the sense of urgency with a focus on making the strongest effort as the campaign comes to a close, and emphasizing the reader’s autonomy and the impact of their contribution.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted to test the impact of introducing more autonomy in emails for a fiscal year end campaign at Care Net showed that the treatment version resulted in a 57.5% decrease in clicks. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.9% so the results are not valid.
One key learning from this experiment is that while the hypothesis that increased autonomy in email copy would lead to higher motivation and increased clicks seemed plausible, the actual results did not support this. It is essential to recognize that autonomy support is more than just the language used in the emails; it is about the overall attitude of inviting donors to participate in a mission they believe in.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to consider the holistic approach to autonomy support rather than just focusing on the language used in the emails. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that experiments are run for a long enough duration to gather sufficient data for meaningful analysis and to have a higher statistical level of confidence before drawing any conclusions.

experiments.title: How a simplified campaign Challenge Grant goal impacts overall revenue and average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the campaign challenge grant goal for Human Coalition housefile will achieve an increase in donor conversion and revenue because it eliminates potential confusion and instead focuses on highlighting the impact of the challenge grant.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2024
For Human Coalition’s Fiscal Year End campaign, readers were presented with two goals: the overall goal of $703,875 as well as the challenge grant goal of $352,000. In reality, these two goals represent the same number. $703,875 is the total of what would be raised if the challenge grant goal was met. We hypothesized that presenting the user with both could cause unnecessary confusion and friction. We believe that by simplifying this goal in the treatment, to only highlight the challenge grant as the main priority will help improve donor conversion and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the campaign challenge grant goal for Human Coalition by focusing solely on the $352,000 goal led to a significant increase in donor conversion and revenue. By eliminating potential confusion caused by presenting two goals, the treatment version saw a 124.8% increase in revenue for all traffic.
Our hypothesis is that by simplifying the ask, and focusing the reader's attention on the doubled impact of the challenge grant, users were more likely to make a larger gift knowing.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing this simplified approach to determine its consistent impact on donor behavior. Further testing with larger sample sizes and variations in messaging could help strengthen the validity of these findings. Additionally, applying this learning to other campaigns within Human Coalition and potentially extending it to other similar nonprofit organizations could help optimize fundraising efforts and improve overall outcomes.

experiments.title: How autonomy in an email impacts revenue and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that utilizing an autonomy technique for email subscribers will achieve higher revenue due to an increase in gifts and average gift because the donor is making the decision themselves.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/08/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During June and December, PTV conducts matching campaigns to increase incentive for donors and prospective donors.
For the final email in the June campaign, we decided to test autonomy to see if that approach would encourage more gifts and higher gifts from donors
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on utilizing autonomy in email campaigns for Pathway to Victory's matching campaigns are:

The treatment group, which received emails with an autonomy technique, experienced a 34.0% increase in revenue for all traffic compared to the control group. This includes a 16% in average gift and 15% increase in donor conversion.

Two gifts were removed from this experiment as they were viewed as outliers. A $1,200 gift from the control and $5,000 from the treatment.

experiments.title: How reducing the length of copy and increasing urgency impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by shortening copy and increasing urgency for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate and revenue because the ask is stronger and less copy to digest.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/05/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During June and December, PTV conducts matching challenges in order to increase donor conversion.
For the next to last email during their June match campaign we decided to test shortening the email and increasing urgency to see if we could get more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that shortening the email copy and increasing urgency can lead to a significant increase in revenue. The treatment group saw a 38.6% increase in revenue for all traffic, which is a promising result.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of shorter copy and increased urgency in driving conversions. Further testing with larger sample sizes and different variations of the email content could help confirm the initial findings and determine the best approach for maximizing donations and revenue during matching challenges. Additionally, we should strive to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence to ensure the validity of the results before making any significant changes to our email strategy.

experiments.title: How including questions to increase relevance impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using conversational “you-focused” language for appeal email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because the reader will have increased curiosity about the personal relevance of the message for them. because the reader will have increased curiosity about the personal relevance of the message for them. because the reader will have increased curiosity about the personal relevance of the message for them.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/02/2024
When sending an appeal email to the email file, we wanted to find out if using a conversational approach and including two-way communication would improve overall engagement. Rather than just telling the reader what to do and what to read, we wondered if adding relevance, credibility and opening a loop of curiosity will improve the conversion rate by using conversational language. We developed a treatment version of the email pointing to the same donation link, but introduced a series of questions to the reader throughout the message to attempt to pull the reader in and asking them questions to get them thinking and interested in the rest of the email content. We split the message audience 50/50 and measured results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment using conversational language and two-way communication in the email did not lead to the desired increase in donations. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 43.3% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 84.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the balance between personalization and effectiveness of the messaging. While using conversational language and asking questions may increase engagement in some cases, it is crucial to carefully test and analyze the impact of these strategies before implementing them on a larger scale. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence is essential for making informed decisions based on the results of an experiment. This experiment highlights the importance of thorough testing and cautious interpretation of results before making changes to email communication strategies.

experiments.title: How mentioning a last match donation impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by mentioning a donors participation in a previous match campaign for December match donors will achieve a high donor conversion rate because the donor is reminded of the impact they have already made.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/28/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During June and December, PTV conducts match campaigns that double the impact of a donors gift.
For the June 2024 campaign, we decided to test with the segment who made a gift during the December 2023 campaign to see if by mentioning their gift in December would encourage them to donate again in June.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that mentioning a donor's participation in a previous match campaign for December match donors did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a significant decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that this strategy may not be effective in encouraging repeat donations.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of mentioning previous donations on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to further test this strategy with different messaging or timing to see if it can yield positive results. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are statistically valid before drawing conclusions. Further testing would be necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this strategy and determine the best approach to increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How mentioning a last match donation impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by mentioning a donors participation in a previous match campaign for December match donors will achieve a high donor conversion rate because the donor is reminded of the impact they have already made.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/28/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During June and December, PTV conducts match campaigns that double the impact of a donors gift.
For the June 2024 campaign, we decided to test with the segment who made a gift during the December 2023 campaign to see if by mentioning their gift in December would encourage them to donate again in June.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that mentioning a donor's participation in a previous match campaign for December match donors did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a significant decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that this strategy may not be effective in encouraging repeat donations.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of mentioning previous donations on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to further test this strategy with different messaging or timing to see if it can yield positive results. Perhaps sharing more of the impact that their first gift has and encouraging them to keep it going with a second gift.

experiments.title: How highlighting that only a small amount of people donate after downloading an ebook affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting that only a small minority people donate for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”. because it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2024 - 03/04/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering a free ebook called The Founder’s Almanac.
They had previously tested the idea of highlighting the fact that “less than 1%” of people who take the survey are willing to take the next step and donate.” This was tested on a survey offer, and it produced a 105% lift in conversion rate with a 98% level of confidence.
This language had increased conversion because it appealed to the ideal donor, who was attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
They wanted to retest this concept on an ebook funnel to see if they would get a similar lift in conversion rate.
They inserted this language in two places, and split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Highlighting the fact that only a small minority of people donate resulted in a significant increase in Transactions for All Traffic, with a 64.0% lift and a statistical level of confidence of 92.8%. This indicates that the language in the treatment version resonated with the ideal donor who is motivated by being part of a select group willing to do the "right thing".

2. The treatment also saw a substantial increase in Transactions - One-Time, experiencing a 770% lift with a 96% level of confidence. This suggests that the messaging specifically appealed to individuals looking to make a one-time contribution.

This is an application of social identity theory. This theory suggests that people tend to categorize themselves and others into various social groups. Supporting the underdog can be related to group identity and solidarity with a particular group.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue using the language that highlights the small minority of people who donate in future experimentation. This messaging appears to resonate with the target audience and create a sense of exclusivity and righteousness in supporting the cause. However, it would also be beneficial to continuously test and optimize this messaging to ensure its continued effectiveness and relevance.

experiments.title: How including a gift handle with the corresponding impact described in a fundraising email affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake will achieve more revenue because we’re providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
The Navigators are running their bi-annual Eagle Lake Campaign to raise money for this kids’ camp that operates every summer. In the kickoff email of this campaign, they wanted to test how including gift handles in the email would affect donor conversion rate. Each gift amount described what that amount would provide for Eagle Lake.
This experiment idea came from their direct mail piece which included “tickets” for people to purchase. Each ticket was for a different amount (same as the email) and described what each level of ticket would provide for the kids at Eagle Lake.
To keep the language consistent between the email and the donation page, the treatment donation page also had the gift handles in the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake can significantly increase revenue. The treatment group saw a 40.2% increase in revenue for all traffic, which indicates that providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used can motivate donors to give more.
However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 82.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that while the results show a positive impact, they are not completely reliable and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of gift handles and impact descriptions in donation appeals to see if the results can be replicated and validated. Additionally, it may be helpful to investigate other factors that could influence donor behavior and revenue generation to further optimize fundraising efforts for Eagle Lake.

experiments.title: How reminding lapsed members about the value of the membership card in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the click-through rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if lapsed members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including reminder text about the value of a membership card can significantly increase the click-through rate for lapsed members. The treatment in this experiment saw a 41.9% increase in Clicks for All Traffic, indicating that reminding members of the benefits they receive can be a compelling reason for them to donate again.

Note: While the treatment email had a higher click-through rate, it also had a slightly lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test and refine the messaging around the value proposition of a membership card. This could include highlighting specific benefits, such as exclusive content, special discounts, or access to events. By effectively communicating the value that members receive in exchange for their donation, we can increase the likelihood of them re-engaging with the organization.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to further test different variations of the value proposition messaging to optimize the impact on member reactivation. By continuing to experiment and analyze the results, we can uncover the most effective ways to encourage lapsed members to donate and renew their support for The Heritage Foundation.

experiments.title: How does focusing on an urgent need rather than a premium in the final email of a campaign impact donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of urgent language instead of an incentive for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because  the sense of urgency and a deadline will act as a motivating factor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test urgent language in the email vs the incentive of a premium. For the majority of this campaign, we have focused on the incentive of a book premium. However, for the final email our hypothesis was that by the final email if they have not converted to a monthly donor by this point, then the premium is not incentive enough for them to give.  Therefore, the urgent language may be motivating due to the presence of a deadline.
The control included the premium and the treatment included the urgent language.
This test ran as a 50/50 A/B test to The Navigators housefile audience in an effort to activate more recurring donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that urgent language instead of a premium may be effective in motivating The Navigators audience to give in the final hours of an appeal. The treatment email saw a 47.2% lift in transactions at an 80.8% level of confidence. While this test did not validate, the results are compelling in that, urgent language resonated well with their audience.
While the clicks did not validate for the treatment in this test, it does suggest that those who were compelled to click the link were more motivated to give once they landed on the donation page.
One thing that is important to note is the presence of the premium throughout previous emails. The lack of the premium in the treatment may have been a deterrent to give with the email because they could have received the premium in previous emails.
In future experimentation, it may be worth including the premium and have the addition of the urgent language in the treatment rather than removing the premium all together from the treatment email.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a content offer email impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including social proof in a content offer email for the housefile will achieve an increase in donor conversions because it reinforces the perceived value of the offer based on what others are already saying about it..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2024 - 02/14/2024
For Reasons to Believe’s resend of the Genesis 1 e-book offer to their housefile, we hypothesized that since this offer has already run previously including a line of social proof in the email to generate interest would impact sign-up rate and donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The inclusion of social proof in the content offer email for the housefile led to a significant increase in donor conversions. The statistical level of confidence was at 88.2%, indicating that the results are approaching validity but further testing is recommended for certainty.

What's notable is that although this experiment only took place at the email level, however the primary metrics impacted were downstream in the funnel. The line of social proof's impact on click rate was negligible, with a 1.2% lift at a 16.1% LOC. However, those who clicked from the social proof treatment were far more likely to sign up and far more likely to give with a 14.3% increase in sign-ups on the landing page and a 59.6% in donor conversions.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the potential effectiveness of social proof in driving donor conversions. By incorporating this strategy into future email campaigns and conducting further testing, Reasons to Believe can continue to optimize their efforts to attract and convert donors effectively.

experiments.title: How does focusing on an urgent need rather than a premium in the final email of a campaign impact clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of urgent language instead of an incentive for potential donors will achieve an increase in clicks because  the sense of urgency and a deadline will act as a motivating factor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test urgent language in the email vs the incentive of a premium. For the majority of this campaign, we have focused on the incentive of a book premium. However, for the final email our hypothesis was that by the final email if they have not converted to a monthly donor by this point, then the premium is not incentive enough for them to give.  Therefore, the urgent language may be motivating due to the presence of a deadline.
The control included the premium and the treatment included the urgent language.
This test ran as a 50/50 A/B test to The Navigators housefile audience in an effort to activate more recurring donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the urgent language in the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign did not result in an increase in clicks from potential donors. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a drop of 13.3% in clicks at a 81.0% level of confidence. While these results did not validate, they do suggest that the urgent language was not a motivation factor for The Navigators audience to click the link, and that they potentially respond better to the presence of an incentive or premium.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the impact of urgent language and deadlines on donor behavior. It is possible that in this instance, potential donors may have already been motivated by the premium incentive in previous emails and adding urgent language only served to create confusion or negatively impact their decision-making process.
Moving forward, it may be beneficial to continue testing different incentives and messaging strategies to see what resonates best with potential donors. Additionally, ensuring that experiments are properly powered and have a high level of statistical confidence will help to draw more reliable conclusions for future decision making.

experiments.title: How removing images and html styling impacted click through rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that stripping html from an email for the Today in the Word Audience will achieve a higher CTR because it will appeal more personal and less spam like.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could improve the CTR of an email by changing the layout to make it feel like it is a personal email. To do this we stripped all of the HTML, besides the header logo at the top.
In doing this it makes the email seem less like a marketing email and more like a 1 on 1 email.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that stripping HTML from an email for the Today in the Word Audience can significantly increase click-through rates (CTR). The treatment, which made the email feel more personal by removing all HTML except for the header logo, resulted in a 10.9% increase in clicks for all traffic.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with lapsed members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for lapsed members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired action is cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/22/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign.  The email was a personal communication informing lapsed members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask lapsed members to “reactivate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage lapsed members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for lapsed donors resulted in a 28.0% increase in the click-through rate. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.
Note: While the click-through rate increased the conversion rate decreased by 50%. The sample size was not large enough to attain the required level of confidence of 95% or above.
In the future, it is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to obtain more reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to continue testing different variations of the call to action to determine the most effective approach for encouraging lapsed donors to re-engage. By using what was learned from this experiment, we can refine our strategies and optimize our communication to better connect with our audience and achieve our goals.

experiments.title: How leading a membership ask email with the problem for lapsed members affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading an email with the problem will increase click-through rate because it will give the reader more context as to why they should renew their membership with Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran on the first email of the campaign for lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how lapsed members would react to leading the email with a question that poses the problem Heritage is trying to solve rather than leading the email with a direct ask to reactivate their membership. In doing so, we believe users will be more likely to click through to the donation page and reactivate their membership because the leading question allows them to relate to the issue and want to be apart of the change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that leading an email with the problem did not increase click-through rates for lapsed members to reactivate their membership with The Heritage Foundation. The treatment actually resulted in a 19.3% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the accepted threshold of 85%, meaning the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making. Additionally, the treatment group saw a 46.9% decrease in donor conversion with an 85.3% level of confidence.

Based on these findings, it is important to reconsider the hypothesis and approach for future experimentation. It may be necessary to use a different strategy to engage lapsed members and entice them to reactivate their membership. This could include focusing on the benefits and value of being a member, highlighting success stories or achievements of the organization, or providing exclusive content or offers. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that future experiments have a sufficient statistical level of confidence to ensure the validity of the results.

experiments.title: How does calling out the loss of exclusive membership benefits for lapsed members affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out what they will lose by not reactivating their membership for lapsed members will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the fear of losing something can be a more powerful motivator than the prospect of gaining something of equal value.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was sent to lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how lapsed members would react to calling out what they will lose by not reactivating their membership in 2024.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that calling out what lapsed members will lose by not reactivating their membership can be a powerful motivator for increasing donor conversion rates. The treatment in which the loss aversion approach was used saw a 105.3% increase in donations compared to the control with an 89% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to refine the messaging and presentation of the loss aversion approach to optimize its impact. Testing different variations, such as emphasizing specific benefits or highlighting the potential negative consequences of not reactivating, could help identify the most compelling approach.

Additionally, it would be valuable to analyze the characteristics and preferences of lapsed members who responded positively to the loss aversion strategy. Understanding their motivations and demographics can inform targeted marketing efforts to similar segments in the future.

Overall, this experiment suggests that leveraging loss aversion can be a successful strategy to reactivate lapsed members. Continued testing and refinement will provide more conclusive evidence and insights for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How using a personalized and gratitude-centered tone for existing members affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: The treatment email, which employs a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, will result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email because the treatment might foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for existing members in 2023 to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how existing members would react to a more personalized and gratitude-centered tone in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment email, which employed a more personalized and gratitude-centered approach, did not result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email. In fact, the treatment email had a 13.0% decrease in donations for all traffic.

While the results did not reach a 95% level of confidence, they are approaching validity, indicating that there might be some truth to the findings. Future experimentation should be conducted to further validate these results.

Additionally, the treatment group saw a 11.6% decrease in click-through rate in the email with an 87.9% level of confidence.

Based on this experiment, it is clear that a personalized and gratitude-centered approach may not be effective in encouraging existing members to renew their membership.

When conducting future experiments, it is important to keep in mind the potential impact of a more personalized and gratitude-centered approach on donor behavior. This could be further explored by testing different variations of personalized content or by targeting specific segments of the existing member base.

Overall, this experiment provides valuable insights into the impact of a personalized and gratitude-centered approach on membership renewal rates, and the learnings should be used to inform future experimentation and optimization of renewal campaigns.

experiments.title: How posing a lower, $10 ask for non donors impacts email engagement and giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a specific, lower ask amount for non donors will achieve an increase in click-through rates and ultimately gifts because having a specific, low amount can reduce decision friction and potential anxiety.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2023 - 12/22/2023
During calendar year end, we wanted to activate more nondonors on RTB’s file. In order to lower the threshold for those who have never given, we posed a $10 ask as a test. The control received no specific amount while the treatment received a $10 ask in their email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a specific, lower ask amount of $10 to non-donors can result in an increase in click-through rates compared to not mentioning a specific amount. The treatment version of the email showed a 20.5% increase in clicks for all traffic with a level of confidence of 87%.

Additionally, only two gifts were generated from this email both of which came from the treatment and  both at an amount of $25 which is higher than the initial proposed amount.

It's suggested that this test is ran again with the $10 ask as the starting point of the gift array that the treatment group receives. That way, there's further congruency in the ask and can potentially activate more donors.

experiments.title: How tying claims of value to a match impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by expounding the claim of value and tie to the match for email subscriebrs will achieve an increase in donation conversions because the value prop and impact of the match is more clearly explained.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/29/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During December, PTV has a $500k match available to increase motivation for CYE gifts.
In an effort to increase conversions further, we decided to test listing out claims of value for PTV and tie them directly to the match as a method to increase impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Expounding the claim of value and tie to the match for email subscribers can potentially lead to an increase in donation conversions.
2. The treatment group showed a 22.2% increase in donations, which is promising, but not statistically significant at the traditional 95% confidence level.
3. The statistical level of confidence reached 91.2%, indicating that the results are approaching validity and further testing is recommended to confirm the effect.
4. It is important to continue testing and refining the messaging and value proposition to maximize the impact on donation conversions.
5. Future experiments should consider adjusting variables such as the match amount, the format of the message, and the target audience to further optimize the results.
6. The insights gained from this experiment can serve as a starting point for future experimentation and allow for more informed decision-making in improving donation conversions.
Note: we did not run this experiment based on revenue due to additional large gifts that significantly skewed results due to calendar year end and additional motivation of the match.

experiments.title: How does addressing someone directly and asking them a personal question affect click-through rate for a high urgency email?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors will achieve a higher click-through rate because it keeps the user engaged and helps them feel more relatable to the situation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2023
The Navigators launched their calendar year campaign with an email that started with a direct statement about how Christians face problems more now than ever before. We decided to test how opening an email with a personalized question to draw them in would affect click-through rate to the donation page.
In previous testing, we’ve seen a more personalized tone perform better because it allows the user to relate to the problem at hand and causes them to engage with the email longer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors can have a positive impact on click-through rates. The treatment group, which received the personalized question at the beginning of the email, saw a 20.3% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group with an 87.2% level of confidence.

Because of these results, we believe that opening an email with a personalized question rather than a direct statement will keep users engaged with the email causing them to be more likely to click the link to donate.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of personalized tones and messaging to optimize click-through rates. Additionally, it would be important to analyze other metrics such as conversions and donations to understand the full impact of the personalized approach. By continuously testing and iterating, we can refine our email strategies to effectively engage potential donors and drive meaningful action.

experiments.title: How aligning call-to-action verbs in a tribute widget impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning all the call-to-action words in the triibue widget for site visitors will achieve an increase in donations because there will be additional clarity and affirmation in the conversion process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2023 - 11/10/2023
CaringBridge’s users heavily rely on the tribute widget in order to make a gift in honor of their loved one. In order to increase conversion rate, we sought to add clarity to the pre-existing copy by using consistent CTA’s and driving the desired action: give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning all the call-to-action words in the tribute widget did not result in an increase in donations. In fact, there was a decrease of 9.3% in donations for all traffic. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means these results cannot be considered valid.

Additionally, we saw the results vacillating between flat and a slight decrease throughout the experiment. This suggests that creating congruency in the call-to-action words for the tribute widget is not a significant motivating factor for journal page viewers. They likely already have enough clarity from the existing copy about what the tribute widget is asking of them

It's suggested we test other concepts in the copy such as an emotional appeal to see if that will drive an increase in giving.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button on article pages with a slide-out donor appeal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky donate button with a slide-out CTA featuring additional value proposition copy will increase donor conversion by strengthening the value proposition of the sticky donate button.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 03/31/2023
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They had previously tested a sticky donate button on article pages that floated on the right-hand side of the page with a pulsing heart and linked to the main donation page. This sticky button improved donor conversion. They wanted to test a new version of that button with an expanded value proposition that would appear in a slide-out when the button was clicked. This they posited would enable visitors to receive a reason to donate before visiting the donation page itself. Visitors could then choose to give or dismiss the message altogether.
For the experiment, they wanted to test two different value propositions. One would focus on FamilyLife’s standard appeal message about making a gift to support families in crisis. The other would focus on the perceived value the visitor received from FamilyLife this year and ask them to make a gift to keep FamilyLife here for them and their family.
Originally these two variations were going to be tested against the prior winning sticky donate button that lacked a slide-out CTA message. Due to a setup error, the control experience lacked a sticky donate button altogether, so it became an experiment between these two competing value proposition slide-outs and no sticky donate button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that inviting visitors to reflect on the value FamilyLife provided them and then asking them to donate to keep FamilyLife there for them and their family was far more motivational than asking them to make a gift to help other families. We hypothesize this is because it is harder for a potential donor to think about the needs of unidentified "families in critical need" than it is to remember their needs and how FamilyLife has been their for them.
We will continue testing this value proposition to further refine it and identify key motivational elements for donor conversion. Our next test will be this slide-out CTA sticky button against the former control experience of just a sticky donate button without a slide-out message.

experiments.title: Which value prop is more affective in a sponsorship ask for PFI–religion or justice?
experiments.researchQuestion: An appeal to someone’s sense of justice will work better at converting PFI’s audience into sponsors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2020 - 12/02/2020
On Giving Tuesday, PFI was going to send out a sponsorship ask to their general donors. Since they are a religious organization that also deals with the justice system, we wanted to test whether an appeal based on religious reasons or on the basis of justice was more motivating.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not have enough results to validate, and need to be careful in using such a small scaled response as proof that the religious element is more motivating. We will continue to test as their file grows.

experiments.title: How a direct ask tied to a wider impact affected clickthrough rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a gift with far-reaching impact for a prospecting audience will achieve an increase in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2022 - 12/20/2022
As part of a calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, Illinois Policy Institute focused on raising funds for a new documentary film they are producing and releasing in 2023. Since this is a new project, we were curious to learn what would motivate donors to give their support. We wanted to test a narrow, specific ask – making a gift of $25 to cover the cost of tickets for people to view the documentary versus making a gift of $25 to help fund the cost of promoting the documentary on social media platforms. We split the audience 50/50 between the control and treatment emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found after sending the email was a 5.9% increase in click-through rates on the treatment email with 84.8% level of confidence so the results of the test are not valid. We believe the lift in click-through rate could be due to the fact that the control language suggests their gift will make an impact locally in their own state, but the treatment language expands the scope of the project to reach a national audience. This could be appealing to potential donors and could have motivated additional clicks. But since the confidence level is only 84.8%, we will have to continue testing the narrow ask vs. the wide ask to understand how the prospecting audience will respond to a tangible ask like a ticket for an audience member to view the documentary compared to a less tangible ask such as general promotion of the documentary.

When reviewing donor conversion rate results among the two variants, the treatment produced 14 donations to the control's 16 donations. The treatment saw a 12.9% decrease in donations at just a 29.4% level of confidence. Again, we will have to continue testing this approach (narrow ask vs. wide ask) to pick up on clicks and donations trends, and to reach a valid conclusion.

experiments.title: How presenting a daily goal in an email appeal affects donations during a year-end fundraising campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a daily goal in the body of an email appeal will increase donation rates by increasing the specificity of the ask and giving recipients a clear ‘micro’ goal that they can help achieve.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/27/2022
For this experiment, we wanted to test how presenting a daily goal (“today I’m looking for X donors”) in the body copy of an email appeal would affect donation rates for a public policy organization during a year-end fundraising campaign.
For this experiment, both previous donors and non-donors were included in the sample universe with ~50% of appeal recipients receiving the control (containing an ask that did not include a daily goal) and ~50% of appeal recipients receiving the treatment (containing an ask centered around a daily goal. Nothing else about the appeal was changed.
Major donors were excluded from this test and our experiment spanned seven appeal emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment over the course of seven emails during the organization's year-end fundraising campaign, we observed that the treatment produced a 12.2% decrease in donations (84% level of confidence).
The sample size of this experiment did not allow us to validate these results and therefore prevents us from drawing statistically valid conclusions about how presenting a daily goal in the body of an appeal email affects donation rates.
However, the results of this experiment do suggest that including a daily goal was not a motivating factor for recipients of this appeal and may even have demotivated them from giving. We also observed that the daily goal did not serve as a significant motivating factor in terms of clicking through to the donation page from the email, producing only a 2.1% lift with a 53.6% level of statistical confidence.
This may be because a daily goal does not add tangible value or a reason to give to the appeal. It is also in essence an organizational-focused goal that lacks a specific outcome that the daily goal will help achieve.
Based on these results, this public policy client may want to pursue a different method for increasing engagement and donations on the next high-urgency campaign or consider including a specific outcome that the daily goal will achieve. In either case, the organization should not assume that including a daily goal, as structured in this experiment, will increase donation rates during a high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How removing organizational language in an email appeal impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing organizational language in an email appeal for prospective donors will achieve more revenue .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2022
For Giving Tuesday, the IMB sent out email appeals to their housefile. In one of the emails we wanted to test how removing organizational language would impact either donor conversion rate or overall revenue. We hypothesized by replacing “the IMB” with words like “us” or “our,” the prospective donor would feel like they’re giving to people instead of a organization or corporate entity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When pulling the results, the donor conversion rate was relatively the same. We saw a 16% increase in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 52%. We also saw little no impact in click-thru rate for these emails. However when looking at revenue data, we saw a 51% directional lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 87.8%. While this is not a statistically valid result, it can be surmised that there are some donors who may have been motivated to give a larger gift when reading the treatment copy. The treatment copy speaks to the donor as if they're joining "us" to accomplish something as opposed to giving to someone–making this much more communal in nature.
It's recommended we retest this concept and also include the donation page copy in the experiment so that the donation page copy also has the organizational language removed in the treatment experience.

experiments.title: How increasing narrative affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing narrative score for previous donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/02/2022
Luther Seminary was running their biannual Working Preacher campaign. They had read some research that increasing the “narrative score” using a tool called CopyOptimizer would increase conversion rate, and wanted to put it to the test. They began with a standard appeal written by their copywriter and looked for ways to “add narrative” to it.
They decided the best way would be to put the reader in the shoes of a pastor who lacked the resources to prepare their weekly sermon. Then, they connected the appeal to provide this imaginary preacher with the resources he or she would need to teach their congregation.
When they read the treatment copy, they realized that it no longer made sense to have the thermometer image at the top. They removed that, knowing that it would add a variable to the treatment, but hoping that it would help the narrative copy make more impact.
They split their email file and send one email to each half to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 47.7% increase in conversion rate due to one, or a combination of adjustments. First, this shows that the image of the thermometer may keep people from engaging. Second—the biggest change—was to change the perspective of the reader. Instead of a

experiments.title: How including branding and fund type in a appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing branding and fund language for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/07/2022
The Navigators were running a Missionary Care campaign where they typically refer to their giving fund by name “Missionary Care Fund.” We hypothesized that by removing the branding and fund type and focusing on the impact of the fund, we would see an increase in donor conversion rate. We believe that donors aren’t motivated by the specific name of a fund but by what their gift is accomplishing.
We tested this hypothesis in an email appeal in their high urgency campaign. Instead of “Missionary Care Fund” our treatment copy used verbiage like ” help missionaries.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results for this experiment did not validate. We saw a 55% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 83.5%. This could indicate a near directional decrease in donor conversion rate when removing the branding and fund language from an email appeal. Upon even closer examination, we noted that the dollar amount in the appeal was supposed to be consistent at $75 and $200 was accidentally left in the control email.

It can be determined that it is likely that the Navigators' audience are familiar with the Missionary Care Fund and are not dissuaded to give by mentioning by name. Additionally, it is surprising that the higher $200 ask in the email resulted in more donations and a higher average gift of $86 vs $31.

Because this experiment did not validate and we actually ended up testing 2 variables instead of the intended one, it is recommended that this concept is retested in a medium that will get more traffic and transactions, like a sticky bar.

experiments.title: How adding specifics to a subject line impacted open rates for Pathway to Victory

rows.name: Join me for the Bible Prophecy Tour of Israel
Join me for the experience of a lifetime

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being specific in the subject line for the email file will achieve a change in response rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2022 - 07/19/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. One of the offerings they have is a Tour of Israel, led by Senior Pastor Dr. Robert Jeffress and his wife.
As part of the ongoing promotion for this tour, we are testing different subject lines to see which one gets a better response rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The difference in subject lines--one referencing the Tour of Israel and the other one being more vague but intriguing--had a negligible impact on open rates and click-through rates. With only a 0.79% increase in open rates, and a decrease of 4.9% in the experiment version in click through rate, this experiment indicates that further testing and finessing of the subject line is called for.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted revenue per donor
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest contribution for existing donors will achieve an increase in overall response and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2021 - 12/02/2021
As part of their Giving Tuesday campaign, Care Net wanted to test a relatively new concept known as the downgraded ask. For this experiment, they created a chart of standards based upon the donors’ previous highest contribution where they would ask for a certain number of donors to give a specific amount. For the treatment version of this ask, they asked for a level below what the donor had previously given and added language acknowledging that they were being asked for less.
This was then tested across both emails that were sent out on Giving Tuesday, and results were determined based upon all online giving (excluding recurring gifts) during Giving Tuesday and the day following.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we came close to validating with a 91.2% level of confidence, the number of actual gifts was too small to determine whether this strategy was successful. To validate this concept with the Care Net donor file, we will need to expand the length of this test for several email during a high urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How email copy length affected click-through-rate on an appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening copy for housefile emails will achieve improved click-through-rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
For a membership renewal campaign, we wanted to look at ways to optimize the email copy for The Heritage Foundation appeals. The test is intended to determine whether shorter or lengthier email copy results in higher click-through-rates. The version with the higher click-through-rate sends more users to the landing page linked in the email.
The control email consists of seven short paragraphs while the treatment email consists of five short paragraphs. The sender and subject line are identical for each email, and the “Renew” and “Sign up to renew” links are included in each email.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the experiment resulted in a 7.2% increase in clicks, the level of confidence was only 80%. The treatment (shorter copy) did ultimately generate more clicks than the control (lengthier copy). To further increase the level of confidence, it may be wise to conduct another test in which the shorter copy is paired with a personalized salutation.

experiments.title: How value proposition in a handwritten note about the impact of a higher gift affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the direct impact of a higher gift will increase the average gift and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2021 - 05/09/2021
The Bill of Rights needed to increase its average gift and in turn its revenue from acquisition, that in turn will decrease the net cost per donor. We were looking for ways to increase the average gift without damaging the number of donations. To encourage higher gifts, we devised concise copy that would show the impact and worth of their $50 gift. Then we used a handwritten note to set it apart and catch the reader’s eye. This was run on three donation pages to acquire the needed samples. All of them were acquisition offers for eBooks that were advertised on social. We had to run it for two month, longer than typically advised, to acquire the needed samples.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This tactic worked primarily on desktop and for new visitors. It increased the revenue of new visitors by 55%, with an $11 increase in average gift. However, it decreased revenue for mobile visitors by 35%. Our hypothesis is that the required placement for mobile led to a less desirable user experience. It also slightly decreased revenue for returning visitors, but not at the same rate as it increase gifts for new visitors. The average gift was only $4 lower for returning visitors. It also decreased the number of gifts.

Our hypothesis is that returning visitors who see this more than once are less likely to give, because this message is no longer new, but is instead a turn off. But new visitors, who haven't seen this element before, are intrigued and inspired.

We will roll this treatment out to just new visitors and desktop experience. And we will continue to search for other ways to increase giving for returning and mobile, that also works for new visitors and desktop.

experiments.title: How emotional appeal versus specific outcome affect open rates

rows.name: Don’t lose hope – together we’re making a difference

experiments.researchQuestion: Will emotional language in the subject line prompt more engagement when compared to one that is purely outcome-oriented?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2020 - 08/07/2020
Historically, much of BRI’s subject lines have focused on stating the topic of the email without adding emotional context.
