From experiments with similar organizations, we have found that using emotional language can help prompt the action that we’re striving for (such as opening or clicking on the email.) We wanted to test this same concept for BRI’s audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this situation, we did not see any statistically significant change between the two subject lines. As with most subject line experiments, this same concept will need to be tested over a series of emails to better understand the impact of this hypothesis for the BRI audience.

experiments.title: How the sender’s title impacted open and clickthrough rates on a weekly newsletter
experiments.researchQuestion: How will including the sender’s org/title in the sender name field impact email open, click and unsubscribe rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
As a part of continuous experimentation with increasing engagement from their weekly newsletter, The Fund for American Studies had recently tested into switching senders, but wanted to experiment with whether or not it was important to keep the sender’s title/department as a part of their name in following emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 4 weekly sends were combined together, we could not validate a lift (or decrease) in any of the metrics we were tracking (open rate, clickthrough rate, or unsubscribe rate).
We did see a slight (albeit not valid) lift in the open rate (and each subsequent metric) when we did not use the sender’s org/title within the “sender name” field, but even so, the difference between the control/treatment was so negligible (at/below 3% across all three metrics), it’s safe to assume that it’s of no great consequence (or threat) to use either variation.
Our recommendation is to always err on the side of being a real person speaking to one individual recipient, and therefore would default to not including the org name/title as a part of the sender name (because most people don’t send emails with that included), however, there is no data in this particular study that would substantiate a significant gain in going that route.

experiments.title: How more personal phrasing in a reply email impacts opens

rows.name: Can You Please Answer One Question For Me?
Can You Please Let Me Know If You Want to Come?

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct subject line increase opens and responses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2019 - 11/06/2019
For an upcoming full-day certification workshop, we sent an email asking people to reply with a ‘yes’ or ‘no’ on whether or not they are interested in attending as a way to a) get commitment but also b) get a sense of numbers to plan the event properly. We wanted to see if being more direct in the subject line by telling them they will be asked to answer a question or being a bit more personal and casual subject line that isn’t as direct about a question would increase opens.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically valid, the more personal, casual approach and not letting them know about the question seems to be a better approach but more testing is needed.

experiments.title: How more personal phrasing in a reply email impacts opens

rows.name: Can You Please Answer One Question For Me?
Can You Please Let Me Know If You Want to Come?

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct subject line increase opens and responses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2019 - 11/06/2019
For an upcoming full-day certification workshop, we sent an email asking people to reply with a ‘yes’ or ‘no’ on whether or not they are interested in attending as a way to a) get commitment but also b) get a sense of numbers to plan the event properly. We wanted to see if being more direct in the subject line by telling them they will be asked to answer a question or being a bit more personal and casual subject line that isn’t as direct about a question would increase opens.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically valid, the more personal, casual approach and not letting them know about the question seems to be a better approach but more testing is needed.

experiments.title: How adding a brand name in the sender line affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a brand name to the sender line increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2016 - 03/07/2016
NextAfter sent an email promoting their new eBook “Turning Likes Into Donors” to a rented email list of nonprofit development professionals. The email was sent from Tim Kachuriak, and meant to look like a personal email. However, we wondered — if one of the recipients of the email wasn’t familiar with Tim, would they be likely to open and click the email? We developed a test that added “NextAfter” to the sender line to give the recipient better clarity in regards to the sender and launched an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The added brand name reduced clickthrough rate by 20.7%. This shows that the presence of a brand name actually decreases the likelihood that the user will engage. This may be because the brand is as unknown as the name. This experiment also may suggest that people are more willing to engage with a name they don’t know, but not if it’s evident that they are being marketed to.

experiments.title: How a new value proposition focus in a sales email affects mystery donor audit applications

rows.name: What are your 2019 online fundraising plans?
Here’s a great way to increase giving for 2019 and beyond

experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing the value proposition on the lack of time to plan and research lead to greater applications?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2018 - 12/20/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if a different value proposition approach to a mystery donor audit sales email would lead to greater applications. The control we had tested into already focused on how expert opinions are really just that : opinions. If your fundraising isn’t based on research, data, and testing, then you’re just relying on intuition.
The treatment focused on the lack of time to the necessary research to create a plan for 2019. We could step in with this mystery donor audit and do the leg work for you to help you create a strategy for your next year of fundraising. Both the subject line and body copy of the email were included in the treatment variables.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version saw a 70% decrease in clicks through to the landing page. While we didn’t reach full statistical confidence, we were approaching confidence at 89.5%. While we didn’t have enough applications to validate off of conversions (3 for the control and 1 for the treatment), this decrease in clicks is a strong indicator that focusing on time constraints is not the right value proposition focus.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity in email copy affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will copy with a clearer call-to-action increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/02/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending a mid-month update on their fundraising appeal for the month. The offer was a book by Joni Earackson Tada. The control email highly incentivized the click by asking the reader to “find out why” the sender (Greg Laurie) admires Joni, the author of the book. While this might drive a lot of clicks, we’ve seen this approach drive much lower conversion rates, as those who click through are not as motivated to accept the offer.
We devised a treatment that moved some of that copy into the email, incentivizing the click by giving them a timely call-to-action to find out how to get their own copy of the book before Mother’s Day.
This treatment produced a 58.7% decrease in clickthrough rate, but we wanted to measure conversion on the following donation page to see if those that clicked had a higher motivation to complete the intended transaction.
experiments.keyLearnings: The clearer call-to-action in the email did produce a 55.1% lift in donor conversion. However, because the email drove much less traffic, the control won in terms of overall revenue.

experiments.title: How the friction added with website navigation affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding the standard website header and navigation to the landing page ease the concerns of visitors about the cost of the course while not affecting or increasing conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2015 - 03/17/2015
Hillsdale College offers a free, online training series named Constitution 101.  After initially enrolling, participants are sent to a contextualized donation opportunity where we ask for a gift to help support the efforts of Hillsdale.
There had been complaints recently that the contextualized donation page gave the impression that the course was not free.  We wanted to alleviate those concerns while not reducing the revenue or conversion rate of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of navigation decreased conversion rate by 17%. Whenever we offer donors the opportunity to explore more areas of the website, we are increasing the likelihood that they will.  While we were trying to decrease anxiety, we inadvertently increased the friction associated with making the gift.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of the value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct copy approach that increases clarity improve donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2015 - 12/02/2015
Harvest Ministries was planning to send fundraising emails on Giving Tuesday. They had crafted an email to the housefile that utilized Greg Laurie’s traditional storytelling style, but wanted to see if a more direct approach that educated their donors about Giving Tuesday would increase results. They tested a new subject line, but also new email copy that took a more direct approach to see if increased clarity improved donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This showed that our donors respond financially when we are straightforward and direct. This 60% lift in revenue was a product of a subject line test that slightly increased open rate and a copy test that increased both clickthrough rate and donor motivation, as evidenced by this experiment.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of the value proposition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which of the three value propositions, backed by evidentials, will increase both average gift and conversion rate the greatest amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2014 - 07/20/2014
Despite many experiments, the control version of the main Heritage Foundation donation form had consistently maintained the highest conversion rate. In an effort to influence both conversion rate and average gift, we developed and tested multiple value propositions and backed them with evidentials.
experiments.keyLearnings: The research aspect of Heritage's efforts had the biggest impact on conversion rate. The main donation form will use this language moving forward and this aspect of Heritage's mission will be emphasized in future campaigns when communicating with donors.

experiments.title: How radically simplifying the email format affects engagement and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that radically simplifying email formatting for an owned-audience email housefile will achieve greater immediate and downstream engagement because readers will perceive greater authenticity and trust, since the email appears to have been sent from an individual rather than a list.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2026 - 02/11/2026
Luther Seminary has been sending emails without many design elements to their housefile for years, believing that the more a message looks as if it was sent by an individual, the greater the trust and engagement will be. While their standard email template does not appear to have many design elements, there is still a bit of extra white space and unconventional line spacing that could subconsciously lead a reader to associate the message with marketing.
We wanted to see if fully simplifying email formatting would boost engagement. For the test, we removed all padding. We matched font, size, and color to a typical personal email.
We determined that click-through rate would gauge impact. We also measured offer sign-ups and donations to assess how a front-end change affected downstream behavior.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Top of Funnel: Email Clicks Radically simplifying email formatting for an owned-audience email housefile did not lead to greater immediate engagement. The treatment actually led to a slight -2% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control, though the difference was not statistically significant and therefore unreliable.

Mid Funnel: Offer Sign-Ups. The control email yielded 317 offer sign-ups, and the treatment yielded 316. The radically simplified email format had no impact on downstream offer sign-ups for email subscribers.

Bottom of Funnel: Donations. The treatment email resulted in a 40% increase in donations, a 48% increase in overall revenue, and a 5% increase in average gift, compared to the control, at a 85.8% level of confidence.

Key Takeaway: While the experiment did not yield the top-of-funnel increase in click-through rate as predicted, the impact on downstream donations warrants further testing. The greater authenticity of the radically simplified design could affect donor perceptions of the sender and carry through to important downstream actions. More experiments could demonstrate a clearer link and answer additional questions, such as: How does a radically simplified email format impact engagement in other email types, such as a direct appeal or cultivation message?

experiments.title: Does including an author image improve email performance for donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: If we add an image of a credible Hoover fellow to the email, it will increase engagement and gifts. Recognizable and authoritative figures can increase trust and attention, making readers more likely to engage with the message and take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/13/2026
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy research organization dedicated to advancing scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom. For this test, we wanted to see whether adding an image to an email would increase donor acquisition and engagement when promoting a free ebook. Our expected outcome was that the email with an image would result in higher donor responses and engagement than the version without an image. Everything else in the email remained the same; the only difference between the two versions was the addition of an image of Victor Davis Hanson, a Hoover fellow with strong recognition among Hoover’s audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Control (No Image)
CTR: 2.27%
 	Signups: 239
 	Donations: 31
 	Revenue: $1,525
 	Click to Donate: 9.69%
 	RPM: $108
Treatment (Image Added)
CTR: 2.13%
 	Signups: 236
 	Donations: 40
 	Revenue: $2,807
 	Click to Donate: 13.25%
 	RPM: $197.86
What we observed
CTR and signups were slightly lower with the image.
 	The image version generated:
 	9 more donations
 	Nearly double the revenue
 	Higher click-to-donate rate
Why do we think this happened?
Donors are already familiar with Hoover’s mission and the work it produces. Because of that, when the content reinforces the type of research and scholarship they support, they may be more inclined to give once they reach the page. While the image did not increase clicks, it may have reinforced credibility and relevance, leading to a higher percentage of donors choosing to give.
Next Steps
Test a Hoover or mission-focused image against an author image to see which better reinforces the work's value.
 	Test language that more clearly connects the scholar to Hoover’s mission, such as framing the author as part of Hoover’s broader research and impact.
 	Test reinforcing this connection on the donation page, helping donors see how the content ties directly to the work their support makes possible.

experiments.title: Does including a specific giving handle in an email appeal lead to an increase in donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a specific giving handle for Email 2 recipients will achieve an increase in donor conversion  because  donors are more likely to give when they can quickly see what their generosity will accomplish.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/13/2026 - 01/13/2026
During IMB’s 2026 Winter Olympic campaign, we tested several approaches to increase donation page traffic and, ultimately, drive more gifts.
In Email 2, we tested a specific giving handle paired with a clear impact statement. The email highlighted a $50 gift and tied it directly to providing 50 booklets of the Gospel of John.
The goal of this test was to determine whether IMB’s housefile audience is more motivated to give when a clear, tangible value is attached to a specific donation amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a specific giving handle in Email 2 was directionally strong, resulting in a 50.1% increase in donations across all traffic. While this lift suggests meaningful donor responsiveness to clearly defined, impact-driven gift amounts, the test did not reach statistical validation. The confidence level of 63.0% falls below the standard 95% benchmark, indicating that the result should be interpreted as directional rather than conclusive.
Even without statistical validation, the outcome provides valuable insight into IMB’s housefile audience. The performance suggests that donors may be more motivated to give when a specific dollar amount is paired with a tangible ministry outcome, reinforcing the importance of clarity and impact in fundraising messaging.
Moving forward, continued testing will be essential to more clearly identify what drives donor conversion. Future experiments should be structured to reach a minimum statistical confidence threshold of 95% to ensure learnings can be acted on with greater certainty. Doing so will allow IMB to make more informed, data-backed decisions that strengthen donation page traffic, increase gift conversion, and ultimately grow the number of donors supporting IMB’s mission.

experiments.title: Does emphasizing collective impact strengthen a donor’s sense of shared mission, or does it unintentionally reduce their sense of personal responsibility to give?
experiments.researchQuestion: Including collective impact language in the Fall Share Email will help recipients achieve an increase in engagement and downstream conversion, as donors will be invited to see their gift as part of a larger, shared mission.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/09/2025
In an effort to better understand what motivates Moody Radio’s audience to engage with an email and ultimately give, we tested how emphasizing collective impact influences donor behavior. Specifically, we wanted to see whether highlighting the power of “together” encourages greater involvement—or if it unintentionally reduces a donor’s sense of personal responsibility to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that using collective impact language in Fall Share Email 5 led to a 15.5% increase in click engagement across all traffic. However, the results reached only a 65.0% confidence level, which falls below the standard 95% threshold for statistical significance. As such, these findings should be viewed as directional rather than conclusive.

When examining donor conversion, the results were mixed:
Control: 10 transactions, $462 in revenue
 	Treatment: 7 transactions, $905 in revenue
While the treatment generated
fewer gifts overall
, it produced a
higher average gift size
, suggesting that collective impact framing may increase perceived value or motivation among those who choose to give.
Moving forward, continued testing is recommended to validate these initial trends. Future experiments could include
larger sample sizes
,
alternative expressions of collective impact
, or
different audience segments
to understand better how this message framing influences both engagement and giving behavior. Additionally, evaluating the
context and tone
in which collective impact language appears may help determine how best to align this approach with Moody Radio’s audience motivations and communication strategy.

experiments.title: How merging two appeals together impacted donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that intertwining messaging and value proposition around Dr. Carson’s birthday card and the Little Patriots appeal for ACI Housefile subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it ties the celebration of Dr. Carson directly to advancing the mission of ACI, allowing donors to honor him in a more meaningful and impactful way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2025 - 09/26/2025
During ACI’s Fall High Urgency Campaign, we included both an appeal around Dr. Carson’s birthday and an appeal to expand the Little Patriots curriculum nationwide.
To add value for the donor, we tested a combined version for the ACI housefile, weaving the ask for Little Patriots into Dr. Carson’s birthday card appeal. This allowed us to see whether a unified message could deepen engagement and increase response
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1.The treatment, which intertwined messaging around Dr. Carson's birthday card and the Little Patriots appeal, saw a 5.2% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group.
2. The statistical confidence level for this result was only 24.9%, which is below the 95% threshold for validity.
3. Due to the low statistical confidence level, this experiment's results should not be used for future decision-making.
4. It is essential to ensure that future experiments have a higher level of statistical confidence before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the results. 5. While the initial results show potential for a positive impact on donor conversion by linking Dr. Carson's work with the Little Patriots program, further testing with a larger sample size or different methodology may be necessary to confirm this potential impact.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for recurring donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for recurring donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for recurring donors did not lead to an increase in click-through rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.6% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. The statistical analysis showed a 63.4% level of confidence, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable results and that any conclusions drawn are statistically significant. Additionally, it is crucial to carefully consider the design and implementation of changes such as adding a button, as they may not always have the desired effect. It is also recommended to conduct further testing and analysis to understand the reasons behind the unexpected outcome and to inform future decision-making. Experimentation is a valuable tool for learning and refining strategies, and it is important to approach it with a curious and open mindset to adapt and improve based on the findings.

experiments.title: How adding a signature block with image in a direct appeal to the housefile impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the email signature block for housefile appeal receiver’s will achieve an increase in engagement with the email because it adds a personal touch—allowing readers to put a face to the name and therefore connect more with the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2025 - 08/05/2025
IMB has been working to optimize the email templates used to communicate with their housefile, with the goal of adding more personal touches to make each message feel like it’s coming from an individual rather than the organization as a whole. To support this effort, we tested adding a signature block with the sender’s photo to a direct appeal email. The control group received our standard typed sign-off, while the treatment group received the signature block with the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment group, which received the signature block with the sender's image, experienced a decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 73.4%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered reliable for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it is essential to carefully monitor and analyze the data to determine the impact of any changes made to the email templates. While personalizing emails with a signature block may seem like a good idea intuitively, it is important to test this hypothesis rigorously to validate its effectiveness before implementing it on a larger scale. Further experimentation with different variations of personalization elements in emails could help determine the most effective strategies for increasing engagement with the audience.

experiments.title: How adding a signature block with image in a direct appeal to the housefile impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the email signature block for housefile appeal receivers will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it adds a personal touch—allowing readers to put a face to the name and therefore connect more with the ask.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/13/2025
IMB has been working to optimize the email templates used to communicate with their housefile, with the goal of adding more personal touches to make each message feel like it’s coming from an individual rather than the organization as a whole. To support this effort, we tested adding a signature block with the sender’s photo to a direct appeal email. The control group received our standard typed sign-off, while the treatment group received the signature block with the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

- The inclusion of an email signature block with a sender's photo in direct appeal emails led to a significant increase in donations for all traffic.

- The hypothesis that adding a personal touch can improve donor conversion metrics was supported by the results of the experiment.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 58.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

However, we also looked at revenue by version and saw a 58.3% decrease in revenue (77.3% LOC) with the treatment.

Average gift:
Control: $150
 	Treatment: $ 31.25

experiments.title: How a two column layout impacts downstream donor metrics on a direct ask email to the housefile
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a two column approach for iMB housefile contacts will achieve an increase in downstream donor metrics because it offers an additional CTA to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/29/2025
For IMB’s Ghana Training Direct Ask offer, we tested our standard one-column email format vs a two-column format that featured a couple of calls-to-action.
On desktop, the treatment was featured as two columns at about 70/30 between the main content and the additional CTAs. However, on desktop, the “second” column went below the main content of the email. Therefore, it will be imperative to look at gifts based on device type to fully understand the impact of the treatment variation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the two-column format with additional calls-to-action in the email for IMB's Ghana Training Direct Ask offer resulted in a 60.0% increase in donations for all traffic. This indicates that providing an extra CTA to give can positively impact donor engagement and conversion rates.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 59.5%, which falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, while the experiment showed a positive outcome, the lack of high statistical confidence means that these results should not be solely relied upon for decision making.

These results indicate that having a secondary CTA to give on desktop did in fact increase transactions, but had no impact on mobile.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different layouts and CTAs in emails to optimize donor engagement. It would also be interesting to test our standard layout against a double CTA within a direct ask email to see if it was truly the secondary column or simply the secondary CTA to give.

experiments.title: How including a button in an appeal email affects donor conversion rates for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button that says “Double My Gift” will increase donor conversion rate because it draws more attention than a plain-text hyperlink, creates a clearer and more compelling call to action, and reduces friction—especially for mobile users—by making the next step more obvious and easier to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/31/2025
The Heritage Foundation conducted this experiment as part of their President’s Challenge matching gift campaign. This campaign was designed to drive donor engagement by offering a limited-time opportunity to double the impact of a donor’s gift through a matching challenge. The email in question was the first send in the campaign sequence, making it a high-leverage moment for influencing donor behavior and maximizing initial response rates.
Given the high stakes of the campaign’s early momentum and the importance of clearly communicating the match opportunity, the Heritage Foundation sought to test whether different call-to-action (CTA) formats would affect click-through and conversion rates.
The test was deployed via email, targeting the Heritage Foundation’s non-member list.
Both versions of the email—control and treatment—were linked to the same donation landing page but differed in their CTA presentation.
The experiment was driven by a hypothesis rooted in behavioral and conversion rate optimization principles: a visually prominent, action-oriented button might outperform a plain-text hyperlink in driving user action.
Key motivations included:
Improving clarity and visibility of the CTA: While the raw URL in the control version is technically functional, it lacks the immediate visual weight and action language of a button. The team suspected that this might cause the CTA to be overlooked or feel less urgent.
Reducing cognitive friction: A clear, concise button like “Double My Gift” directly mirrors the donor’s intent and may simplify decision-making.
Encouraging mobile-friendly design: Buttons often perform better on mobile devices where touch interaction is key, and plain-text links can be harder to tap.
This test is a good example of using micro-optimizations in email fundraising design to explore how seemingly small changes can have an outsized effect on user behavior and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button with a clear call to action like “Double My Gift” can potentially increase donor conversion rates compared to a plain-text hyperlink. While the treatment did show a 0.6% increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence was only 1.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for significance.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of small design changes such as CTA formats on user behavior and conversion rates. However, it is crucial to ensure that the statistical significance of the results is high enough to draw valid conclusions. Therefore, when conducting similar tests in the future, it is recommended to continue testing different CTA formats while ensuring a larger sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence before making any definitive decisions based on the results. Additionally, considering other factors such as audience segmentation, messaging variation, and testing on different platforms can further enhance the reliability and applicability of the experiment's findings.

experiments.title: How a plain text appeal email affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending out a plain text email that looks similar to emails in someone’s inbox for the housefile will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it would look more humanized as it would look like someone you know is sending you an email, encouraging the user to read through the email and donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/14/2025
Time of Grace sends out appeal emails every month for their new premium. They send out stylized emails with organization branding. For this test, we wanted to see how a plain text email that you see for normal emails in your inbox would affect donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that sending out a plain text email for donation appeals resulted in a 33.8% increase in donations for all traffic. This indicates that the more personal and familiar format of the plain text email may have resonated better with recipients, potentially leading to higher engagement and conversion rates.

However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 55.6%, below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity. This means that while the treatment showed a positive result, it cannot be considered conclusive or reliable for future decision-making.

We saw an 18% increase in clicks with a 67% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test the effectiveness of plain text emails for donation appeals with a larger sample size or different variations to see if the results can be replicated and validated with a higher level of confidence. Additionally, incorporating elements of personalization and humanization in email communications could continue to be explored as a strategy for increasing donor engagement and conversions.

experiments.title: How adding a quote in email copy impacts email acquisition and donor conversion with a donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a quote from Victor Davis Hanson to the body of the email for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition and donor conversion. because the Hoover audience is likely familiar with and influenced by Victor Davis Hanson’s thought leadership. Seeing a direct quote may deepen their connection, reinforce credibility, and drive more meaningful engagement.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. This specific experiment was targeting their donor audience with an email promoting Victor Davis Hanson’s latest eBook: Ten Paradoxes of Our Age. Given this audience’s familiarity and affinity with VDH’s work, the goal was to explore whether featuring a direct quote from Hanson in the email body would increase engagement—particularly email acquisition and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Below you can find the impact on donor conversion:

Control: 65 donations

Treatment: 56 donations

 

Here are our takeaways:
Familiarity Doesn’t Always Translate to Action - Despite the donor audience’s familiarity with Victor Davis Hanson, simply adding a quote—without new content framing or emphasis—did not improve performance. This suggests that passive recognition is not always a strong enough motivator to change behavior.
 	Subtle Changes Can Sometimes Dilute Impact - The quote was added near the end of the email, without prominent placement or context. It’s possible that the quote blended into the copy too subtly, failing to act as a clear call to action or point of differentiation. Strategic placement and visual emphasis may be critical when incorporating recognizable names or thought leaders.
 	Performance Declines Were Modest but Directional - While the treatment underperformed slightly in both acquisition and donations, the declines were not statistically significant. However, the downward trend is worth noting. It reinforces the idea that every added element must serve a clear persuasive function—especially when engaging a high-performing audience like Hoover’s donors.
• Test Placement and Framing:
• Segment Further by VDH Engagement History:
• Use Quotes in Multichannel Reinforcement:
• Explore Narrative over Quotation:

experiments.title: How an autoresponder sent from a person versus the organization impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the autoresponder coming from a person for Grace Upon Grace eBook requesters will achieve an increase in clicks because people are more likely to engage and trust personalized, human communication.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2025 - 07/09/2025
For Moody Radio, we wanted to test how an autoresponder sent from a notable person within the organization versus from the organization directly would impact email engagement.
This test ran for a little over a month with traffic being split equally between the control and treatment versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the autoresponder sent from a notable person within the organization resulted in a 2.1% decrease in clicks compared to the autoresponder sent from the organization directly. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 25.9%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not conclusive and should not be used for future decision-making.

While we know that opens are no longer reliable, we did pull the results of both opens and click-to-open rates to understand the range of where each email performed.

Control: 65.48% open rate, 64.93% click to open rate

Treatment: 61.23% open rate, 67.96% click to open rate

These results suggest that the treatment email, coming from Wes Ward, resulted in a slightly lower open rate of the email, but led to a slightly higher click-to-open rate.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, considering factors such as the timing of the emails, the messaging, and the audience segmentation could help to identify what truly drives engagement and clicks. It is also important to test different variables in future experiments to gain a better understanding of what resonates with the audience and drives the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How adding a soft donation ask to an email about a survey impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a soft ask for contacts that received the FYE survey email will achieve more donations.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/08/2025
DTS was sending their annual FYE Survey before launching their FYE campaign. We decided to test how adding a line in the email that said “After completing the survey, you’ll also have the opportunity to deepen your partnership with DTS if you choose.” would impact donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a soft ask for donations in the email sent to contacts receiving the FYE survey resulted in a significant increase in donations. The treatment group, which included the line about deepening the partnership with DTS, saw a 95.3% increase in donations compared to the control group.

However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 73.8%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing the impact of including a donation ask in communications with contacts. By conducting further tests with larger sample sizes and ensuring a higher level of statistical significance, we can determine more accurately if this strategy is effective in driving donations for Dallas Theological Seminary.

experiments.title: Does adding an image of a direct mail envelope impact donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an envelope image for direct mail donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
In March, the direct mail team wanted to test sending a new design of an envelope to donors as a remail strategy. We wanted to target this list with a digital test that looked at whether including the picture of the envelope in the email led to higher conversion rates.
Our hypothesis was that because the envelope was so different from what PTV usually uses, and could easily get lost in the mix, adding an image to the email would drive higher response rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image of the envelope to the email for direct mail donors did not lead to higher conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a 29.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 73.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It is also important to note that the true impact of this test will be whether it influenced offline giving at all; however, direct mail results won't be available for another month, so we are only reporting on instant digital transactions here.

This experiment also had a similar result when looking at it from a revenue perspective. The treatment version had 38% less revenue per visitor than the control, with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How introducing a new email sender in the beginning of an email appeal affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a new sender at the beginning of an email appeal for email subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will create a sense of novelty and engagement, capturing the recipient’s attention more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/26/2025
This experiment was conducted as part of The Navigators’ kickoff email for their Sustainers Campaign, which aimed to acquire new recurring donors.
The test was designed to evaluate whether introducing a new sender at the beginning of the email appeal would increase donor conversion rates. Given that email engagement plays a crucial role in fundraising success, we wanted to explore whether a fresh or recognizable sender could capture attention, build trust, and drive more donations.
The decision to run this experiment was driven by the need to optimize email performance and maximize donor acquisition at the start of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. **Introducing a new sender at the beginning of an email appeal can lead to an increase in donor conversion rates**: The treatment, which featured a new sender introducing themselves at the start of the email, resulted in a 41.8% increase in donations. This suggests that changing up the sender in email appeals can have a positive impact on donor engagement.

2. **Confidence in the results**: While the increase in donations was significant, the statistical confidence level was only 64.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered completely reliable or valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. **Continue testing with different sender introductions**: The positive impact of introducing a new sender indicates that further experimentation with different sender personas, introductions, and stories could continue to be beneficial. Testing a variety of approaches can help identify the most effective ways to connect with donors and drive donations.

2. **Increase sample size for higher confidence**: To ensure more reliable results in future experiments, it may be necessary to increase the sample size. This can help boost the statistical confidence level and provide clearer insights into the effectiveness of different email strategies.

3. **Iterate and refine based on learnings**: While the results of this experiment may not be conclusive, the learnings can still be valuable in shaping future email campaigns. Take the insights gained from this experiment, such as the impact of a new sender introduction, and incorporate them into future iterations of email appeals to continue optimizing donor engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and relevancy on the email copy on a recirculation email for nondonors impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and relevancy for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in donations because a direct, credible, and more streamlined approach will cause people to be more engaged .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. Hoover recirculates their content offers by sending out an email to their Donors and Nondonors every Thursday offering a free eBook or course. On this Thursday, Hoover sent out the offer, “Leaving Socialism”. This specific email test was to their nondonor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity and relevancy for Hoover's donor audience did not achieve the desired increase in donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only at 14.4%, which means that these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.
We did see that in the control the average gift was $48.53 and the treatment was $37.19.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. It is also crucial to carefully design the messaging and content of the email offers to resonate with the donor audience.

experiments.title: Does a more personal subject line increase engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal subject line for email subscribers will achieve a higher email engagement because they are more likely to open and engage.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
We are seeing a decrease in email engagement so we decided to do some testing within the subject line to increase opens, clicks and hopefully donations.
For this round we decided to test a very personal approach by using the First Name and asking a question
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that a personalized subject line using the subscriber's first name and asking a question can potentially lead to a slight increase in email engagement, specifically in the form of clicks. However, the statistical significance level of 16.5% is too low to draw any definitive conclusions from these results. Therefore, it is not advisable to make decisions based on these findings for future experimentation.

We know that Opens are no longer a reliable metric due to iOS updates.

But for donations the control saw a 12% decrease with only 40% level of confidence of the treatment.

experiments.title: How scarcity affects donor conversion rate during digital kick off campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing scarcity in the email will increase the donor conversion rate because framing the opportunity as rare and time-sensitive leverages the psychological principle of urgency and exclusivity, motivating recipients to act quickly to avoid missing out.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2025
The membership team tested language that focused on scarcity. Copy used words like “rare and fleeting” and “a unique chance” to evoke a feeling that the opportunity to donate would slip pass them if they didn’t act now.
Results varied between segments. Members saw an 8% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 40-50% LOC, but prospects saw a 16% lift in conversion rate with a 78% LOC.
Putting the results together the added copy did not seem to make difference. The test ran during a mid campaign deadline, which makes testing difficult because the donor intent to give is much higher during deadlines.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that emphasizing scarcity in the email did not have a significant impact on donor conversion rates. While the treatment did see a slight increase in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was below the threshold of 85%, making the results inconclusive and not valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the context in which the test is being conducted. In this case, the mid-campaign deadline likely affected donor behavior, making it difficult to isolate the impact of the scarcity messaging. It may be beneficial to run similar tests outside of peak donation periods to get a clearer understanding of the impact of scarcity messaging on donor conversion rates.

Additionally, it is important to continue testing different messaging strategies and elements to determine what resonates best with donors. While scarcity messaging may not have had a significant impact in this particular experiment, it is still valuable to explore other tactics that may help increase donor conversion rates.

Overall, the key takeaway is that while scarcity messaging did not show a significant impact in this experiment, continued testing and experimentation are essential to optimize messaging strategies for increased donor engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How leading with supporting the mission over the book impacted donations for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting giving to the ministry for nondonors will achieve a higher impact in donations because the prospective donor can know what their money is going toward and what they will get out of it.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For the PTV Feb 2025 offer, we decided to test a “give then get” strategy. Where we emphasize more toward giving toward the mission and then receiving the book.
In previous strategies, we would focus primarily on the value of the book and giving to the ministry was a more secondary response.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting giving to the ministry for non-donors resulted in a 39.6% decrease in donations for all traffic. Although this result did not reach a 95% statistical confidence level, it did show a significant impact on donation behavior.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the balance between emphasizing giving to the ministry and the value of the offer. It may be beneficial to continue testing this "give then get" strategy with variations in messaging and targeting to determine the most effective approach. Additionally, conducting further experiments with larger sample sizes or different audience segments could help confirm the impact of highlighting giving to the ministry on donation behavior.

Overall, the results of this experiment indicate the importance of carefully testing and refining strategies to understand their impact on donor behavior and optimize fundraising efforts for Pathway to Victory.

The nondonor segment saw an increase from this strategy https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/9216

experiments.title: How a consistent CTA throughout the donor funnel impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a consistent CTA for the housefile during a high-urgency appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it helps reinforce one clear CTA with consistency, rather than mixing calls to action. because it helps reinforce one clear CTA with consistency, rather than mixing calls to action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we hypothesized that a consistent CTA from the email to donation page copy and final “donate/give” button would help reinforce the desired action and increased donor conversion rate. In the control, the ask was presented with various ways of phrasing the call to action: “make a gift” “donate” “give” “match your donation” etc. In the treatment, we used “donate” consistently as the CTA in email copy and donation page copy while also changing the give button to “Donate.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a consistent call-to-action (CTA) does not always lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this case, using "donate" consistently as the CTA in both the email and donation page did not result in a higher number of donations compared to using various CTAs like "make a gift" or "match your donation."

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, as the confidence level is below 95%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be worthwhile to test these in other funnels – besides just email (consistent CTA from a Sticky Bar or Facebook ad for example), in addition to swapping the consistency for a different CTA (using Give throughout rather than Donate throughout)

experiments.title: How an identity call to action in email, emphasizing “become a donor,” impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an identity CTA for non-donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it helps emphasize the user becoming a donor, speaking to their identity/what they’re joining, versus the action CTA of “make a gift”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we hypothesized that using an identity CTA for non-donors, emphasizing “become a donor” rather than just “make a gift” will increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The hypothesis that using an identity CTA for non-donors emphasizing "become a donor" would increase donor conversion rate was not supported by the results.

2. The treatment group, which used the identity CTA, actually saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

3. The statistical confidence level of 66.4% is below the standard threshold of 95%, meaning that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It is possible that for non-donors, the identity CTA framed the ask in a way that felt too big. As someone whose never given, "become a donor" may seem more intimidating than "make a gift" having a reverse effect given our hypothesis. It would be worthwhile to re-test this experience for higher validity during a period outside of the increased urgency.

experiments.title: How impacted gift array links in email impacts donor conversion during a high urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and overall revenue because it eliminates friction, pre-filling the selected amount on the donation page, while also reinforcing that their gift is being matched in the copy.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday campaign, we hypothesized that using impacted gift array links to drive urgency and eliminate friction during the 2-day match would increase donor conversion rate and overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign led to a decrease in donations for all traffic. Additionally, the treatment saw a dropoff in average gift with $146 in the control versus $65 in the treatment.

Ultimately, the level of confidence was 78.9%, meaning these results are not valid for future decision-making. It would also be worth re-testing this experience for validity outside the window of a high-urgency appeal. Potentially, the treatment may have resulted in too much decision friction at the top of the funnel, whereas the links would have proved more effective simply by increasing clicks and greater traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: Does mentioning the reach potential of a recurring gift impact donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that mentioning the reach value for email subscribers will achieve a increase in donor conversion because people will get a tangible impact of their gift and know the number of lives they are impacting each month.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For PTV’s January recruitment of Pathway Partners, PTV’s recurring donor program, we decided to test mentioning the reach value of a monthly gift for a year.
We calculated, based on Facebook CPM rates for 2024, that it costs PTV approximately $25 to reach 1,000 people. Therefore over the span of 12 months, a recurring gift of $25 could reach 12,000 new people for PTV’s mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that mentioning the reach value of a monthly gift can lead to a significant increase in donations for Pathway to Victory. The treatment version, which included information about how a monthly gift of $25 could reach 12,000 new people over the span of a year, saw a 61.2% increase in donations for All Traffic compared to the control version. While the statistical level of confidence was only 52.2%, indicating that these results may not be entirely reliable, the overall trend suggests that highlighting the impact of a donation can resonate with donors and encourage them to give.

For revenue, while $25/month was the amount mentioned in the email. The treatment saw a 43% increase with a 70% level of confidence. This is primarily due to a $50 gift given to the treatment version. Implying that this donor wanted to reach twice as many people mentioned in the email.

experiments.title: How focusing on the impact of a donor’s gift affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on the impact of the donor’s gift rather than mentioning the total financial goal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it emphasizes the immediate and tangible outcomes of their support, making the decision to give feel more personal and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
This experiment was conducted as part of The Navigators’ calendar year-end fundraising campaign. The experiment was conducted through an email campaign sent to The Navigators’ donor base. It involved A/B testing, where the donor audience was split into two groups: one receiving the Control email and the other receiving the Treatment email.
The experiment aimed to refine messaging by focusing on tangible outcomes that resonate with The Navigators’ mission-driven donor base. Highlighting direct impact was expected to feel more personal and actionable, avoiding the discouragement large financial goals can create, and ultimately improving conversion rates.
The goal was to test if shifting focus from organizational goals to donor impact would improve donor engagement and conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that focusing on the impact of the donor's gift rather than mentioning the total financial goal can result in an increase in click-through rate, as evidenced by a 19.3% increase in clicks for all traffic in the treatment group. This indicates that emphasizing immediate and tangible outcomes of support can make the decision to give feel more personal and actionable for donors. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the 85% threshold, meaning these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Additionally, the treatment group saw am invalid 16% decrease in donor conversion rate with only a 44% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing messaging that highlights the impact of donor support in a personalized and actionable way. By refining messaging to focus on tangible outcomes, we have the potential to improve donor engagement and conversion rates. However, it is essential to ensure that results reach a statistically significant level of confidence before implementing any changes based on experimentation. Additionally, exploring different ways to communicate impact and value to donors can further optimize messaging strategies for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a signer’s image in an email footer signature in a high urgency appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including Chitwood’s image in the email footer for CYE Email 12 will achieve an increase in clicks because the image will enhance the personal connection between the organization and the reader and humanize the message. because  the image will enhance the personal connection between the organization and the reader and humanize the message.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
In an effort to drive higher email engagement during IMB’s CYE campaign, we conducted a test incorporating an image of Paul Chitwood, IMB’s President, in the email sign-off. The hypothesis was that featuring Chitwood’s image would enhance the personal connection with readers, allowing them to associate a face with the name and fostering a stronger sense of trust and rapport. This, in turn, was expected to increase the likelihood of readers making a donation.
We implemented this test in Email 12 of the CYE campaign, with 50% of the traffic receiving the version featuring Chitwood’s image to assess its impact on engagement and conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including Paul Chitwood's image in the email footer for CYE Email 12 did not result in the expected increase in clicks. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 3.8% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 64.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%.
Based on these results, it is clear that the impact of including Chitwood's image on email engagement is inconclusive. Therefore, it is not advisable to make any definitive conclusions or decisions based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size and ensure that the results reach a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing any changes to the email strategy.
Furthermore, we saw a 24.5% decrease in transactions at a 61.4% LOC. While this metric did not reach 95% validity, it does suggest that the presence of Chitwood's image reduced the motivation to give once those who clicked landed on the donation page.
Finally, it may be beneficial to explore other ways to enhance personal connections and trust with readers, such as through compelling storytelling, personalized content, or highlighting the impact of donations. By continuously testing and refining different strategies, IMB can better understand what resonates with their audience and optimize their email campaigns for improved engagement and conversions.

experiments.title: How highlighting the impact of a gift impacts click rate in a high urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the impact of the donor’s gift for Email 5 recipients will achieve an increase in the click through rate because it allows the donor to connect their action directly to meaningful outcomes.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For The Navigators CYE campaign, we tested into highlighting the impact of a donor’s gift within the email copy.
We tested this concept on Email 5 with 50% of email recipient’s getting the treatment version.
Our hypothesis was that doing so would connect their action (the gift) directly to meaningful outcomes, such as discipling young believers and strengthening faith communities. We thought the emotional appeal would be likely to resonate with recipients, inspiring a sense of purpose and urgency to give, compared to the practical framing of simply checking off a to-do list.
With this change, we expected to see an increase in the click through rate and as a result an increase in donor conversion metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting the impact of the donor's gift in the email copy for The Navigators' CYE campaign did not result in the expected increase in click-through rate. In fact, the treatment version saw a 10.7% decrease in clicks compared to the control version at a 59.6% LOC.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, as the confidence level is below 95%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

Furthermore, we wanted to know whether this language produced more motivated clicks. The control produced 25 donations out of the 107 clicks and the treatment produced 19 donations out of the 115 clicks. Therefore, the treatment resulted in a 29.3% decrease in transactions at a 79.8% LOC. While again, these results are not valid, they do suggest that this type of language reduces the motivation to give for The Navigators audience.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to continue testing different messaging and approaches to determine what resonates best with donors. It may be worth exploring a combination of emotional and practical appeals to see what drives engagement and donations. Additionally, conducting experiments with a larger sample size and ensuring a statistically significant confidence level will provide more reliable results to inform future campaign strategies.

experiments.title: Will adding a humanized touch to an appeal increase conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making a humanized touch for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion because having the ask come from a specific person increases trust, and makes the ask a little more personal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/15/2025
For the CYE homepage sticky bar, we wanted to see if adding an image of Roland on the ask and positioning it to be from Roland will convert donors better, rather than having just a regular ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

- Adding an image of Roland on the homepage sticky bar increased donations by 24.0%.

- The statistical level of confidence of 71.8% is below the recommended 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid.

- The hypothesis that a humanized touch would increase donor conversion was supported by the results.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

- Consider factors that may have influenced the lower statistical confidence, such as sample size or variability.

- Repeat the experiment with adjustments to ensure that the results are reliable and can be used for decision making.

- Continue exploring the idea of a humanized touch in donation asks, as it showed potential for increasing conversions.

experiments.title: How using a gift array based on HPC impacted email donations during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a gift array based on highest previous contribution for donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. During their year-end campaign, we wanted to see whether basing a gift array on a donor’s highest previous contribution made an impact on conversion rates.
The control version had a standard gift array of $100/250/500/Other.
The treatment based the gift array on HPC, and included 1.5X HPC, 2X HPC, 2.5X HPC, and other.
This experiment was run across 3 of the last emails of the year (12/27, 12/30, and 12/31) to active and lapsed donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a gift array based on a donor's highest previous contribution can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment group in this experiment showed a 23.8% increase in donations compared to the control group, indicating that personalizing the gift array based on donor behavior may impact conversion rates positively.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was 78.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with a larger sample size to ensure more statistically significant results. It would also be valuable to explore other factors that could influence donation behavior, such as the timing of the donation appeal, messaging, or donor segmentation.

Overall, while this experiment showed a potential impact of personalized gift arrays on donation rates, further testing is needed to confirm these findings and make informed decisions for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How using a donor “statement” for lapsed donors impacted donor conversions rates during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donor statement for lapsed donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2024 - 12/31/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
This year during their year-end campaign, Avila was promoting their largest-ever match, with a triple match as well. We wanted to see whether using a donor ‘statement’ at the top of emails to remind lapsed donors of their last gift date and amount would impact donor conversion rates. We ran this copy test on their active donor audience, through a total of 8 emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved using a donor statement for lapsed donors, resulted in a 16.8% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 46.6%, meaning that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision-making.

3. Despite the increase in donations, the lack of statistical significance indicates that the effect may not be reliably replicable.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Ensure that the sample size is sufficient to detect statistically significant differences.

2. Consider running the experiment for a longer duration to capture more data and increase the confidence level of the results.

3. Test the impact of donor statements on different donor segments to see if the effect varies among different groups.

4. Analyze additional metrics such as open rates, click-through rates, and conversion rates to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior and preferences.

5. Consider testing other variables such as messaging, imagery, or incentives to identify factors that truly drive donor conversion rates.

Overall, while the experiment showed a positive trend in donations with the use of donor statements for lapsed donors, it is essential to gather more robust data to make informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How including compelling images in a high urgency appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including compelling images in a high urgency appeal for CYE Email 14 will achieve an increase in clicks because the presence of the images allows the reader to visually connect withe the mission of the IMB.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
To increase email click rates during IMB’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding compelling images to Email 14. The treatment was presented to 50% of the recipients of this email.
Our hypothesis was that incorporating these visuals would help readers establish a stronger emotional connection with IMB’s mission, thereby increasing the likelihood of clicking the donation link.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including compelling images in high urgency appeal emails may not necessarily lead to an increase in clicks for donation links. The treatment group, which received emails with images, actually saw a 2.9% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 51.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it would be important to thoroughly analyze the data and ensure that the findings are statistically significant before drawing any conclusions. It may also be beneficial to test different types of images or messaging to see what truly resonates with recipients and drives them to click on donation links.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomy language during a year-end campaign impacts email revenue.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using donor autonomy language for active donors will achieve a change in email donation conversions..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2024 - 12/31/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
This year during their year-end campaign, Avila was promoting their largest-ever match, with a triple match as well. We wanted to see whether using donor autonomy language–allowing the donor to make a choice to give–throughout the entire campaign would make a difference. We ran this copy test on their active donor audience, through a total of 8 emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using donor autonomy language for active donors may have a positive impact on donation conversions, as evidenced by the 4.3% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 21.9%, which is below the threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure a larger sample size to improve the statistical significance of the results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other factors that may influence donation conversions among active donors, such as the timing of the campaign, the messaging used, or the specific calls to action.

Overall, while the initial findings suggest a potential benefit of using donor autonomy language, further research is needed to confirm these results and make informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page impacts revenue during a holiday appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that campaign-related copy for users on the Tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the value prop is expanded upon, greater urgency is tied to the campaign, and the donor has a sense of how their gift fits into the December goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, the Tribute Donation page largely remains the same. Aside from brief mentions of the match, the copy is stripped down, short, and doesn’t tie into the larger goals of the organization – instead focusing more on a donor’s gift to the individual. We believed that by using the campaign donation page on Tribute, tying to the larger December goals and reinforcing the match more, it would increase average gift and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page led to a 3.6% increase in revenue for all traffic as well as a slight bump in donations overall. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.7%, below the 85% threshold for validity.

However, it is an encouraging first step to continue testing on the Tribute donation page, which historically, has been treated very separate from the rest of CaringBridge's campaign and goals – because users are there for a specific user. To have this broadened and more general appeal copy not result in a decrease, but also perhaps have a slight positive increase, could open up door for continued testing.

experiments.title: How a personal ask to match a gift amount affects overall conversions in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal ask to match a gift for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because because it creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, especially when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
American Cornerstone Institute wanted to test how a more personal approach to asking to match a gift during year-end would affect overall conversion rates including clickthrough rate from the email to donation page and donation conversion rate.
They tested this concept within three emails they sent throughout their year-end campaign. This write-up shows the results of email 7 which was an appeal for education.
