The control email presented the matching gift by stating that their gift would be doubled thanks to their year-end matching gift challenge up to $100,000. The treatment email presented the matching gift by stating that a generous donor wants to personally match their gift allowing them to have double the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a more personal approach to asking for a matching gift can potentially lead to an increase in conversion rates. The treatment email, which presented the matching gift as being from a generous donor wanting to personally match the contribution, resulted in a 33.7% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control email.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was 77.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

This suggests that creating a sense of urgency and personal connection in donation appeals can motivate potential donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, particularly when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.

As for the effect on email click-through rate, the treatment email saw no difference in click-through rate. There was a 1.9% increase in clicks with only a 21% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of a personal ask for matching gifts with a larger sample size to see if the trend towards increased conversion rates holds true. Additionally, considering other variables such as the timing of the ask, the messaging used, and the target audience could provide further insights into what drives donor behavior and how to optimize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How sharing the impact of a gift to the mission of the organization impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by expounding upon the mission of PTV for email subscribers will achieve a higher click rate because there is greater correlation between their support and where it is going.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV emails Pastors Pen each month, a piece of encouraging content from Dr. Jeffress.
For this one, we decided to test the PS messaging.
The control thanks the recipient for supporting PTV, the treatment we included how financial support makes encouragement like Pastors Pen available to hundreds of thousands of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment version of the email, which emphasized the impact of financial support on reaching hundreds of thousands of people with encouraging content, resulted in an 18.2% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version.

2. Despite the increase in clicks, the statistical level of confidence of 63.6% is below the accepted threshold of 95%, rendering the results inconclusive and not suitable for future decision-making.

3. The hypothesis that expounding upon the mission of PTV for email subscribers would achieve a higher click rate due to a greater correlation between their support and its impact was partially supported by the results.

4. Future experimentation should focus on refining the messaging and testing it with a larger sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence for conclusive results.

5. It is important to continue experimenting with different messaging strategies to effectively engage email subscribers and drive desired actions without solely relying on the results of this inconclusive experiment.

experiments.title: How including the reach of $20 in an email to existing donors impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including a statement of reach for existing donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor can be confident of how their gift will be used and the impact. because the donor can be confident of how their gift will be used and the impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For PTV’s final email of 2024, we decided to test adding in a how far a gift of $20 would go toward growing the reach of the ministry.
Our hypothesis is that by including this information, it would add confidence to the donor of how their gift would be used and the impact of it.
We calculated the reach by looking at the CPM for paid media channels, specifically Facebook as that is where PTV spends most of its acquisition media budget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on including a statement of reach for existing donors in the email were as follows:

1. The treatment group, which included information on how far a gift of $20 would go toward growing the reach of the ministry, saw a 4.6% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 12.7%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

3. While the treatment group showed a positive impact on donations, the results cannot be considered valid or reliable due to the low statistical confidence level.

4. Future experimentation should focus on increasing the sample size or making adjustments to the experiment design to ensure results are statistically significant and reliable before making decisions based on them.

5. The hypothesis that including information on how the donation would be used could increase donor confidence and donations needs to be further tested with a more robust experimental design to draw concrete conclusions.

experiments.title: How including the reach of $20 in an email to nondonors impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including a statement of reach for nondonors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor can be confident of how their gift will be used and the impact..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For PTV’s final email of 2024, we decided to test adding in a how far a gift of $20 would go toward growing the reach of the ministry.
Our hypothesis is that by including this information, it would add confidence to the potential donor of how their gift would be used and the impact of it.
We calculated the reach by looking at the CPM for paid media channels, specifically Facebook as that is where PTV spends most of its acquisition media budget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The inclusion of information on how far a gift of $20 would go toward growing the reach of the ministry led to a 21.5% increase in donations.

2. While the increase in donations is encouraging, the statistical level of confidence was only 58.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for validity.

3. This suggests that the results should not be used for future decision making, as the increase in donations may not be solely attributed to the inclusion of the reach information.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Consider increasing the sample size to improve the statistical significance of the results.

2. Continue testing the impact of including information on how donations are used, but also explore other variables that may influence donor behavior.

3. Analyze the data and results carefully to ensure that any conclusions drawn are based on valid and reliable statistical evidence.

4. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future testing strategies and make data-driven decisions to optimize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How adding transparency about the allocation of a donor’s gift affects conversion rates in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding transparency about where the donor’s gift is allocated will increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by clearly showing how their generosity directly supports missionaries and Kingdom work.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/16/2024
IMB tested how adding transparency about the allocation of the donor’s gift would affect the donation conversion rate on both of their Giving Tuesday appeals. We kept the control and treatment audiences consistent throughout both emails. The note of the allocation was added to the treatment emails as well as the donation page for those who received the treatment email.
This experiment saw a 43.9% lift in donor conversion rate with a 90% LoC.
Because it did not validate, they wanted to test it again on one of their CYE email appeals. The set-up was the exact same as the GT appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Adding transparency about the allocation of a donor's gift can have a significant positive impact on the conversion rate.
2. The hypothesis that transparency builds trust and confidence in the organization, leading to increased donations, was supported by the results.
3. Despite the treatment showing a substantial increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence fell below the threshold of 85%, making the results inconclusive.
4. These results should not be used for future decision-making due to the lack of statistical validity.
In future experimentation, it is important to:
1. Continue testing the impact of adding transparency about allocation on donation conversion rates to validate the findings.
2. Ensure that experiments are properly powered and have a sufficient sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence.
3. Consider other factors that may influence donation behavior to develop a more comprehensive understanding of donor motivations.
4. Use the learnings from this experiment as a basis for formulating new hypotheses and designing future experiments to optimize donation conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: How listing out bundles in bullet points inpact conversion rate for existing donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that listing out items in a bullet list for existing donor subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it is easier to read and to see the value in each bundle.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
During late November and December, PTV promotes their brand-new Daily Devotional bundles. The bundles vary in the number of devotionals and if it is paired with additional items.
For this test we decided to test listing the bundles as a paragraph versus a bullet point list.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that listing out items in a paragraph form for Daily Devotional bundles resulted in a decrease in donations compared to listing them in a bullet point list. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 36.3%, which is below the 85% threshold for valid results. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different formats for presenting information to determine which method resonates best with donors. Additionally, ensuring a higher statistical level of confidence is crucial in drawing reliable conclusions from A/B testing results.

experiments.title: How listing out bundles in bullet points inpact conversion rate for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that listing out items in a bullet list for nondonor subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it is easier to read and to see the value in each bundle. because it is easier to read and to see the value in each bundle.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
During late November and December, PTV promotes their brand-new Daily Devotional bundles. The bundles vary in the number of devotionals and if it is paired with additional items.
For this test we decided to test listing the bundles as a paragraph versus a bullet point list.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted to determine the effectiveness of listing out items in a bullet list for non-donor subscribers in promoting the Daily Devotional bundles for Pathway to Victory showed a 60.0% decrease in donations for all traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 74.3%. While the control group performed better in this instance, the results are not statistically significant at a level of 85% confidence, and therefore cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

Key learnings from this experiment include the importance of ensuring statistical significance in A/B testing results before drawing conclusions or implementing changes based on them. In future experimentation, it is crucial to gather a larger sample size or consider other factors that may have impacted the outcome to ensure that conclusions drawn from the experiment are reliable and can be used to inform decision-making. Further testing may be warranted to determine the most effective way to present information about the Daily Devotional bundles to non-donor subscribers for optimal donor conversion rates.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by expounding upon the mission of PTV for email subscribers will achieve a higher click rate because there is greater correlation between their support and where it is going.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV emails Pastors Pen each month, a piece of encouraging content from Dr. Jeffress.
For this one, we decided to test the PS messaging.
The control thanks the recipient for supporting PTV, the treatment we included how financial support makes encouragement like Pastors Pen available to hundreds of thousands of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment version of the email, which emphasized the impact of financial support on reaching hundreds of thousands of people with encouraging content, resulted in an 18.2% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version.

2. Despite the increase in clicks, the statistical level of confidence of 63.6% is below the accepted threshold of 85%, rendering the results inconclusive and not suitable for future decision-making.

3. The hypothesis that expounding upon the mission of PTV for email subscribers would achieve a higher click rate due to a greater correlation between their support and its impact was partially supported by the results.

4. Future experimentation should focus on refining the messaging and testing it with a larger sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence for conclusive results.

experiments.title: How shortening email copy for a content offer impacts donor signups and donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening copy for active donors will achieve a change in content offer KPIs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2024 - 09/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation, we wanted to test whether shorter, more-to-the-point email copy impacted clicks and email signups, as well as donation conversions.
This experiment result is for active donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that shortening the email copy for active donors resulted in a 6.3% decrease in clicks for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Moreover, when looking at downstream metrics:
The treatment version had a 13% decrease in email sign ups for the content offer, with an 80% level of confidence.
 	There was no difference at all when looking at donation conversions after the sign-up compared to total sent, but of those that signed up and THEN donated, the treatment had a 16% increase in donation conversions with only a 36% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Does adding a sample of a daily devotional in the body of an email impact donations for an active donor audience?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a devotional sample for active donors will achieve a change in donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2024 - 12/13/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
One of their most popular offers every year is a 265-page daily devotional that is sent to the donor as a thank you for their donation of any amount.
For this experiment, which was run on their housefile and targeted a donor audience, we wanted to see whether adding a sample passage from the devotional made an impact on donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a devotional sample for active donors led to a decrease in donations for all traffic by 8.9%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 54.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable and statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further testing with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the experimental design to increase the likelihood of obtaining valid results. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the outcome of the experiment and take these into account when interpreting the results.

experiments.title: How asking for a one-time or monthly gift in the email and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page effects donations during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday will achieve an increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During Giving Tuesday the Membership team designed an email and landing page test to see whether an ask for a one-time donation or monthly donation vs. an ask for a “best gift” made any difference on transactions or monthly gifts. The treatment which featured the one-time or monthly ask also led to landing page with the monthly tab first.
Here are the results breakdown:
Members: The experiment showed no statistical difference in donor acquisition between asking for a one-time or monthly gift vs. asking for their best gift. There was a 145% lift in monthly transactions with 90% LOC (5 vs. 10 gifts). There was a directional decrease in clicks on the link in the email for the treatment with a 5% decrease with 63% LOC.
Nonmembers: The test showed the treatment caused a statistically valid decrease in donor acquisition of -59% with 97% LOC. There was no added benefit in monthly acquisition, but the treatment did validate a 5% decrease in email clicks with 99.4% LOC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The treatment asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page did not lead to an increase in monthly gifts for all traffic.

2. Despite seeing a decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 8.3%, which is below the 85% threshold for results to be considered valid.

3. The experiment did not achieve the hypothesized increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions among members during Giving Tuesday.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce statistically significant results with a higher level of confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to revisit the hypothesis and consider potential adjustments to the treatment based on the insights gained from this experiment. Further testing with larger sample sizes and potentially refined treatment variations can help validate findings and inform decision-making for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a popularity call out impacted email clicks and donations for a Donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that refreshing the email copy for Hoover’s Donor audience will achieve an increase in clicks and conversions because they see that people wanted this offer with the “30,000 people” callout.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2024 - 11/13/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Victory and the Savior Generals. This experiment was for their Donor audience.
We wanted to update the copy to have these updates:
Shorter
Mentions well-known and respected Hoover fellow, Victor Davis Hanson, at the beginning of email
And have the callout “Already downloaded by more than 30,000 people in the last 6 months, you don’t want to miss your chance to learn from history’s most influential leaders from one of our country’s most esteemed historians.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the email copy for Hoover's Donor audience with a shorter, more engaging message mentioning Hoover fellow Victor Davis Hanson and including a callout about the popularity of the offer can lead to an increase in clicks. The treatment saw a 5.4% increase in clicks for all traffic, indicating that the updated copy resonated better with the audience.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 40.3%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, while the results suggest a positive impact, they should not be used for future decision-making.

 

However, with the donations Version B showed a 11% increase in number of donations with a 34% level of confidence.

With total revenue being:

Version A: $1859 with 33 gifts

Version B: $2595 with 36 gifts

 

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of the email copy to further optimize for engagement and conversions among Hoover's Donor audience. It's important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results, allowing for more reliable insights to guide decision-making.

experiments.title: How a more conversational tone on an email offering an ebook impacts click through rates for an existing donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a more conversational approach for donors will achieve a change in clicks and email sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/03/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Income Inequality, we wanted to see if a more conversational approach to email copy made an impact on click throughs and email sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved using a more conversational approach in the email copy for donors, resulted in a 2.0% increase in clicks for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 17.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

3. When we look at full funnel statistics, we see the following:

The treatment had a 14% increase in email sign-ups, with an 80% level of confidence.

The treatment had also had a 28% increase in instant donations after people signed up to download the offer, with a 57% level of confidence.

 

Overall, having a more conversational approach to copy did result in increases across the board for an existing donor audience.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts active donors during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional reinforcement for active donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment, which included additional reinforcement about WUNC and its purpose, resulted in a 16.0% decrease in click-through rates compared to the control group.

2. The statistical confidence level of 44.6% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid for decision-making.

3. Moreover, the treatment version had a 50% decrease in donations.

This experiment shows us, based on a small sample size, that active donors to WUNC do not need to be reminded about what WUNC is and does to be motivated to donate.

experiments.title: How using more focused copy on a non-donor email offering up a content offer impacted offer performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing copy for a non donor audience will achieve a change in content offer performance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Personal Freedom and the Moral Case for Capitalism, we wanted to see whether copy that focused more on the offer, and not use pop culture references, impact email and content offer performance. This experiment was for their non-donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that focusing the copy for a non-donor audience resulted in a 2.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. While this shows a potential positive impact on performance, the statistical level of confidence was only 47.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before making any definitive conclusions or decisions based on the results. This may involve running the experiment for a longer duration, increasing the sample size, or making adjustments to the variables being tested.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to continue testing different variations of copy focused on the offer and compare the performance results to further understand the impact on audience engagement and conversion rates. By consistently experimenting and analyzing the results with a critical eye on statistical relevance, Hoover Institution can make more informed decisions to optimize their email content and offers for better performance in the future.

experiments.title: Will Adding “Moody Bible Institute” in an Appeal Increase Transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding Moody Bible Institute for a Fall Share appeal email will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because adding in Moody Bible Institute adds even more credibility to the organization and gives potential donors the chance to associate their gift with something bigger.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/04/2024
Moody Radio runs a Fall Share each year and it reaches a lot of people on air and online.
For this test, we wanted to see if associating Moody Radio with Moody Bible Institute in an email will increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding "Moody Bible Institute" to a Fall Share appeal email for Moody Radio resulted in a 10.8% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 18.6%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. As a result, these findings should not be used for future decision making.

It is important to note that this experiment did not validate for our original hypothesis, however we did see a directional increase in revenue. The treatment saw a 127% lift in revenue at a 85% LoC.

experiments.title: Rad: Does a raw link or a button create more clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a raw link for any email links will achieve a higher number of clicks because having a raw links makes the email feel more personal and not like a marketing email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/04/2024
Moody Radio runs a Fall Share each year that includes a full email campaign.
For this test we wanted to see if the moody radio subscribers prefer a button in an email or a raw link.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a raw link in an email campaign resulted in a 9.0% increase in donations compared to using a button. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 45.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%.
Therefore, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different elements of the email campaign to determine what resonates best with Moody Radio subscribers.

experiments.title: How adding copy that emphasizes how many people downloaded the offer on an email to donors impacts click throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding claims of popularity for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in email sign up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2024 - 09/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Trickle Down, we wanted to test whether adding copy that emphasizes how many downloads of the offer have happened in the past impacts sign-up rates. This email was aimed towards a donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding claims of popularity for content offer downloaders can potentially impact email sign-up rates positively. The treatment group, which included this additional copy, saw a 15.2% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.8%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.
Moreover, when digging into downstream results, we see the following:
The treatment version had a 4% decrease with a 21% level of confidence. In terms of instant donor conversions, the treatment had a 55% DECREASE in donations, with a 95% level of confidence.
Future variations of this experiment could look at the congruency between email and landing page and donation form to make sure content offer downloaders see the same messaging across all points of the funnel.

experiments.title: How refreshing copy impacted click through rate on a recirculation offer for Donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy for Hoover’s donors will achieve an increase in click-through-rate because people will be more likely to read the copy if they’ve never seen it before.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2024 - 10/04/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. This specific test was on their Donor audience. They have enough offers to do different offers for roughly 3-5 months before the same offer is sent out. We suppress anyone who has previously downloaded this offer. So for this test, we decided to try new copy in the email to “refresh” the offer and hopefully increase the click through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy for Hoover's donors did not result in an increase in click-through-rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is only 56.3%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to test different variables such as formatting, visuals, or timing of the email to see what truly resonates with the audience. It is also important to continue monitoring and testing different variables to optimize performance and achieve the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How adding copy that emphasizes how many people downloaded the offer on an email to non-donors impacts click throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding claims of popularity for a non-donor audience will achieve a change in email sign up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2024 - 09/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Tricke Down, we wanted to test whether adding copy that emphasizes how many downloads of the offer have happened in the past impacts sign-up rates. This email was aimed towards a donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding claims of popularity to the email sent to a non-donor audience did not have a significant impact on email sign-up rates. The treatment group, which included claims of popularity, actually saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 74.5%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.
Moreover, when looking at downstream results, we see the following:
The treatment version had an 8% increase in email signups, with a 74% level of confidence.The treatment version had a 35% increase in donations, with a 60% level of confidence.
For non-donors specifically, adding in references to the popularity of the ebook increased all key metrics. Future testing would look at testing congruency on the messaging--does adding references to the popularity on the landing page or donation page impact performance metrics?

experiments.title: How shortening an email impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by shortening the copy for email subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the CTA is short, to the point and no fluff.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/24/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
Each month PTV promotes a different book, for September PTV offered a brand new book by Dr. Jeffress.
In an effort to increase donor conversions, we decided to test a shorter, more direct copy vs the standard longer version
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the shortened copy for email subscribers did not result in a higher donor conversion rate compared to the standard longer version. While the treatment saw a 7.9% decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 37.5%, which is below the threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the shorter copy to see if there is a more effective version that can be used to increase donor conversions. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the campaign or the target audience. Overall, it is important to continue testing and analyzing different strategies to find the most effective approach for increasing donor conversions.

experiments.title: How an in-line banner ask on an article page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a wiki style banner for the article pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the additional value prop on the page can motivate people to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2023 - 01/01/2024
For a high urgency campaign we wanted to test if having an ask on an article page above the main hero image would impact donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: Having the wiki style banner ask on the articles pages increased the total number of transactions by 6.6% but with only a 41% LOC. While the transactions did increase, the revenue actually saw a decrease by 7.6%. This leads me to believe that there could have been an large gift in the control that should not be taken into account when reviewing the results for this experiment.

experiments.title: How including a staff testimonial video within an email impacts donor acquisition metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the staff testimonial video for Eagle Lake 2 email appeals will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion metrics because “people give to people” and the video helps establish a personable connection with the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/29/2024
For The Navigators Eagle Lake 2 campaign this year, we wanted to test including a staff testimonial video within an email appeal to see how it impacted donor conversion metrics. The Navigators audience tends to respond more positively to more genuine communication and real stories.
Therefore, for email 7 of this high urgency campaign, we included an applicable video of an Eagle Lake Counselor sharing how God was working within the camp this year. We expected to see an increase in overall donor conversion metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a staff testimonial video in email appeals for The Navigators Eagle Lake 2 campaign can lead to a significant increase in donor conversion metrics. The treatment group, which received the email with the video, experienced a 44.5% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that the video helped establish a more personable connection with donors, leading to a higher conversion rate.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.0%, which falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of the experiment should not be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
Further, we saw a 37.2% lift in overall revenue at a level of confidence of 78.2%. While this metric is also not considered valid, it does suggest that The Navigators audience responded positively to the inclusion of the video.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of incorporating more person to person connection within appeals, but ensuring that the sample size is sufficient to reach a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be valuable to test different types of testimonial videos or other forms of personal and genuine communication to determine the most effective strategy for engaging donors.

experiments.title: How does calling out the CTA at the top of an email impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by providing the CTA at the very top of an email for email subscribers will achieve a higher gifts and revenue because it highlights what is most important without much reading.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/22/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
Each month, PTV promotes a different book in an effort to encourage donors to give as an incentive
For July, we released a brand new kids book by Dr. Jeffress.
We received an email from another company that used a text box at the top of the email that plainly and briefly gave the CTA in case the reader did not have time to read the entire email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing the call-to-action (CTA) at the very top of an email did not yield the desired results. In fact, the treatment group, which received the CTA at the top of the email, saw a 24.8% decrease in revenue compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below the 85% threshold, which means the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. This indicates that the decrease in revenue may not be solely attributed to the placement of the CTA at the top of the email.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies to optimize the placement of the CTA in emails. It may be beneficial to test variations in the messaging, design, or timing of the CTA to determine what resonates best with donors and drives the desired actions. Additionally, considering other factors that could have impacted the results, such as the content of the email or the timing of the campaign, could provide further insights for future experiments.

experiments.title: How Adding an Additional Link in an Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional link for the fye appeal emails will achieve a higher click rate because there is an additional opportunity that wasn’t there before.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Mood runs a FYE campaign each year that relies a lot on email. For this test we wanted to see if adding an additional link in the copy would increase clicks to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While adding an additional link does increase the click rate by 4%, it is only at a 75% LoC. Furthermore, looking down the adding an additional link does not negatively or positively impact donor acquisition or revenue.

experiments.title: How Adding an Additional Link in an Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional link for the fye appeal emails will achieve a higher click rate because there is an additional opportunity that wasn’t there before.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Mood runs a FYE campaign each year that relies a lot on email. For this test we wanted to see if adding an additional link in the copy would increase clicks to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While adding an additional link does increase the click rate by 4%, it is only at a 75% LoC. Furthermore, looking down the adding an additional link does not negatively or positively impact donor acquisition or revenue.

experiments.title: How Adding Sharing Where Donations Are Being Allocated in an Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being realistic about the support that runs Moody Radio for the FYE campaign will achieve a higher click rate because it shows the user the impact that they will make.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
During Moody’s FYE campaign we wanted to see if we added copy that said exactly what their donations are supporting could increase motivation to get to the donation page
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding specific information about what donations are supporting can potentially increase motivation and click rates for the FYE campaign on Moody Radio. Although the treatment group did see a significant increase in clicks compared to the control group, the statistical level of confidence was below the desired threshold of 95%.

Results on the donation page show little to no impact on the amount of transactions or the average gift.

This indicates that while the results are promising, they are not conclusive enough to make a definitive decision based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing this hypothesis with larger sample sizes or different variations in the message to determine if this strategy consistently leads to increased engagement with the donation page. It is important to remember that valid results with a high level of statistical confidence are crucial for making informed decisions based on experimental outcomes.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a gift impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the impact of a gift by restructuring the copy for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it will help direct the reader’s attention to it and place greater emphasis on the impact of the donor rather than the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2024 - 06/23/2024
In an effort to increase donor conversion metrics on The Navigators Crossword Puzzles Content Offer, we decided to run an A/B test split 50/50. With previous offers, we have seen a positive lift in transactions and revenue when calling out the impact of a gift. Therefore, we expected to see similar results on this test.
The treatment reworked the structure of the copy to direct the reader’s attention toward the portion of the copy that emphasized the impact of their gift with the expectation that we would see a lift in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that reworking the copy to emphasize the impact of a gift can have a positive effect on donor conversion metrics, specifically increasing transactions. In this case, the treatment saw a significant 69.0% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was 68.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, while the results suggest that emphasizing the impact of a gift can be effective in driving donations, they cannot be considered valid for future decision-making due to the low level of statistical confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before making any definitive conclusions or implementing changes based on the results. It may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period to gather more data and increase confidence in the results. Additionally, we plan to further test this concept with The Navigators audience to help validate these findings for future offers.

experiments.title: How does mentioning the free resources a subscriber has received impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that mentioning that the subscriber has received free resources for nondonors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they reciprocate the value they have received.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/28/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During December and June, PTV conducts matching challenges where a donor’s gift will have double the impact.
In an effort to convert nondonors to donors, we wanted to test mentioning that the subscriber has benefited from the free resources provided by PTV.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that mentioning that the subscriber has received free resources for non-donors led to a decrease in donations, contrary to our hypothesis. However, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further investigate the reasons behind the decrease in donations when mentioning the free resources. This could involve gathering feedback from subscribers or conducting additional tests to understand the impact of different messaging strategies.

Overall, while this specific experiment did not provide conclusive results, it highlights the importance of rigorous testing and the need to interpret results with caution before making any significant changes based on the findings.

experiments.title: How does mentioning the free resources a subscriber has received impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that mentioning that the subscriber has received free resources for nondonors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they reciprocate the value they have received.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/28/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During December and June, PTV conducts matching challenges where a donor’s gift will have double the impact.
In an effort to convert nondonors to donors, we wanted to test mentioning that the subscriber has benefited from the free resources provided by PTV.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that mentioning that the subscriber has received free resources for non-donors led to a decrease in donations, contrary to our hypothesis. However, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further investigate the reasons behind the decrease in donations when mentioning the free resources. This could involve gathering feedback from subscribers or conducting additional tests to understand the impact of different messaging strategies.

Overall, while this specific experiment did not provide conclusive results, it highlights the importance of rigorous testing and the need to interpret results with caution before making any significant changes based on the findings.

experiments.title: How removal of an array in favor of a simplified ask in email impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying an expanded gift array in email for consistent $26 ask in the copy for Human Coalition housefile will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it eliminates decision friction and maintains the consistency of the $26 ask presented in all other parts of the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2024
For Human Coalition’s Mother’s Day campaign, the main ask presented to the donor was that $26 help reach a mom seeking an abortion. However, in various email the reader would also be presented with an expanded array ask that requested $26, $52, $104, or their best gift in the email copy. Our hypothesis is that by simplifying this array to instead continue focus on the $26 ask would improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the gift array in email by consistently focusing on a single ask amount of $26 can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment group saw an 18.5% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 39.4%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

Interestingly, it should also be noted that despite the treatment eliminating the expanded array of asks, ultimately average gift remained similar between the control and treatment.

Control: 112,985 Samples | 17 Donations | .1% Revenue Per Visitor | Avg Gift $78.22

Treatment: 112,131 Samples | 20 Donations | .1% Revenue Per Visitor | -.5 Relative Difference | 53.8% Significance | Avg Gift $62.56

Overall, the results of this experiment indicate that simplifying the ask amount in email communications can have a positive impact on donor conversion rates, but further testing and larger sample size is needed for validity.

experiments.title: How including a gift handle with the corresponding impact described in a fundraising email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we’re providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
The Navigators are running their bi-annual Eagle Lake Campaign to raise money for this kids’ camp that operates every summer. In the kickoff email of this campaign, they wanted to test how including gift handles in the email would affect donor conversion rate. Each gift amount described what that amount would provide for Eagle Lake.
This experiment idea came from their direct mail piece which included “tickets” for people to purchase. Each ticket was for a different amount (same as the email) and described what each level of ticket would provide for the kids at Eagle Lake.
To keep the language consistent between the email and the donation page, the treatment donation page also had the gift handles in the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including gift handles with corresponding impact descriptions in a fundraising email may not always lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease of 15.1% in donations for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid or conclusive.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies and elements in fundraising emails to see what resonates best with donors. This could include testing different gift handle amounts, impact descriptions, or even the placement of this information within the email. It's also important to ensure that experiments are conducted with a sufficient sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results.

Overall, the key takeaway is to not solely rely on the results of one experiment, especially if the statistical confidence level is below the recommended threshold. By continuing to test and iterate on different strategies, we can gather more reliable data to inform decision-making for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How including a gift array impacted clicks on a direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift array options for nondonor email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because there is greater clarity around the giving amounts and easier entry point to the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
During a fall recurring campaign, we wondered if introducing the gift array options for monthly donors would drive additional clicks on an appeal by introducing the decision at an earlier stage in the funnel. We split the non-donor file 50/50 and included a clickable gift array for the treatment group.
experiments.keyLearnings: The inclusion of gift array options for non-donor email recipients did not achieve the expected increase in clicks to the donation page. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 44.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. For future testing, we will consider testing different variations of the gift array options, such as the layout, wording, or placement within the email, to see if any of these factors may impact the click-through rates.

experiments.title: How letting members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, are appreciated affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Letting members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they will know their gift is helping support the mission
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if more members would click if they knew they would receive a Member’s Card no matter how much they gave. The treatment included the phrase, “Any amount you give is greatly appreciated” as part of the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version, which included the phrase "Any amount you give is greatly appreciated", did not perform as well as the control version in terms of click-through rates. There was a 9.3% decrease in clicks for all traffic with a 44.1% confidence level, signaling that the control version was more effective at encouraging action. The conversion rate was also lower for the treatment email.

2. The statistical confidence level of 44.1% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that the results are not considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Consider the messaging carefully and ensure that it aligns with the organization's goals and mission. While emphasizing that any amount is appreciated may seem like a positive way to encourage donations, it's crucial to test different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with the audience.

2. Continue to experiment with different messaging, calls to action, and incentives to understand what drives donor behavior. By testing various approaches, we can gather valuable insights to inform future campaigns and optimize results.

3. Increase the sample size or test over a longer period to improve the statistical confidence level of the results. This will help ensure that the findings are reliable and can be used to make informed decisions.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different strategies and messaging to determine the most effective way to engage supporters and drive action. By learning from these results, we can continue to refine our approach and improve the effectiveness of our campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: How letting non-members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, are appreciated affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Letting non-members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they will know their gift is helping support the mission
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked non-members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if more non-members would click if they knew they would receive a Member’s Card no matter how much they gave. The treatment included the phrase, “Any amount you give is greatly appreciated” as part of the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment version, which included the phrase "Any amount you give is greatly appreciated," did not perform as well as the control version in terms of click-through rates. There was a 4.2% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic and a slight increase in conversions with a 58.4% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

2. The hypothesis that letting non-members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate was not supported by the results of this experiment.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the impact of wording and messaging on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variations of language to determine the most effective way to communicate the value of donations to non-members. Additionally, it is important to ensure that experiments are designed to achieve a statistically significant level of confidence to obtain reliable results that can inform decision-making.

experiments.title: How explaining the value of the membership card to non-members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the open rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value non-members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked non-members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if non-members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including reminder text about the value of a membership card can potentially increase the click-through rates for donation emails, as evidenced by the 0.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical significance level of 11.0% is below the standard threshold of 85%, indicating that these results may not be reliable for future decision-making.

Note: While the click-through rate increased in the treatment email, the conversion rate had a slightly lower conversion rate.

Moving forward, it is important to continue exploring the impact of including value proposition reminders in donation appeals, perhaps refining the messaging or placement within the email to maximize effectiveness. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes to validate the findings and make more informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How reminding members about the value of the membership card in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the click-through rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.

Note: The treatment email also had a lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the wording and placement of value proposition texts in emails. To improve the effectiveness of future experiments, it may be helpful to test different variations of value propositions, incorporate feedback from focus groups or surveys, and ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw valid conclusions. experiments.title: How mentioning a match offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the match gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members will increase the click-through rate because the incentive to give is given greater emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if lapsed members would click if the matching gift offer was placed earlier in the body of the email. The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action that mentioned the matching gift opportunity after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the matching gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members did not increase the click-through rate. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Note: The conversation rate of the treatment email also experienced a slight decrease.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing the placement of the matching gift offer within the email to determine the most effective position for maximizing click-through rates. Additionally, exploring other factors such as the messaging around the matching gift offer or the design of the email could also be valuable in optimizing fundraising efforts for lapsed members.

experiments.title: How mentioning a match offer sooner in a fundraising email to members affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the match gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to members will increase the click-through rate because the incentive to give is given greater emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click if the matching gift offer was placed earlier in the body of the email. The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action that mentioned the matching gift opportunity after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the matching gift offer earlier in a fundraising email did not increase the click-through rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment saw a slight decrease in clicks compared to the control. However, the statistical confidence level of 23.9% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Note: While the click-through rate decreased in the treatment email, the conversion rate increased slightly.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the placement of incentives like matching gift offers in fundraising emails carefully. While the hypothesis in this experiment did not hold true, it does not mean that emphasizing incentives early in emails is ineffective across the board. It may be worth testing different messaging strategies, placements, or types of incentives to see what resonates most with members and drives higher engagement. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence in results before concluding is crucial for making informed decisions based on experimentation.

experiments.title: How addressing one specific problem in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for members
experiments.researchQuestion: Addressing a specific problem/issue will increase the click-through rate because members have a clearer understanding of the problem they will help solve.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if describing one issue America faces instead of three issues would encourage members to click. The control mentioned three issues while the treatment focused on one. The issues were mentioned in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that addressing a specific problem/issue in an email campaign can potentially increase the click-through rate. In this case, the treatment focusing on one issue saw a 13.5% increase in clicks compared to the control that mentioned three issues. This indicates that providing a clear and specific understanding of the problem members will help solve can be more impactful in encouraging action.

Note: The donation conversion rate for the treatment email also experienced a slight increase.

However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is 57.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for statistical significance. Therefore, these results should not be considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of addressing specific issues versus multiple issues in email campaigns. By conducting further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations, we can gather more conclusive data on whether this approach effectively increases engagement and donations. Additionally, it may be helpful to explore other factors such as the framing of the issue, the language used, or the placement within the email to further optimize campaign effectiveness.

experiments.title: How adding match-giving language to the call to action affects the click-through rate for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe adding match-giving language to the calls to action will increase the click-through rate because it provides urgency and a feeling of greater impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click more if the matching gift offer was mentioned again in the call to action text. The control email described the matching gift opportunity once in the body of the email. The treatment mentioned the match in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding match-giving language to the calls to action can potentially increase the click-through rate. The treatment group, which included the matching gift offer in both the body of the email and the call to action, experienced an 11.3% increase in clicks compared to the control group.
Note: The treatment email also had a higher conversion rate.
However, it's important to note that the confidence level of the results was only 70.1%, falling below the 85% threshold typically required for statistical significance. As a result, these results should not be considered valid for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of emphasizing matching gift opportunities in calls to action. This can help to determine if this strategy consistently drives higher engagement from recipients. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure results have a high level of statistical significance before drawing any definitive conclusions or making changes based on the findings.

experiments.title: How belonging to a like-minded community in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for lapsed members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe creating a sense of unity will increase the click-through rates because supporters want to belong and connect with other people who have the same values and passions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked lapsed members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if including text about being in a community of like-minded individuals would encourage lapsed members to click. Text was added to the body of the email and in the call to action.
The idea of unity is based on a principle from Robert Cialdini and the psychology of persuasion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the inclusion of text about being in a community of like-minded individuals did not have a significant impact on click-through rates for The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control, with a statistical confidence level below the threshold for validity.
Note: The donation conversion rate experienced a slight increase in the treatment email.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider alternative hypotheses and strategies for increasing engagement among lapsed members. This could involve testing different messaging approaches, incentives, or targeting tactics to better understand what resonates with supporters and drives them to take action. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are designed with a sufficient sample size and statistical power to draw meaningful conclusions.

experiments.title: How 1 call-to-action vs 2 calls-to-action in an email impacts click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the number of CTA for an email will achieve a higher click rate because the opportunity to click has grown.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
For our first set of experiments for Moody Radio we wanted to test into a more personalized email instead of a heavily templated email.
For this we came up with several tests that we wanted to do.
HTML vs. Plain Text
Hyperlink vs. Button
1 CTA vs 2 CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the number of calls-to-action (CTA) in an email can potentially lead to a higher click rate. In this case, the treatment with two CTAs resulted in an 18.5% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group with only one CTA. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 48.5%, which means that these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size to increase the statistical significance of the results. It may also be beneficial to explore the impact of different types of CTAs (such as buttons vs. hyperlinks) and the effect of using HTML vs. plain text emails. By continuing to experiment and test different variables, we can gather more reliable data to make informed decisions about optimizing email performance for Moody Radio.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired actin is clearer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. The email was a personal communication informing members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask members to “activate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the call to action from a question to a statement did not lead to a higher click-through rate among members. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks and the number of conversions, although the results were not statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it is important to note that small changes in wording or formatting may not always have the desired impact on member engagement. It is essential to conduct thorough testing with a significant sample size and statistical confidence to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence member behavior, such as the timing of the email, the content of the message, or the overall design of the communication.

Overall, the findings from this experiment should not be used to make decisions about future email campaigns, as the results do not provide a clear direction. Instead, it would be advisable to continue testing different strategies and elements to determine the most effective approach for encouraging members to take action.

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. The email was a personal communication informing non-members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask non-members to “activate” their membership.

The number of conversions for both emails was the same.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits of a premium affect recurring donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that explaining the benefits of a premium book for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversions because the additional clarity on the premium will motivate them to click the link and learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test calling out the perceived benefits of the premium in an effort to pinpoint motivating factors for their audience. The control email called out the impact that their monthly gift would have on the organization whereas the treatment called out the benefits of reading the book that they would receive as a premium for becoming a monthly donor.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the benefits of a premium in a fundraising appeal may not necessarily lead to an increase in donor conversions.
