In this case, the treatment that focused on the benefits of receiving a book as a premium for becoming a monthly donor actually resulted in a decrease in donations compared to the control, which highlighted the impact of the monthly gift on the organization. The treatment saw a drop of 44.1% in transactions at a 67.7% level of confidence, which is below the validity threshold.

This copy test saw a valid increase in clicks, but an invalid drop in transactions. Therefore, indicating that the copy was persuasive in getting potential donors to click the link, but their audience was less motivated to give.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing different messaging strategies to understand what truly motivates the audience to give. This could involve testing different types of premiums, highlighting various impacts of donations, or even testing different audience segments with tailored messaging. By continuously testing and learning from these experiments, we can gain a deeper understanding of our audience and optimize our fundraising strategies for better results.

experiments.title: How does receiving a premium compared to receiving personal recognition for their gift affect clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personal recognition for monthly partners will achieve an increase in clicks and donor conversion because donors would feel more valued by the organization and the personal touch of their name being added to the group.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test whether a premium or personal recognition for their recurring gift would be more effective.
The control focused on the same premium that had been used throughout the campaign, and the treatment focused on a more personal recognition for their monthly gift. The treatment also promised to send the monthly partner a picture of their name on the window with all of the other monthly partners.
We hypothesized that the treatment would greatly outperform the control due to the personal touch and recognition of becoming a monthly partner because recognition is a motivating factor for the majority of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which focused on personal recognition for monthly partners, did not outperform the control, which offered a premium for recurring gifts. The treatment actually saw a drop of 3.7% in clicks at a 21.6% level of confidence. These results are inconclusive; however, we also wanted to look into if this test impact donations rather than clicks.

The control had a total of 13 donations, and the treatment had 8 donations. The results in terms of transactions show a drop of 36.1% in transactions at a 71.3% level of confidence. While transactions also did not validate, it indicates that The Navigators audience did not respond well to recognition for their gift.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of personal recognition versus premiums for recurring donors, ensuring that the sample size is sufficient to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, considering other variables or elements that could influence donor behavior, such as messaging or incentives, could provide a more comprehensive understanding of what motivates donors to contribute.

experiments.title: How including an image of a premium in an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including an image of the premium for email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there is greater clarity about the children’s book and its intended audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
When offering to send a book as a premium for gifts of a certain amount, we wondered if having an image of the book being offered would have any impact on email engagement by generating interest and curiosity about the contents of the book as well as creating congruency between the email and the donation page, which also had the image of the book displayed at the point of transaction. Since the author also writes books for adults, we wanted to provide clarity at the point of decision-making that this book would be best suited for young readers or any children in the visitors’ lives.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including an image of the premium in email communications might have a positive impact on engagement and click-through rates. Although the treatment group did see a 7.1% increase in clicks, the statistical confidence level of 64.8% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of visuals, such as images of premiums, in email communications to gauge their impact on recipient engagement. However, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results. Additionally, incorporating other elements such as A/B testing different visuals or messaging strategies could provide further insights into what resonates best with the audience and drives the desired actions.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits associated with becoming a recurring donor impact click-through rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the benefits of becoming a monthly partner  for the donor will achieve an increase in click through rate because  of the perceived exclusivity and incentives associated with becoming a monthly partner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For The Navigator’s February Sustainers Appeal, we hypothesized that calling out the benefits of becoming a monthly partner would increase our click-through rate as well as our donor conversion rate. The control email emphasized the importance and need for monthly partners, focusing heavily on the difference it would make for the organization. Whereas, the treatment focused on the benefits for the donor themselves.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted for The Navigator's February Sustainers Appeal tested the impact of emphasizing the benefits of becoming a monthly partner for the donor, as opposed to focusing on the organization's need for monthly partners.
The treatment email, which highlighted the benefits for the donor, showed a 7.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control email. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 52.7%, meaning the results cannot be considered statistically valid.
However, the treatment greatly outperformed the control in terms of transactions. The treatment saw a 161.3% lift in transactions at a 99.6% level of confidence, meaning that the results are valid!
Therefore, the key learnings from this experiment are that The Navigators housefile is much more motivated to give when exclusivity and incentives are coupled with an appeal. While the results showed that readers were slightly more inclined to click the link when benefits to the donor were included, the individuals who did take the next step within the treatment, were much more motivated to give.

experiments.title: How having multiple calls-to-action impacts the click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the number of ctas in an email for the mass communication emails will achieve a higher percentage of engagement because there will be more opportunities throughout the email to engage with the content.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/23/2024
This is the last test in our design of experiments to make a mass marketing email feel like a personal 1-on-1 email.
We started testing with HTML vs. Plain text and then pivoted to how we present CTA’s within the email (button or raw URL). This final test will be seeing how many cta’s we should put in the email itself. Will the Audience respond better to only having one or is it more ideal to have two?
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the number of CTAs in an email can lead to a higher percentage of engagement, as evidenced by a 6.9% increase in clicks for all traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 20.4%, which is below the typically accepted threshold of 85%. This experiment will need to be retested with a plain text email.

experiments.title: How calling out security reinforcement impacts clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out the security of a gift for potential donors will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate because it will reduce the anxiety of making a gift by emphasizing that their personal information will remain confidential.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2024
For The Navigator’s February Sustainers Appeal, we hypothesized that calling out the security of a gift within the email would increase the click-through rate as well as the donor conversion rate. This ran as a 50/50 split test to their housefile to test the effectiveness of security language for their audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email resulted in a 9.2% drop in clicks at a 60.2% level of confidence as well as a 72.6% drop in donor conversion rate with an 80.2% level of confidence indicating that the control email was more effective. Since both of these metrics fall below the validation threshold of 95%, we can not conclude that The Navigators audience does not respond well to calling out the security of a gift, and it may be worth testing again. It is possible that calling out the security of the gift before the potential donor landed on the donation page caused additional confusion and friction. Furthermore, this campaign sent people to a recurring only donation form which could have played a role in the lack of validation of the experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be interesting to test the impact of a security callout on the donation page as it may convert more donors as well as test this concept on an appeal where one-time donations are also accepted.

experiments.title: How does adding a more personalized introduction affect email revenue on Giving Tuesday?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized introduction with rhetorical questions framed from personal experience relating to the project or topic being discussed for our email housefile will achieve a lift in revenue and an increased conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/15/2023
This test was run to our email housefile on Giving Tuesday 11/28/23 and was our first morning email. We tested a personalized introduction that featured rhetorical questions framed from the donors perspective and text specifically calling out bureaucrats and unlected agencies vs. a control without those additions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results recorded here reflect cumulative results for members (168K emailed) and nonmembers (790K emailed). Below are results for subsegments.
Member: treatment created a 58% increase in revenue with a 62.3% level of confidence
 	Increased conversion rate by 13.7% with a 64.1% level of confidence
 	Nonmembers: treatment created a 8% increase in revenue with a 52.3% level of confidence
 	Increase conversion rate by 24.8% with a 58.1% level of confidence
 	LC: Treatment created a 8.2% increase in revenue with a 52.2% level of confidence
 	No statistical difference between conversion rates
 	Lapsed Members: Treatment crated a -61.1% decrease in revenue with a 71.8% level of confidence
 	Decreased conversion rate by -26% with a 33.6% level of confidence

experiments.title: Does mentioning secure in a direct appeal email impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the word “secure” for subscribers will achieve a higher conversion and revenue impact because of the additional perceived security.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a different premium to its housefile in an effort to acquire new donors and retain existing donors.
For this email, we decided to test adding the word “secure” when asking the subscriber to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the word "secure" when asking subscribers to make their gift can potentially have a positive impact on conversion and revenue. Although the results of this experiment are not statistically valid, they do show a significant increase in revenue for the treatment group.

Revenue impact, treatment saw an increase of 63%. 

Donation impact, treatment saw a increase of 33%.

The treatment version also saw more larger gifts made from the link within the PS (7 gifts vs 3 gifts)

Based on these findings, it would be recommended to further test the use of the word "secure" in future experiments. This could involve testing it with different segments of the audience, using it in different parts of the email, or testing variations of the word itself.

By conducting further experiments and gathering more data, it will be possible to determine with more certainty whether adding the word "secure" consistently leads to increased conversion and revenue. This will help inform future email campaigns and potentially increase the effectiveness of fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How using a personalized and gratitude-centered tone for nonmembers affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: The treatment email, which employs a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, will result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email because the treatment might foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for nonmembers to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how nonmembers would react to a more personalized and gratitude-centered tone in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the personalized and gratitude-centered approach used in the treatment email did not result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email. In fact, the treatment email saw a decrease in donations.

Based on this, it is important to recognize that not all alternative approaches will yield better results. In this case, the treatment email might have been perceived differently by the recipients, leading to a decrease in donations. It is also worth noting that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means the results are not valid.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and higher statistical confidence. This will help ensure more accurate and reliable results. Additionally, it might be beneficial to explore other approaches and messaging strategies that resonate well with the target audience.

Overall, this experiment emphasizes the importance of testing different strategies, analyzing the results, and using the learnings to inform future experimentation. It highlights the need for ongoing testing and optimization to effectively engage and retain members.

experiments.title: How a simplified message that focuses only on impact in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects conversions for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the message and only focusing on the impact in the final email of the campaign will produce a higher conversion rate because the treatment email presents a more straightforward and concise message, which can be easier for recipients to understand quickly.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for members to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how a concise message that only focuses on their impact in the final email of the campaign would affect conversion rates. We believe a clear and simple call-to-action may lead to a better response as it reduces cognitive load for the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment email, which focused solely on the impact of The Heritage Foundation, did not have a significant impact on conversion rates compared to the control email.

The treatment email saw a .35% decrease in clicks for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence is only 3.4%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Although there was no difference in clicks, we did see an 8.9% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 77.4% level of confidence. This tells us that using concise copy for the members-only segment does not motivate them as much to renew their membership when compared to other segments like lapsed donors and non-members.

experiments.title: How does calling out an incomplete membership with a suggested ask amount for non-members affect membership conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting in the email that their membership is incomplete and giving them a suggested gift amount  for nonmembers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it adds clarity to the ask which can cause Potential members appreciate knowing what is expected of them and how much they are expected to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for non-members to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how non-members would react to calling out in the top of the email that their membership is incomplete and providing them with a suggested gift amount to activate it. The callout also included the deadline of January 31st.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting in the email that their membership is incomplete and suggesting a gift amount for non-members can potentially increase donor conversion rate. The treatment group showed a 12.8% increase in donations compared to the control group but only with a 60.6% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment group saw a 21% decrease in clicks on the email with a 99% level of confidence.
Because the statistical level of confidence of 60.6% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, this means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to increase the sample size or conduct the experiment for a longer duration to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. This will provide more reliable results and help make informed decisions about the effectiveness of different strategies.
Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations or combinations of strategies in order to identify the most effective approach for increasing donor conversion rate. This could include testing different types of messaging, callouts, suggested gift amounts, or deadlines.
Overall, the key learning from this experiment is that highlighting membership incompleteness and providing a suggested gift amount may be a promising strategy for increasing donor conversion rate. However, further testing is needed to validate these findings and make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How using a personalized and gratitude-centered tone for lapsed members affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: The treatment email, which employs a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, will result in a higher membership reactivation rate compared to the control email because the treatment might foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how lapsed members would react to a more personalized and gratitude-centered tone in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment email, which employed a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, did not result in a higher membership reactivation rate compared to the control email.

2. The treatment email saw a 7.5% decrease in donations for all traffic.

3. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was 21.6%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.

4. The hypothesis that the treatment email would foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection was not supported by the data.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the personalized and gratitude-centered approach used in the treatment email did not have the intended effect on lapsed members. However, since the results are not valid, it is not recommended to make any decisions based on this experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a similar test with a larger sample size to increase the statistical significance of the results. Additionally, conducting user research or surveying lapsed members to gather insights on their motivations and preferences could help inform future experiments and improve the effectiveness of reactivation campaigns. It is important to continue testing different approaches to find the most effective strategy for reconnecting with lapsed members and increasing reactivation rates.

experiments.title: How using an image of the content offer in a housefile email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image to the email for potential downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because users can see the value of the offer at the beginning of the funnel.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2024
The Navigators sent a free content offer to their housefile list through email. The content offer was a free 12-month calendar and users had to provide their name, email, and home address on the landing page to receive a physical copy. Following the landing page, they were redirected to an instant donation page where they were asked to make a gift to support the Navigators work.
Within the email, they wanted to test how adding an image of the calendar would affect click-through rate from the email to the landing page. They also wanted to know if adding the image to the email would affect donor conversion rate since users can see the value of the offer at the beginning of the funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image to the email did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the threshold of 85%, which means these results should not be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct a larger sample size or make adjustments to the experiment design to increase the statistical power and ensure valid results. The Navigators should also consider testing other variables or elements of the email and landing page to identify potential factors that may influence the donor conversion rate. It is important to continue conducting experiments and collecting data to make informed decisions and optimize the effectiveness of email campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: Does additional value prop drive results for a transactional type email?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value prop and missional language for email audience will achieve a higher conversion rate because the reason for the purchase is more thoroughly fleshed out. .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/11/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
In November and December PTV has seen great response to two premiums, the 2024 Pathway to Victory Daily Devotional and You Can Pray Big Things.
In an effort to drive a bit more response, we decided to send an email encouraging people to make their last minute Christmas purchases by December 7th so that they will arrive in time to homes.
For this email, we decided to test a very short copy, straight to the point versus a longer copy with more missional and value prop language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the shorter, straight-to-the-point copy performed better than the longer copy with more missional and value prop language. Although the results are not statistically significant, there is a trend indicating that the shorter copy may be more effective in driving donations.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test the effectiveness of shorter, concise messaging in driving conversions. This could involve testing different variations of the short copy, or comparing it against other types of messaging, such as emotional appeals or storytelling.

Additionally, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence in the results is sufficient before drawing any conclusions. Conducting tests with a larger sample size or running the experiment for a longer duration may help achieve a higher level of confidence in the results.

Overall, the key takeaway is that concise messaging may have a positive impact on conversion rates, and this should be further explored in future experimentation.

experiments.title: How adding clarity around the impact of a donation affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity about the impact of their gift and the matching gift opportunity for subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because  it provides more clarity and transparency, addressing potential concerns and building trust.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/14/2023
ACI is running their CYE campaign. We wanted to test in the pro-life-focused email how adding clarity about the impact of their gift and the impact of the matching gift opportunity would affect the conversion rate. We did this by adding specific bullets about what their gift will do to help move ACI’s mission forward.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity about the impact of their gift and the matching gift opportunity in the pro-life-focused email had a negative effect on the donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which received the email with the added clarity, saw a 21.2% decrease in donations compared to the control group.
However, it is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, as the level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a more robust study with a larger sample size and a longer testing period to gather more reliable results. Additionally, it may be worth exploring different variations of the email content to see if there are other messaging strategies that can effectively communicate the impact of their gift and the matching gift opportunity without negatively affecting the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How removing a signer’s image in an email footer signature impacted clicks

rows.name: Control - With Image in Footer Signature
Treatment - No Image in Footer Signature

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a photo from the email signature for housefile recipients will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because the email will appear more human and personalized.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2023 - 12/14/2023
When sending a course registration email, we wondered if the signature image was causing the email template to appear more like marketing and less like a personalized email coming from a real human. We split the file 50/50 and tested each version to measure engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing a photo from the email signature for housefile recipients resulted in a 7.5% increase in clickthrough rate. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.8%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.
We will want to re-test this concept in future experiments to fully measure the impact of removing the photo from the email signature.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array in the body of an email appeal impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a sample gift array for a direct email appeal will achieve an increase in engagement because there will be additional cognitive momentum to continue the process to the next step.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/28/2023
In a high-urgency campaign, we typically keep the decisions isolated per each step. In the email step, we help the reader decide “Do I want to click?” and then at the donation page step we help the reader decide “How much do I want to give?” In one email, we wanted to test if adding gift array options at the email level would help more people begin thinking about both questions at once. The treatment version added a gift array of buttons, letting the reader know they could click one of the buttons to choose their donation amount and begin the process of making a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: After splitting the email between the control and the treatment, we observed a 1.2% lift in clicks at 9.4% level of confidence for the treatment version which is not statistically significant so the experiment results are not valid. The engagement remained flat, even when we added buttons with gift array amounts in the body of the email. We will want to look downstream and see if there is any significant difference in completed donations from the treatment group that would lead to more meaningful results to see if moving the gift array into the body copy helped produce more motivated visits from email traffic who had already decided what amount they would be interested in giving and continue the cognitive momentum in the process when they arrive on the donation page.

experiments.title: How adding a second link in an email impacted conversion for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a second link for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate because there are more opportunities to click . because there are more opportunities to click .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2023 - 11/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
This is an experiment we ran for PTV a couple of years ago, but we wanted to test again to see if the results still held.
We tested a second link in the email to see if it would increase engagement and down funnel donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a second link in the email did not result in a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks for all traffic, implying that subscribers did not engage more with the email when presented with an additional link.

2. The control group, which did not have the second link, outperformed the treatment group in terms of clicks. Although this result is not statistically significant, it suggests that the original email format may be more effective in driving engagement.

3 Of those who received the treatment only 37% clicked the first link and 63% clicked the second link.

4 Donations saw a 0.9% increase with a level of confidence 4.5%.

 

Based on these results, the presence of a second link did not impact engagement or donations.

experiments.title: How a brief, to the point email impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by shortening the email to the important parts for emails subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is increased urgency and less copy.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/18/2023
PTV is the global media ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a book written by Dr. Robert Jeffress. For August, Dr Jeffress release a new book that was a available for only a couple weeks. In an effort to see if we could drive results in the final days, we tested a shorter, straight to the point email vs the standard descriptive version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the shorter, straight to the point email was more effective in driving conversions and generating revenue compared to the standard descriptive email. Despite the statistical level of confidence being below the threshold of 85%, the treatment still showed a significant 31.1% increase in revenue for all traffic.

In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue exploring the use of shorter and more concise emails for promotional purposes. This experiment suggests that there is potential in leveraging increased urgency and reducing the amount of copy to drive better results.

However, it is important to note that the results of this particular test should not be used for future decision making due to the low statistical level of confidence. It would be beneficial to run further experiments to validate these findings and ensure their replicability before implementing them as a standard practice.

Additionally, it would be useful to analyze any qualitative feedback or customer insights to better understand the reasons behind the improved performance of the shorter email. This can inform future experiments and help refine the email strategy further.

experiments.title: Will asking for a larger specific amount increase donations and average gift size
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by asking for a larger specific donation amount for a smaller donor set will achieve an increase donations and average gift size.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2021
We wanted to test if sending a direct ask email mentioning a matching gift and asking for a small number of donors to donate a larger amount VS the standard direct ask mentioning the match would help increase the average gift as well as overall revenue.
So the control email mentioned the match and asked for a gift while the treatment mentioned the match and asked for 10 donors to make a gift of $1,000.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the control email performed well and received 54 transactions for $11,737 with an average gift of $217.35 the treatment email saw a smaller number of donations with only 20 transactions but saw a significant increase in revenue and average gift. The total amount raised in the treatment email was $16,745 for an average gift of $837.25!

From this, we see that asking for a smaller number of donors with a larger donation amount can provide exclusivity and an urgency to donate now.

experiments.title: How stating the “deadline” in a subject line for a high urgency campaign impacted open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving clarity to an email subject line amid a high urgency campaign for email recipients will achieve an increase in email open rate because recipients will know their exact window to take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/13/2023
The Illinois Policy Institute is amid a high urgency fundraising campaign. The campaign deadline is tomorrow, and we want to test how making that clear in the subject line of today’s email impacts open rate.
The control variant (subject line A) is “Urgent reminder” while the treatment variant (subject line B) is “Deadline tomorrow”. We’ve split these variants to where 50% of our recipients will experience one subject line and 50% will experience the other.
We want to find out how our recipients respond to each subject line in terms of choosing to open the email. We expect “Deadline tomorrow” to produce the most opens because it clearly communicates the deadline to recipients and the fact that the current opportunity will expire after tomorrow.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment variant, which had the subject line "Deadline tomorrow", did not perform better than the control variant, which had the subject line "Urgent reminder". The treatment variant saw a 4.8% decrease in Opens for All Traffic with a 70.5% statistical level of confidence, indicating that these results cannot be considered valid. Therefore, we cannot conclude that giving clarity to the subject line in a high urgency fundraising campaign leads to an increase in email open rate.
It's also worth noting that the treatment subject line produced a 32.3% drop in click rate with an 82.2% level of confidence.
The "Deadline tomorrow" subject line may have been less effective because recipients didn't know what the deadline was for. Instead of opening the email to find out, they may have decided it wasn't worth their time to find out. The "Urgent reminder" SL on the other hand, tells the recipient that they've been told about the opportunity before and they may then feel more inclined to open the email see what they need to be "reminded" about.
From a psychological standpoint, there is also the possibility that the word "deadline" is a turn off to recipients. Humans often view deadlines in a negative context because they're used to hitting deadlines at work or with tasks in life. They may not take kindly to the mention of another deadline.
This angle is worth testing again for a future high urgency email to get more data behind it.

experiments.title: How eliminating a confirmation message post-survey impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that drawing attention to the donation page copy for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will keep their eyes on the donation page longer rather than leaving immediately upon arrival.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/26/2022
After submitting a survey that led to an instant donation page, we wondered if people were reading the confirmation message “YOU DID IT! Your survey is submitted.” and automatically thinking they had no other action to take on the page and they could click to exit without reading the donation ask immediately following their submission. We developed a treatment that removed the confirmation message and instead led with the headline “One More Thing…” to indicate that their participation in the process was not fully complete. We still mention in the opening paragraph that they filled out the survey and thank them for their time and input. We ran these two versions side by side and measured the impact on donor conversion over a 10 day period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the A/B experiment showed that the treatment version, which removed the confirmation message and led with the headline "One More Thing", had a 10.7% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control version, with a statistical level of confidence of 40.2%. Although the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results suggest that drawing attention to the donation page copy can increase donor conversion rate by keeping their eyes on the page longer.

In future experimentation, we can try to modify the treatment version and test it again to achieve a higher statistical level of confidence. We can also try to identify other factors that might affect donor conversion rate and run further experiments to optimize the donation process. It is essential to keep testing and optimizing the donation page and messaging to increase the number of donations and maximize the impact of the nonprofit's work.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy language to an acquisition offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement underneath the donation button for visitors to the donation page will achieve a greater donor conversion rate because  the privacy statement adds perceived security to the form and reduces anxiety about how the donor’s information will be used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2023 - 02/24/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They run several different acquisition offers to attract donors on Facebook. We decided to run a series of tests to determine the impact of adding a statement promising to protect their privacy on the landing pages and donation pages.  This was the second test in the series where we examined the impact this disclaimer would have on their acquisition offer donation pages.
In our first test on the landing page, we saw that people who had the privacy and communication statement had a higher donor conversion rate than those who did not. We wanted to see if adding the same statement to the donation page would have the same effect. Could it raise donor conversion or even increase the average gift because of the added sense of security and transparency?
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the privacy statement under the donate button resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion and revenue.
Donor revenue increased by 63% with a 94% Level of Confidence. This could be due to adding the perceived sense of privacy, the transparency of what the donors are signing up for, or both.
The donor conversion rate increased as well by 37% with a 75% LOC, this experiment will need to be retested to see if the donor conversion rate would have been able to reach validity with a larger sample size.
The increase in donations observed during this experiment suggests that adding extra assurances and anticipating objections pertaining to how sensitive personal information is handled at the point of the transaction can have a positive effect on donation rates by reinforcing the security of the donation and conveying transparency in how the donor's information will be used.

experiments.title: How giving is impacted by the use of buttons in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing donation buttons from an email appeal for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2023
RTB historically uses multiple “Give Now” buttons in email appeals. During calendar year end we decided to run an experiment where we removed the buttons from the email appeal. The idea is that the removal of the buttons would actually humanize the communication more, make the email seem less promotion-like, and encourage people to read the entire appeal/case for support before deciding to give. This in turn, has the potential to increase donor conversion rate. We chose one email in the campaign to test this concept.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results in this experiment were flat. We saw .35% decrease in donor conversion rate with a .68% level of confidence. The average gift across both the control and treatment were also negligible (control: $250, treatment: $238) so the humanized treatment did not inspire people to give more either. A concern in removing the buttons from the email was that it would negatively impact click thru rate. However, results indicate that the removal of the buttons has no effect on click thru rate either. Click thru rate stayed the same at .4%.
Where we did see a noticeable difference was the impact on new donors. New donors experienced a 497% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 94%.  More non donors were activated to give through the treatment (no buttons) than the control. While it's not a large number of transactions, the organization should consider using this new layout (no buttons) knowing that it will not impact giving overall but may active more new donors. It's likely that non donors saw the email as less transactional/promotional and decided to read more of the appeal and were therefore more motivated to give.

experiments.title: How a forward style email in a high urgency campaign affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a forward style email  for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2023
In an effort to continue to humanize communications during a high urgency campaign, we wanted to use a “forward” style email in one of our email appeals. This email would refer to a previous email and make it seem as if the email was forwarded to the recipient. The control for this experiment had the intended, original email (a message from the President). The treatment was also a message from the President but it asked the email recipient to refer to the previous email sent.
In both of these emails previous donors were suppressed so as not to accidentally include those who may have given in the previous email. The email tested was an evening email on the last day of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 3.6% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 6.69%. This means that this experiment had almost no effect on donor conversion rate. The control seemingly has a higher average gift that is skewed by a $3k gift. There is also no noticeable change in click thru rate. Our hypothesis is that because this was tested in an email that's in the "high urgency" phase of the campaign, motivation to give before the deadline is already high. It's recommended that this is retested at a different phase in a campaign to more clearly discern motivation.

experiments.title: How adding an additional and different CTA in the PS of an email appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional call to action in an email appeal for prospective donors will achieve a decrease in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2023
During calendar year end RTB wanted to be sure to let their constituents know about additional giving options for their 70+ aged donors. There was concern that too many CTA’s would interfere with donor conversion rate so we tested the P.S. message across two different emails in order to get more data around this decision. Our hypothesis was that an additional, different CTA would negatively impact donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not see a valid result from this experiment, we did see a 29% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 70%. It's recommended that before rolling this out to additional emails that this is tested across several more emails to get a large enough sample size as the data seems to indicate a decrease in donor conversion rate when adding the different CTA in the P.S. of the email.

experiments.title: How adding a campaign name in an email appeal affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a familiar campaign name for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/30/2022
The IMB historically runs a Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign and they communicate this fund by name in all their communications. When developing emails for the year end campaign there was initially no mention of the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering. We wanted to see if adding the name of this campaign would impact giving for IMB’s housefile list (who have historically known about this campaign). We tested this verbiage in one email appeal in their campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 15% increase in donor conversion rate with only a level of confidence of 35%. However, when looking at the impact on revenue, we saw a 360% directional lift with a level of confidence of 93%. However, there were several larger $1k+ gifts in the treatment. Nonetheless, this could be an indication that those who are familiar with the Lottie Moon offering may give larger gifts because of the familiarity of this campaign. It should also be noted that mid-level and major donors were suppressed from this campaign so it is likely that some donors upgraded their giving in this appeal and gave larger gifts than they typically would. It is recommended that this is tested across multiple emails in the future in order to confirm that while it may not affect donor conversion rate, it may impact the average gift in an appeal.

experiments.title: How beginning an email with a declaration vs a personalized sentence affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting an email with a more personalized sentence rather than a declaration for email openers will achieve a higher click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/08/2022
Luther Seminary was running their year-end high-urgency campaign, and for the first two appeal emails of the campaign, they wanted to test if starting the email with a more personal sentence instead of a declaration would keep users reading longer which would ultimately increase the click-through rate.
The control email started off with an explicit statement that could be viewed as not personal or more “organizational”. The treatment email started with a personal statement that sounds more conversational or like you’re speaking with someone, not just an organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment email saw a 10.1% decrease in click-through rate with only a 57.7% level of confidence. Because of this, we can assume that starting an email with a declaration or personalized statement does not affect the click-through rate of the email. We believe this does not have as much of an impact because it's changing something very small in the email when compared to the overall value proposition the rest of the email provides.

experiments.title: How acknowledging someones previous gift history affects giving in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out previous giving history for subscribers and donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 11/29/2022
For Luther Seminary’s Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to test how calling out the user’s previous giving history would affect the conversion rate. To do this, we split their file into four segments: never donors, lapsed donors, lybunt donors, and active donors. After those were segmented, we split traffic 50/50 where the control segment saw language around “someone who supports the mission of Luther Seminary” and the treatment segment saw language that specifically called out their last gift DATE and last gift AMOUNT. (Or for never donors, specifically calling out that they had never given a gift to Luther Seminary before.)
We ran this experiment on the AM and PM emails with the same segmentation for Giving Tuesday. In order to have the potential to reach statistical validation, all results have been complied into this one experiment write up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email saw a -.02% decrease in conversion rate with only a .05% level of confidence. These results tell us that there was no difference in giving because of calling out previous donor history.
We would like to test this concept on a different high-urgency appeal email during a time of year when the motivation to give is not as high.

experiments.title: How adding a statement of giving impacts lapsed reactivation rates in an email
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor statement for lapsed donors will achieve a difference in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 11/28/2022
Giving Tuesday is generally a big day of giving for many non-profits. For the Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate and their early Giving Tuesday email targeting lapsed donors, we wanted to see whether including a ‘statement’ of their donation history would help increase lapsed reactivation rates. This email was deployed on 11/28, with the only difference between the control and the treatment being the addition of a statement at the top of the email of the donor’s last gift date, amount, etc.
Our hypothesis was that by adding this statement at the top, it would serve as a visual reminder for lapsed donors of how long it had been since their last gift to the Oblates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With the addition of a donor statement at the top of the email, the treatment version had a 39% higher conversion rate than the control. However, given the lower number of donations total for this lapsed audience, this experiment only had a 43% level of confidence.

Further testing throughout the year-end campaign is warranted to see if a long-term test would validate.

experiments.title: How campaign first messaging impacts clicks from donors in Giving Tuesday emails
experiments.researchQuestion: What messaging will get a more positive response for Giving Tuesday in an email campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/02/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that does its primary fundraising through monthly premiums. For Giving Tuesday, they offered an exclusive bundle only available on that day.
As Giving Tuesday has grown exponentially around the world, we wanted to test how we could stand out during a small timeframe when all organizations are making an ask.
We tested this by using Giving Tuesday based messaging at the beginning of the email and then later talked about the mission of PTV for the control and developed a treatment using PTV mission-focused messaging at the beginning of the email and then later used Giving Tuesday messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not receive a sufficient change in clicks, only 2.5% increase with a 16.8% level of confidence. We did see a 9.2% increase in opens with a 99% level of confidence.
While email opens is no longer a reliable metric, due to Apple and other platform privacy rules. We did observe a high enough increase that warranted a deep look.
When the control email hit inboxes, the viewer saw the subject line and the first few words of the email which were "Hello [firstname], On #Giving Tuesday..." For the treatment, the viewer saw the same subject line but a different first few words. They saw "Hello [firstname], As you know Pathway to Victory..."
Our hypothesis is that the increase of opens is due to the initial response of the email not being Giving Tuesday focused like ever other email in the inbox.
As we did not see a valid response to this test, we will continue testing to see if the order of messaging can impact conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding an additional ask in the P.S. line of an appeal email impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ask in the P.S. line of an email for email recipients will achieve more donors and revenue .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/04/2022
The Navigators were running a short email campaign for their collegiate ministries. In order to increase donor conversion rate and revenue we wanted to see how adding one additional opportunity to give would impact giving. Typically, The Navigators’ have used the “P.S” line as a place to opt out of communications from that specific campaign. We wanted to test using the P.S. line as an additional ask with a link to the donation page. The control for this experiment used the P.S. line to opt out and the treatment used the P.S. line for a call to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at the donor conversion rate, there was virtually no difference. We saw a .2% lift in donor conversion rate with only a level of confidence of .4%--making it a statistically insignificant difference. We did however see a 74% directional lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 86.9%. While this seems positive, it is unlikely that an additional ask would sway a prospective donor to give significantly more than they were originally intending. Furthermore, we only had 29 transactions in this experiment and did not reach the ideal 50 transaction minimum. While there are no outlier gifts, it is more likely that the list was split in such a way that giving level wasn't evenly stratified and the treatment may have had larger donors or more key multiyear donors.

It is recommended that this is retested with an evenly stratified list across multiple emails to gain a more concrete learning.

experiments.title: How distinguishing between two topics in an email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that distinguishing between an offer and an incentive for Working Preacher donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/06/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign in the spring of 2022, and we wanted to test how distinguishing between the two topics in an email (the match for the campaign first, then the free content offer, Sermon Brainwave next) would affect the conversion rate.
The original email seemed jumbled between the incentive (Serman Brainwave content unlock) and the offer (matching gift).
experiments.keyLearnings:
As you can see in the chart above, distinguishing between the offer and incentive did not have an impact on the conversion rate for this email. The treatment saw a 3.8% lift with only an 11.5% level of confidence.

We believe the Working Preacher audience either did not need the extra clarification between the offer and incentive in the email or we did not distinguish enough between the two topics which caused no change in the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How sticking to urgency in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sticking to urgency and removing extended value proposition for the final email in a high urgency campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/06/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign in the spring of 2022, and we wanted to test how sticking with urgency and reducing the amount of extended value proposition copy in the final email would affect donor conversion.
We did this by removing the extra language from the control email centered around what their gift would do for Working Preacher, because these points had been conveyed heavily in every email in the campaign prior to the final email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As you can see in the chart above, removing the extended value proposition and focusing mainly on urgency did not have an impact on the conversion rate for this email. The treatment saw a 6.6% decrease with only a 20.2% level of confidence.

From this experiment, we can assume that the difference in language did not affect the donor's decision to give at this point in the campaign. We believe this is because both emails had the urgency of that being the final day, and whether or not we included the extended value proposition, it did not affect the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How creating intrigue in an email promoting new research impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that building intrigue for subscribers we are promoting new research to will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/08/2022
As a part of launching our new research study for how the industry handles a New Donor Welcome Series, we decided to experiment with a bullet list of questions that the research would answer, thereby increasing the “intrigue” factor for the reader.
Our thought was that this intrigue would drive more motivated clicks and increase email signup rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, this treatment did not have any substantial impact on the email signup rate. Although it shows a -7.6% email signup rate, the level of confidence is less than a coin flip probability to be repeated.

This means that the treatment did not create more (or less) motivation for a subscriber to get our new research.

This could simply be due to the fact that new research is just in such high demand that any lower-level experiments to manufacture "more demand" aren't necessary. On the other hand, it could be due to the fact that this approach was ineffective and should be retested in future opportunities to re-test the concept.

experiments.title: How “hero-focused” language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leveraging hero language for previous donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/22/2022
Donors are heroes. Or, so the common logic goes. CaringBridge had heard over and over again at conferences and speaking engagements how important it is to make the donor feel like a hero. So, they decided to test it themselves.
In their first appeal for their anniversary campaign, they had a very personal story from the campaign manager. They decided to replace this copy with copy that highlighted the donor as the hero.
They tested this by splitting their email file in half and sending one treatment to each half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the results weren't statistically valid, there was a directional lift using the "hero" copy that focused the attention on the donors.

This will need to be re-tested, but signs point to the fact that giving the donor a sort of platitude that makes them feel good can increase results.

experiments.title: How “hero-focused” language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leveraging hero language for previous donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/22/2022
Donors are heroes. Or, so the common logic goes. CaringBridge had heard over and over again at conferences and speaking engagements how important it is to make the donor feel like a hero. So, they decided to test it themselves.
In their first appeal for their anniversary campaign, they had a very personal story from the campaign manager. They decided to replace this copy with copy that highlighted the donor as the hero.
They tested this by splitting their email file in half and sending one treatment to each half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the results weren't statistically valid, there was a directional lift using the "hero" copy that focused the attention on the donors.

This will need to be re-tested, but signs point to the fact that giving the donor a sort of platitude that makes them feel good can increase results.

experiments.title: Does adding a donor’s last gift into a supporter card renewal appeal increase renewals?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reminding a donor of their last gift date for members of AFP will achieve higher click throughs and donor conversion rates..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/19/2022 - 03/05/2022
Americans for Prosperity has a long-standing membership card, which is promoted through mail and digital channels. For this year’s renewal campaign, we wanted to test whether including the image of the card, with a donors’ information overlaid on top of that image, coupled with a shorter copy, would lead to a higher click rate and higher donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: Our treatment version with image and donor information performed better than the control version, with a 10% increase in click throughs. While not a valid result, it also resulted in 800% more donations than the control version.

experiments.title: How adding donor cultivation stories on top of a campaign appeal schedule of emails impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending donor stories for year-end campaign recipients will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 01/02/2021
The Leadership Institute decided to experiment with adding donor stories on top of their regularly scheduled calendar year-end campaign email appeal schedule to experiment with whether or not it inspired more people to give at all or increased generosity from those that saw the donor story emails.
To administer the test, we evenly split the file based upon (a) donor vs. non-donor statuses, (b) the donor’s highest previous contribution tier, (c) how long the person was on the email file, and finally (d) whether or not they were currently considered “engaged” or “disengaged” from the email program (where “engaged” was whether or not they had opened any of the last 10 emails that we had sent to them before splitting the email file evenly for this experiment).
The donor stories were sent from a “secondary sender” for the organization—someone who was planned to be used in two of the year-end campaign messages.
Finally, to ensure that the samples within the study are accurate, we looked at unique individual emails for both segments that received any year-end email (including the “donor story” emails for the treatment segment)
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 53.7% level of confidence, we observed that the treatment segment did not increase revenue in any significant way.

Although this treatment segment saw a decrease in the donor conversion rate of -23.2% (with a valid level of confidence of 99.4%) however, we don't believe that the additional donor story emails drove the decrease in donor conversion rate.

We did notice, however, that there was a +27.8% average gift value for those donors that did decide to give from the treatment segment over that of those in the control segment (who did not receive the donor stories).

Further experimentation should be considered with this approach.

experiments.title: How adding donor cultivation stories on top of a campaign appeal schedule of emails impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending donor stories for year-end campaign recipients will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 01/02/2021
The Leadership Institute decided to experiment with adding donor stories on top of their regularly scheduled calendar year-end campaign email appeal schedule to experiment with whether or not it inspired more people to give at all or increased generosity from those that saw the donor story emails.
To administer the test, we evenly split the file based upon (a) donor vs. non-donor statuses, (b) the donor’s highest previous contribution tier, (c) how long the person was on the email file, and finally (d) whether or not they were currently considered “engaged” or “disengaged” from the email program (where “engaged” was whether or not they had opened any of the last 10 emails that we had sent to them before splitting the email file evenly for this experiment).
The donor stories were sent from a “secondary sender” for the organization—someone who was planned to be used in two of the year-end campaign messages.
Finally, to ensure that the samples within the study are accurate, we looked at unique individual emails for both segments that received any year-end email (including the “donor story” emails for the treatment segment)
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 53.7% level of confidence, we observed that the treatment segment did not increase revenue in any significant way.

Although this treatment segment saw a decrease in the donor conversion rate of -23.2% (with a valid level of confidence of 99.4%) however, we don't believe that the additional donor story emails drove the decrease in donor conversion rate.

We did notice, however, that there was a +27.8% average gift value for those donors that did decide to give from the treatment segment over that of those in the control segment (who did not receive the donor stories).

Further experimentation should be considered with this approach.

experiments.title: How specificity in a subject line impacts open rate

rows.name: You should read this with your family
You should read this to your kids

experiments.researchQuestion: Will specificity in the subject line increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2020 - 11/24/2020
Care Net was sending a cultivation email to their file and wanted to test the element of specificity in their subject line. The control was “You should read this with your family” and the treatment was, “You should read this with your kids”.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren't able to validate the results, but we did see a directional lift from the treatment. This tells us that the specificity from the treatment minimally helped increase engagement, but wasn't a significant enough difference to have a greater impact.

experiments.title: Will a more clear and direct subject line lead to more course signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more clear with the offer in the email in the subject line for email subscribers will achieve more email course signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2021
We wanted to let people know about an updated online course that was now available for free. We’ve seen how more mysterious subject lines can evoke a sense of curiosity and get more opens but we’ve also seen that a clear direct subject line can sometimes lead to more action. So we put it to the test. The control was a more mysterious subject line that said there was ‘exciting news for you’ but didn’t say anything about what it was while the treatment was clear with the fact that there was a ‘new, free course for you’. Both subject lines used personalization in the first name in the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the final result on conversion wasn't statistically significant, there was a directional lift towards the Treatment increasing conversion. If we go 'up the funnel' or 'down the mountain' and look at clicks, we actually observed 15.8% increase in clicks with a 91% LoC which does indicate more intent to sign up from the clean subject line compared to the mysterious subject line. And when we also see that open rate for the clear subject line was 11% lower with 100% LoC, it does seem to validate both the hypothesis that:
Mysterious subject lines are better at generating opens
 	Clear subject lines are better at generating actions

experiments.title: How a relevant subject line affected housefile engagement for a cultivation email

rows.name: Are you familiar with Mattress Mack?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing “you” to add relevance in a subject line for a cultivation series to the housefile will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
We were noticing a recent decline in open rate for a weekly cultivation email series to the housefile so we wanted to test some subject line elements to see how we could improve how the audience was responding to different subject lines and increase engagement with the cultivation emails. We established a control—“An interesting interview”—using the element of mystery to invoke curiosity and a treatment—“Are you familiar with Mattress Mack?”—using the word you to create relevancy for the email recipients. We set up the A/B split test to see which of the two elements had better performance so we can continue to adjust subject lines for future emails in the series by using what works for this particular audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was set up in Hubspot to monitor results for the first 4 hours after the email test was sent to 50% of the total audience and then send the email with the most opens to the remainder of the file. Because of this, we did not have a large enough sample size to validate the experiment. The treatment saw a 6.3% decrease in traffic, but we did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence to draw any conclusions based on these results. Moving forward, we will want to run a similar test as a split to the full housefile rather than just half of it so we can validate results and find out how to best optimize the subject lines for this audience.

experiments.title: How changing the sender name in a membership email affects the open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a different sender name for the housefile audience will achieve a higher open rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test different sender names to measure open and click response. We wanted to test the effect a different sender name would have on the open rate of an email. The control email was from “Maggy” while the treatment was from “Andrew.” The audience is accustomed to receiving membership emails from “Maggy” and weekly cultivation emails from “Andrew.”
experiments.keyLearnings: Though there was a .74% decrease in traffic this experiment does not validate because only a 47.3% confidence level was achieved. Further testing and a larger sample size would be needed in order to validate these results. We recommend sending from another person in the organization to see if that affects the open rate since the housefile is accustomed to seeing emails from both "Maggy" and "Andrew."

experiments.title: How changing the sender name affects the open rate of an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the sender name for the housefile audience will achieve a greater open rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test sender names to measure open and click response. We tested two different names from the organization in order to see how each name would affect the open rate of an email. We used “Maggy Smith” for the control email and “Andrew McIndoe” for the treatment. “Andrew” sends a weekly cultivation email while “Maggy” is the usual sender for membership card appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though we saw a 1% increase in traffic, this test did not validate because it resulted in a confidence level of 66.8%. However, a retest with a larger sample size is recommended in order to validate results. There was also no statistical difference in the click thru rates for each email. We recommend potentially using another person's name to see if it would significantly increase open rates since the housefile is used to seeing both "Andrew" and "Maggy" in their inbox.

experiments.title: How a subject line test impacted open rates on a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a new subject line for returning donors will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test various subject lines to measure open and click response. We developed a treatment that used more active rather than passive language. The control subject line asked the question “Are you ready?” while the treatment asked “Are you coming?” while the body copy remained the same for both versions.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see any valid results from the experiment and only reached 50.2% level of confidence so this concept would need to be re-tested in the future. Looking at the body copy for the treatment, we would want to also test aligning the body copy with the subject line to make sure the original question is addressed or expounded upon to help the message flow more congruently.

experiments.title: How a more humanized subject line affects open rate on an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a humanized subject line for a housefile audience will achieve an increase in open rates .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test various subject lines to measure open and click response. We wanted to test the effect of a more humanized subject line vs. a standard subject line in an email. The control subject line read “Membership question” and the treatment read “I hope you will be joining us.”
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a .69% increase in traffic on the treatment but this test did not validate because it resulted in a confidence level of 49.2%. This would require retesting and tested with a prospecting email instead of a housefile email in order to test a larger sample size. We also recommend testing a more humanized subject line again using a first name in the subject line instead of "you" if testing the housefile email again.

experiments.title: How changing the sender name affected the open rate on an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the sender for housefile email recipients will achieve increased open rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement, we wanted to test different email senders to measure open rate. We wondered if users would be more inclined to open an email from one sender than another. The control sender was “Maggy Smith” and the treatment sender was “Andrew P. McIndoe.” Maggy works in Membership Programs and Andrew is the Vice President of Development. Each email contains the same subject line and body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment resulted in a -0.59 decrease in traffic and a 39.0% level of confidence. Users engaged less with Andrew P. McIndoe than they did with Maggy Smith. A possible reason is users are more familiar with Maggy Smith, as they've heard from her before about membership relations. Also, an email from a representative of Membership Programs may be more engaging than an email from the Vice President of Development, assuming the user is familiar with each.

The next experiment could be to change the subject line to something more specific, such as "Renew your membership today" rather than "I haven't heard from you." The user may be more inclined to open the email because of a needed action, regardless of the sender.

experiments.title: How using communal language in an email appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: How will communal language impact conversion in an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2021 - 03/08/2021
Care Net found very positive results in a previous experiment with using communal language. This experiment was run on the homepage of their website. They wanted to test this similar approach in an email appeal to see if they would see a similar response through this donor theory. To test it, communal language was added to the call-to-action of the email appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings: The communal language variation had no impact on click rate or donor conversion. This is due to the low engagement rate of the email. We will test this approach again in an email appeal and incorporate more communal language throughout the email to see if we can increase engagement and conversion.

experiments.title: Will a more clear and direct subject line lead to more course signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more clear with the offer in the email in the subject line for email subscribers will achieve more email course signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2021
